title: Exploring CEOs' decision making in Fintech Financing: evidence from UK SMEs
abstract:
The recent COVID-19, Brexit and current inflation made access to credit harder for UK SMEs. The innovation in financial services such as financial technology (Fintech) designed to redefine the delivery of financial services which dramatically influence and transmute the financing system. However, how CEOs make their Fintech financing decisions is not fully investigated. This research aims to fill in this gap.

To capture different characteristics of CEOs, this research interviewed 20 CEOs from UK SMEs to explore CEOs’ Fintech financing decision at the individual (micro), organisational (meso) and country (macro) levels. Results, supported by the cognitive theory of decision-making, confirm that CEOs make rational decisions and significantly rely on their leadership attributes to choose the best financing option for the benefit of their organisations. Results support the pecking order theory of financing indicate that CEOs prefer Fintech financing due to its flexibility in funding options, low costs and fewer regulations. Further findings suggest that the effects of micro factors on CEOs, such as family, vision, values, education level and prior experience, positively support CEOs to make Fintech decisions. Interestingly, at the meso level, results indicate that CEOs’ networking and the board's collective decisions play an important role in Fintech decision-making. In addition, the organic organisational structure stimulates Fintech financing. Lastly, the complexities of macro factors to navigate CEOs in their Fintech financing decisions uncovered that there is no specific Fintech law, forum or guidelines from the government to create awareness.

This research contributes to the extant knowledge by providing compelling evidence that most SME CEOs make rational decisions, underpinned by their transformational leadership approach, which are influenced by factors at the micro, meso and macro levels. These findings provide insights to government policies, International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank initiatives aimed at fostering innovation in finance.

ii 
 
Abstract 
The recent COVID-19, Brexit and current inflation made access to credit harder for UK SMEs. 
The innovation in financial services such as financial technology (Fintech) designed to redefine 
the delivery of financial services which dramatically influence and transmute the financing 
system. However, how CEOs make their Fintech financing decisions is not fully investigated. 
This research aims to fill in this gap. 
  
To capture different characteristics of CEOs, this research interviewed 20 CEOs from UK 
SMEs to explore CEOs’ Fintech financing decision at the individual (micro), organisational 
(meso) and country (macro) levels. Results, supported by the cognitive theory of decision-
making, confirm that CEOs make rational decisions and significantly rely on their leadership 
attributes to choose the best financing option for the benefit of their organisations. Results 
support the pecking order theory of financing indicate that CEOs prefer Fintech financing due 
to its flexibility in funding options, low costs and fewer regulations. Further findings suggest 
that the effects of micro factors on CEOs, such as family, vision, values, education level and 
prior experience, positively support CEOs to make Fintech decisions. Interestingly, at the 
meso level, results indicate that CEOs’ networking and the board's collective decisions play 
an important role in Fintech decision-making. In addition, the organic organisational structure 
stimulates Fintech financing. Lastly, the complexities of macro factors to navigate CEOs in 
their Fintech financing decisions uncovered that there is no specific Fintech law, forum or 
guidelines from the government to create awareness. 
  
This research contributes to the extant knowledge by providing compelling evidence that most 
SME CEOs make rational decisions, underpinned by their transformational leadership 
approach, which are influenced by factors at the micro, meso and macro levels. These findings 
provide insights to government policies, International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World 
Bank initiatives aimed at fostering innovation in finance. 
 
Keywords: Financial Technology (Fintech), CEO Leadership, SMEs, decision making, 
Financial Regulations

1 
 
1. Introduction  
Fintech is designed to redefine the delivery of financial services using technology with better, 
decentralised and efficient business models (Godfrey, 2018, Mention, 2019; Suryono, et al., 
2020). The technological advancement has transformed the financial sector (Yan, et al., 2021). 
Fintech has eased access to financial services through online payments, savings and 
investments, mobile financial services, crowdfunding, and peer-to-peer lending (Mention, 
2019; Suryono, et al., 2020; Yan, et al., 2021). Factors such as rapid evolution in technology, 
performance expectation, security perception, price value, changing consumer expectations, 
and government regulations influence the adoption of Fintech in SMEs (Jayalath & 
Premarathne, 2021; Najib, et al., 2021; Moreira-Santos, et al., 2022). The FinTech market has 
grown around the world during the last decade. In the UK only, the FinTech adoption rate is 
42% compared to 33% in the rest of the world (Department for International Trade, 2019). 
China, India, the UK, Brazil, and the USA are among the early adopters of FinTech worldwide 
(Bloomberg News, 2019). Fintech, through lower operational costs and increased 
convenience (Chuen, et al., 2015; Arner, et al., 2015), is massively revolutionising how 
financial services are conducted. Evidence suggests an emergence of FinTech in the global 
market (Wei, 2017; Sung, et al., 2019; Ghazali & Yasuoka, 2018; Huei, et al., 2018; Haddad 
& Hornuf, 2019).  
The innovative features of blockchain technology as effective security measures to safeguard 
customers' privacy (Ducas & Wilner, 2017; Saimima & Patria, 2021; Aldboush & Ferdous, 
2023) is central to the growth of FinTech include cryptocurrencies, crowdfunding, and peer-
to-peer (P2P) lending (Philippon, 2016). Evidence from the growth trend suggests that 
FinTech has become one of the main players as an alternative source of funds for SMEs to 
fulfil their business funding needs. For example, Peer to Peer (P2P) lending, serving as online 
platforms that unite lenders and borrowers, is one of the applications of FinTech to offer digital 
financial solutions (Arjunwadkar, 2018; Hamarat & Broby, 2022; Gu et al., 2022). With a 
potential growth of 1.6% in 2023, reaching a total value of £376.6 million (Gaw, 2023), the 
2 
 
innovative platforms link up potential investors with individuals seeking finance needs 
(Mattarocci & Gibilaro, 2016; Yan & Zhou, 2023). Crowdfunding is a collective process where 
individuals pool resources or funds through online sites to support an organisation or a project 
(Arjunwadkar, 2018; Bao, et al., 2022). The resources and the people are mobilised to achieve 
a given objective, such as equity capital, donation, start-up capital, etc. (Mohamed & Ali, 
2019). Crowdfunding is also known as social media fundraising since the entire fund 
generation process occurs through an online platform (Lynn, et al., 2019). Crowdfunding 
covers the various donation, equity and loan-based online models (FCA, 2023). The more 
commonly used crowdfunding for SMEs is equity-based, where the crowd funders invest to 
seek a potential return on the business shares in exchange for equity (Arjunwadkar, 2018; 
Chu et al., 2017). Equity-based crowdfunding is widely used by SMEs in the UK (Wachira & 
Wachira, 2022; Erasmus et al., 2022; Li, 2022) for start-up businesses and capital expansion. 
The global crowdfunding market is growing continuously, and it is projected for $1.41 billion in 
2023 from $1.25 billion in 2022 (Fortune Business Insights, 2023). Cryptocurrency is a digital 
currency that funds individuals and businesses with a secure and efficient blockchain 
technology (Lee, 2019; Tredinnick, 2019; Hashemi Joo, et al., 2020). Even though 
cryptocurrency is unregulated, it is likely to become mainstream in the upcoming years. The 
current law, such as the Financial Service and the Market Act 2000, was updated in 2023 to 
increase the power of the UK government to support cryptocurrency activities in the UK.  
Therefore, the UK government has already taken steps to safeguard the organisations that 
trade cryptocurrency (HM Treasury, 2023). Increasing the growth from £0.1 billion US dollars 
in 2019 to £1.9 billion US dollars in 2023, the cryptocurrency market is projected to be £3.8 
billion US dollars in 2027 (Statista Market Insights, 2023).  
Not only the growth but also trends of accepting Fintech in the UK are also increasing (Keary, 
2020) than any other country in Europe despite the economic challenges, especially the use 
of crowdfunding, P2P and crypto assets (OECD , 2019). The trends show that the funding 
increased through crowdfunding from 2020 to 2023 (Statista Market Insights, 2023), P2P 
3 
 
lending (Research Reports World, 2023) and also cryptocurrency (Statista Market Insights, 
2023). This is because of distinct crowdfunding models (Brown, et al., 2015), which require 
less information from the SMEs and little time in the contract negotiations between the 
borrowers and investors (Macht & Weatherston, 2014). The other driving factors are lower 
interest rates, bringing borrowers and lenders to secure and transparent platforms with the 
highest accessibility by Fintech. Initially, all forms of traditional funding were conducted offline. 
This was challenging for the SMEs, as they consumed more time to access financing. 
Conversely, Fintech funding deploys technology to conduct verifications and other measures, 
resulting in a more flexible process. Flexibility proves significant to UK SMEs in saving time. 
Furthermore, Fintech funding provides higher efficiency to the SMEs through automated and 
customised transactions.  
One of the key questions that academicians, policy players and practitioners have grappled 
with is whether the FinTech industry would displace the traditional banking industry in the 
same way digital technology has disrupted traditional advertising and publishing (Chiu, 2016; 
Zetzsche, et al., 2017). There is no definite answer because FinTech is in the early stages of 
evolution. The banking system started thousands of years ago, and in the UK, banknotes were 
first introduced in 1661 (Richards, 2019). Financial services institutions have robust systems 
in the world, and their history is rooted in centuries. Financial institutions have an essential 
role in providing their services to individuals and businesses in any economy (Swift, 2020). 
The UK financial sector employs 1.1 million people (Cherowbrier, 2020) and contributes £132 
billion to the UK economy (Rhodes, 2019). In addition to professional bodies, almost every UK 
University offer undergraduate and postgraduate degrees in Banking and Finance and trains 
learners to become managers and CEOs of leading financial institutions. On the other hand, 
FinTech firms employed 76500 people in 2019 (Cotton, 2019), and these firms also 
contributed £6 billion to the UK economy (KPMG, 2019).  Many leading UK universities have 
started to offer FinTech qualifications to prepare learners for future managerial positions. That 
indicates that managers and leaders of FinTech firms will have healthy competition with the 
4 
 
traditional financial sector in the UK. The demand for traditional financial institutions, such as 
banks and Fintech, will continue to grow due to their unique features and economic roles.  
 
1.1. Problem statement and key research questions 
SMEs play a significant role in the UK economy in terms of tax revenue, employment 
opportunities, and economic output generation. According to the UK National Statistics, the 
UK has approximately 5.83 million SMEs, accounting for 99.9 per cent of all the businesses 
in the UK (Department for Business, Energy & Industrial Strategy, 2020). These SMEs include 
small and medium-sized organisations established per the UK Companies Act 2006. A small 
company has a number of employees not more than 50, and the turnover should not be more 
than £10.2 million with a balance sheet of £5.1 million or less. For medium-sized companies, 
the turnover should not be more than £36 million, not more than 250 employees and the 
balance sheet should not be more than £18 million (Lu, 2018). The UK SMEs also employ 
approximately 60 per cent of the total number of private employees in the UK and contribute 
49.8% of the total GDP in the economy (Lu, 2018). 
Despite the vital role that SMEs play in the UK economy, a large body of literature has explored 
the various challenges that the UK’s SMEs face in accessing finance (Hughes, 1997; Cassar, 
2004; Udell, 2015; Dheer & Salamzadeh, 2022). However, financial institutions strive to 
provide better services due to robust regulatory frameworks (HM Treasury, 2019) after the 
2008 financial crash and the Covid-19 disastrous effects on the economy (Machkour, 2020; 
Chenguel, 2022; Alkayed, et al., 2023), SMEs, unlike the larger businesses, may not have the 
audited financial statements, thus increasing the informational friction between the lenders 
and the borrowers (Berger & Frame, 2007; Lee and Brown, 2017). On the other hand, Fintech 
funding provides lower barriers, quicker transactions, and enhanced security and privacy 
features (FinanceFeeds, 2023) that favour the customer immensely. Unlike traditional 
financing, which often requires stable employment history from senior officers of SMEs or 
collateral to seek a loan, Fintech funding provides loans based on credit rating that do not 
5 
 
require collateral and stable employment history. Consequently, Fintech funding is more 
accessible to various CEOs in SMEs. Higher accessibility enhances the reliability of these 
loans among the SMEs in the UK. Additionally, Fintech funding may be cost-friendly when 
compared to traditional financing. Most FinTech companies seek to compete against 
traditional financing companies. Consequently, they strive to provide lower interest rates to 
attract customers. This process favours SMEs. Fintech funding is better than traditional 
funding methods, such as bank loans, but it faces several limitations. For instance, Fintech 
funding lacks adequate regulations in the UK. Additionally, Fintech funding requires effective 
security measures such as blockchain technology to safeguard customers' privacy (Ducas & 
Wilner, 2017; Saimima & Patria, 2021; Aldboush & Ferdous, 2023).  
Due to the innovative features of Fintech, funding through Fintech is more accessible, making 
it more readily available to a variety of businesses led by CEOs, thereby enhancing the 
credibility of these loans. This prompts a question: How are the decisions made in these 
businesses regarding funding preferences through traditional financial institutions or Fintech, 
especially under the leadership of their respective CEOs? Meanwhile, evidence suggest that 
the leadership in the organisation is crucial for the funding decisions (Jonas, et al., 2011; Kline, 
2019; Strielkowski, et al., 2022; Putra & Adawiah, 2023) for its sustainability (Metcalf & Benn, 
2013; Howieson, et al., 2019; Haque, 2021) in the competitive market. However, there are 
many tiers of leadership in the organisation. The organisational tiers are levels of authority or 
hierarchical levels from lower management to the top-level management. The level of tiers 
depends on the organisational functions and the size of the organisation.  
The leadership at the CEO level is the top-level leadership in the organisation (Miao et al., 
2019; Ren et al., 2021), who ultimately provides the overall leadership to the organisation. The 
lower-level tiers are generally first or middle-level managers who provide leadership to their 
subordinates. The existing literature argue that leadership styles change at each hierarchical 
level of the organisation (Zenger & Folkman, 2019; Mansaray, 2019; Fries, et al., 2021). 
Because CEOs’ decisions travel from the top to the lower level of organisations, they impact 
6 
 
managerial positions by adopting the relevant leadership style to fulfil organisational decisions. 
In other words, management supports and implements CEOs' decisions. CEOs expect timely 
and accurate information from management to make Fintech financing decisions. These 
decisions are based on different decision-making models and theories such as such as 
cognitive decision-making (Wu, 2020; Andrade, 2020; Featherman, et al., 2021), rational 
decision making (Gabor, 1976; Doyle, 1999; Certo, et al., 2008; Cabantous, et al., 2010; 
Acciarini, et al., 2021; Sáenz-Royo, et al., 2022), and others such as the casual decision, 
game, evidential decision, and Bayesian theories (Ahmed & Omotunde, 2012; Peterson, 2017; 
Icard, 2021; Ahmed, 2021; Harper, 2022). It depends on the nature of their decisions whether 
such decisions are strategic, tactical, and operational (Lucey, 2004; Bello, et al., 2022; Laganà 
& Colapinto, 2022) to meet the funding requirements for their organisations. Therefore, the 
CEOs’ roles in SMEs are essential due to their highest-ranked executive position in the 
organisation to make key decisions for their stakeholders' benefit. The SME CEOs’ leadership 
role in the organisation includes the financing decisions (Naseem et al., 2019; Schopohl et al., 
2021) to promote innovation (Cortes & Herrmann, 2020; Akbari et al., 2021; Bagheri et al., 
2022). Financing decisions can involve funding organisations’ operations via traditional 
financing through banking channels or FinTech. It could be both, depending on the 
organisational financing choices. In the financing theories, the CEO's decision-making is 
based on two main financing theories – the trade-off and the pecking order (López-Gracia & 
Sogorb-Mira, 2008; Kokeyeva, 2019; Martinez et al., 2019; Jarallah et al., 2019; Agyei, et al., 
2020; 28 Hoang, et al., 2021; Frank et al., 2021). Empirical evidence have mix findings, for 
example, some organisations financing decisions support trade off theory (Mukherjee & 
Mahakud, 2012; Mazen, 2012; Moritz et al., 2015) and take external debt while others follow 
Pecking order theory (Shyam Sunder & Myers, 1999; López-Gracia & Sogorb-Mira, 2008; 
Degryse et al., 2009; Godke Veiga & McCahery, 2019) and avoid external debt as their first 
choice for funding decisions. In Fintech financing, CEOs try to achieve Fintech benefits while 
dealing with the challenges and barriers of traditional financing.  
7 
 
However, many unexplored areas exist regarding FinTech financing, its revolution, and its 
long-term impact on SMEs’ performance. Especially in this period of COVID-19, where 
FinTech firms and other financial sectors are facing economic crises, it is tough for SMEs to 
finance their business operations. In the previous financial crash of 2008, almost 1.8 million 
small businesses were closed, and 8.7 million jobs were lost (Weltman, 2020). In the current 
period, the Covid-19 damages are worse than any previous financial crash, and the UK GDP 
has already fallen by 20.4% in June 2020, the most significant fall since 1997 (Office for 
National Statistics, 2020). The impact of these falls will be seen in all the UK's financial and 
non-financial sectors. Therefore, leadership is crucial at the country level to lead the economy 
with better decisions. At the organisational level, CEOs are the main executive officers who 
deal with such situations to ensure the smooth running of their businesses. There is no single 
research in recent years to confirm how SMEs' top leadership, such as CEOs, make decisions 
to support their business growth with financing decisions; what alternative sources of funding 
are available to them at start-up, maturity or growth stage of SMEs; how their leadership and 
factors associated with their financing decisions and its impact on SMEs.  
Empirical evidence suggests that several factors play significant roles in influencing the 
financing decisions made by CEOs (Daniele, 2017), such as personal attributes like beliefs 
and values (Slovic, et al., 1977; Teräs, et al., 2020), risk-taking personality (Glaser & Weber, 
2007; Cronqvist, et al., 2012; Cain & McKeon, 2016), education (Hambrick, 2007; Soriano & 
Castrogiovanni, 2012; Herdinata, et al., 2019), experience, ability, age, leadership qualities, 
and socioeconomic status (Dietrich, 2010; Corbett & Spinello, 2020; Van Laar, et al., 2020; 
Anastasiou & Garametsi, 2021), which lead them to make the rational decisions (Oliveira, 
2007; Jonassen, 2012; Sáenz-Royo, et al., 2022). As CEOs work with a wide range of 
stakeholders in and outside their organisations, their networking contributes to their financing 
decisions, for example, the professional networking (Xiaowei & Zhang, 2010; Wang & Tu, 
2016; Eze, et al. 2018), the board of directors (Dunne & Morris, 2008; Leardini, et al., 2013; 
Cummings, 2018) and the organisational structure (Carzo & Yanouzas, 1969; Meltzer & Salter, 
8 
 
1962; Chehade & Vigneron, 2007; Nieuwenhuizen, et al., 2012; Muthukannan, et al., 2020). 
These factors can be categorised into three categories: micro-factors are directly related to 
CEOs’ subjective characteristics, such as their leadership, vision, values, family, beliefs, 
education, experience, and risk-taking attitude; meso factors are forces around CEOs in their 
professional practice, such as professional networking, the Board, and the organisational 
structure, and macro factors are external to micro and macro factors and may directly or 
indirectly influence their financing decisions, such as government policies and regulations. 
With increasing trends of crowdfunding, P2P, and cryptocurrency financing from 2020 to 2023 
(Statista Market Insights, 2023), it is essential to explore what contributes to CEOs' decision-
making on Fintech financing, especially after COVID-19, economic recession, high inflation, 
and interest rates. To fill in the gap, the researcher has the following research aim and 
objectives. 
 
Research Aim:- 
Explore the CEOs' decision making relating to Fintech financing in UK SMEs 
 
Research Objectives:- 
To achieve the aim of the this study, the researcher has the following research objectives, 
Research Objective 1 - Exploring the perceptions of CEOs regarding their financing choices 
at the UK SMEs. 
Research Objective 2 – Exploring the micro factors such as CEOs’ personal leadership traits, 
vision, and risk-taking attitude to navigate their Fintech financing decisions at the UK SMEs. 
Research Objective 3 – Exploring the influence of meso factors such as professional 
networking, board of directors, and organisational structure in CEOs’ Fintech financing 
decisions at the UK SMEs. 
9 
 
Research Objective 4 - Exploring the complexity of government support and regulations as 
macro factors to navigate CEOs' perceptions to make Fintech financing decisions at the UK 
SMEs. 
The following four key research questions help to achieve the research objectives, 
Research Question 1 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing choices in the 
context of a rapidly evolving economic and financial technology environment?  
Research Question 2 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs perceive the influence of micro-level 
factors, such as personal leadership traits, vision and risk-taking attitude, on their Fintech 
financing decisions? 
Research Question 3 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs make their decisions on Fintech financing 
through professional networking, the board of directors and organisational structure contexts 
at the meso level?  
Research Question 4 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate the complexities of macro-level 
factors, including government support and laws, when making Fintech financing decisions? 
 
1.2. Key findings and contribution 
The present research focuses on semi-structured face-to-face interviews to collect data from 
CEOs who are participants of this study. The interviews were conducted from February 2021 
to 3rd August 2023, leading to the analysis and presentation of the findings. The participants 
were recruited from non-financial SMEs with an age of more than one year and at least two 
people with other criteria that they should have used fintech or at least know about fintech 
financing. The researcher focused on the constructivism paradigm to explore the reality of 
CEOs’ financing related decisions with assumptions that realities are multiple (Pale & Lilford, 
2011) and based on the individuals subjective stance (Kusch, 2020; Krol, 2021; Merlo & 
Pravato, 2021). Therefore, the ontological stance of this study is relativism. CEOs have their 
own opinions and perceptions when making decisions about Fintech financing because of 
10 
 
their unique social structure (Archer, 1995; Archer, 1982; Gorski, 2013) in which they work 
and interact with others in society. The epistemological stance is social constructivism with 
assumptions that knowledge is constructed through social and cultural practices (Young & 
Collin, 2004; Cohen et al., 2004; Stead, 2004; Richardson et al., 2009) and the subjective 
stance of CEOs’ financing decisions will uncover many truths (Stead, 2004). The study follows 
the grounded approach (Gligor et al., 2016; Charmaz, 2000; Charmaz, 2006; Charmaz, 2017) 
based on inductive reasoning (Pope, et al., 2000; Ketokivi & Mantere, 2010; Bendassolli, 2013; 
LUKENCHUK, 2017; Zeithaml et al., 2020) to get detailed insights into CEOs' views through 
interviews and explore the influence of factors to navigate their decision-making relating to 
Fintech. 
The result from the first research question of how CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing 
choices in the context of a rapidly evolving economic and financial technology environment 
supported three themes: (a) CEOs in the UK prefer Fintech to traditional financing, (b) 
Increasing demand and banking challenges necessitate Fintech financing options, and (c) 
limited awareness about the Fintech options. Results show that CEOs in the UK SMEs prefer 
Fintech over traditional financing. Specifically, 70% of CEOs prefer Fintech financing through 
crowdfunding, P2P lending and Cryptocurrency. 15% of participants prefer traditional 
financing through banks, while the remaining 15% prefer both. In the first theme, the findings 
confirm that CEOs prefer Fintech because it provides more financing options at a lower cost 
and with fewer regulations. The results of more funding options are supported by the outcome 
of the current research by Haddad & Hornuf (2019) and Bollaert et al. (2021). The result that 
the Fintech provides lower cost is consistent with the research report published by the 
European Central Bank (Eça, et al., 2022) and the previous studies by Carlin et al. (2017), 
Najib et al. (2021), Kumari & Devi (2022), Ornelas & Pecora (2022) and Elliehausen & Hannon 
(2023). The study results of CEOs' decisions for Fintech financing due to its lower regulations 
are consistent with the findings of Magnuson (2018), Alam et al. (2019), and Chorzempa & 
Huang (2022). In addition, due to COVID-19 and the history of the financial crash, CEOs have 
11 
 
more intention to use efficient funding platforms rather than rely on banks. However, the 
second theme emerged because participants preferred traditional financing and switched to 
Fintech if they found that banks did not offer such services—for example, access to investors 
to invest in their business for expansion or payment gateways. That increases the demand for 
Fintech services, including the increase of its adoption rate due to benefits, and also poses 
challenges for banks to improve their services. The study results are congruent with the 
findings of Razzaque and Hamdan (2020), Ahern (2021), and Abdul-Rahim et al. (2022) that 
CEOs' preference for Fintech is because of its benefits and advantages. The third theme, 
limited awareness of Fintech, led CEOs to choose traditional financing. This is because of low 
digital financial literacy, awareness of cyber security risks, and online fraud with Fintech 
financing. The limited awareness about Fintech options is supported by the findings of existing 
research by Das and Das (2020), Niu et al. (2020), and Basar et al. (2022), who emphasise 
literacy and awareness. CEOs have concerns about the government's limited support of 
Fintech options, which barred them from funding through Fintech.  
The themes obtained from the second research question of how CEOs in UK SMEs perceive 
the influence of micro-level factors, such as personal leadership traits, vision and risk-taking 
attitude, on their Fintech financing decisions include  (a) leadership traits, (b) using vision to 
guide Fintech decisions, (c) using values to guide decision-making, (d) incorporating family 
members’ perspectives in making Fintech decisions, (e) using personal beliefs to reinforce 
Fintech decision-making, (f) use educational knowledge to improve Fintech decision making, 
and (g) use prior experience to improve Fintech decision making (h) risk approach to making 
Fintech decision. The first theme at the micro level factors revealed that CEOs have leadership 
traits that reflect ethics, morality, dedication, and integrity in their work. They focused on their 
SME's long-term vision by promoting collaborative teamwork, accountability, and innovation 
to achieve shared organisational goals. These results are similar to the findings of Waldman, 
et al. (2001), Simsek, et al., (2010), Kaplan, et al. (2012), Chen, et al. (2013), Haas & 
Speckbacher (2017), Huang, et al. (2019), and Ibarra et al. (2020). The second theme at the 
12 
 
micro level includes CEOs having their vision to guide their Fintech decisions are consistent 
with the findings of Nwokedi (2015), Collins (2020), Slåtten et al. (2021), Maran et al., (2022), 
Yuanyuan et al. (2022) and Ji et al. (2023). CEOs work as visionary leaders to look for the 
future impact and the benefits of Fintech financing, which contribute to the long-term success 
of their SMEs. Their vision promotes innovation decisions to deal with the competitive 
environment of their organisations. The third theme confirms that CEOs have personal values, 
and they consider their values of transparency, kindness, integrity, and honesty to lead them 
to make ethical financing decisions. These findings are supported by Vitt (2004), Berson et al. 
(2008), Gorgievski et al. (2011) and Lofving et al. (2023) outcomes of the research where 
researchers concluded that CEOs' leadership styles in their organisations are guided by their 
values. Values further promote their innovation decisions by attracting investors to fund their 
business. The four themes at the micro level revealed that incorporating family members’ 
perspectives in making Fintech decisions positively influences their financing decisions 
because it provides them with emotional support and enables them to make decisions with 
confidence. The significance of family involvement with the business is consistent with the 
findings of Anderson & Reeb (2003), Chrisman et al. (2004), Jonathan (2021), and Buchanan 
et al. (2022) that it has positive impacts the organisation success. However, in this research, 
the organisation's success is not measured; instead, the focus is only on the support from 
family involvement as the influential factor contributing to CEOs' financing decisions. In 
addition, CEOs don’t discuss their professional matters with their families because of privacy 
and confidentiality issues, and they are bound not to share their organisational matters with 
them. The fifth theme is that CEOs believe that the future period is with information technology 
and belongs to innovation to remain successful in the competitive business environment. That 
is their personal belief in reinforcing Fintech decision-making. Personal beliefs are associated 
with religion, trust, risks, and their perception of the Fintech financing benefits and advantages. 
The study findings are congruent with Baxamusa & Jalal (2016) that religious beliefs affect 
decisions and also with the findings of Setiawan et al. (2021), Mascarenhas et al. (2021), 
Abdul-Rahim et al. (2022), Gupta et al. (2023) that the personal beliefs are based on trust 
13 
 
including perceived risks and benefits to affect their perceptions and intentions to accept 
FinTech. The sixth and seventh themes at the micro level confirm that knowledge through 
education and prior experience improve their Fintech financing decisions. The education and 
experience help enhance their decision-making capacity to identify better financing options, 
assess risks, and lead them to make innovative financing decisions. Education further enables 
them to understand regulations, costs and benefits, advantages and disadvantages of 
financing options. Results are coinciding with the findings of Hambrick (2007), Soriano & 
Castrogiovanni (2012) and Herdinata, et al. (2019), Xiaowei & Zhang (2010), Moritz, et al. 
(2015), Zheng & Piao (2019), Mohd Thas Thaker (2019) and Duréndez, et al. (2023). The final 
theme at the micro level confirms that CEOs have a risk-avoider and taking attitude, which 
influences their decision-making. CEOs have opinions that the Fintech market is volatile, 
uncertain, and unpredictable and prefer not to take risks. The study findings oppose the 
literature of Jianakoplos & Bernasek (2006), Steinberg (2007), Mitsopoulou (2019), Breivik et 
al. (2020), and Gunier et al. (2022) that younger people are more risk takers. However, the 
fact that can’t be ignored is that this study deals with CEOs who are all educated and 
experienced in their profession and Fintech financing, which does not have a long history as 
already established traditional financing; therefore, the literacy and experience of Fintech are 
the determinants to establish their risk attitude. The 55% of participants who are middle-aged 
CEOs believed they were risk takers, which is consistent with the findings of Melović et al. 
(2022) that experienced individuals make risky decisions. In addition, age and gender are 
micro factors determining the CEOs' financing preferences. The study demographic statistics 
show that 45% of young CEOs, 15% of middle-aged CEOs, and 5% of older CEOs prefer 
Fintech financing. While 64% of males and 67% of females prefer Fintech as their preferred 
financing option. However, age and gender demographic representations of the data do not 
firmly constitute the CEOs' financing preferences due to their age and gender. Other micro, 
meso and macro-level factors should be considered, along with the demographics of age and 
gender statistics, such as CEOs' perceptions of Fintech financing and their leadership style.  
14 
 
The findings relating to the third research question of how CEOs in UK SMEs make their 
decisions on Fintech financing through professional networking, the board of directors and 
organisational structure contexts at the meso level revealed three major themes: (a) 
incorporating professional advice and contributions, (b) consulting the board, and (c) the 
organisational structure's impact on Fintech decisions. Within the board of SMEs, the 
researcher also found the role of the Chairman to be a significant influencing factor for CEOs’ 
financing decisions. In the first theme at the meso level, CEOs develop professional 
networking with their team in their organisations and outside experts, including investors, 
which helps them enhance their awareness of financing solutions. The results of the influence 
of the professional networking factor in this study are consistent with the findings of Xiaowei 
& Zhang (2010), Wang & Tu (2016), and Eze et al. (2018) that opinions through professional 
networking assist in decision-making. The second theme at the meso level reveals that SMEs 
have a board in their organisations, which influences CEOs' Fintech financing decisions. The 
board includes executive and non-executive directors to bring a wealth of experience to SMEs 
who also elect their chairman for important board-related decisions such as strategic financing 
choices. The board is a separate body from the management, a further tier of decision-making 
at the CEO's top, usually called the Board decision. There are many reasons for having a 
board, such as the requirements of the organisation's constitution and or to show external 
lenders for their SMEs’ good governance. The researcher found that some SMEs have CEOs 
dual roles as chairman and CEO simultaneously, but some SMEs have a separate role of 
Chairman from CEO. SMEs that are not listed bodies and, therefore, don’t have regulatory or 
legal requirements to have an independent chairman on the board as per the UK Governance 
Quality Code requirements. The study findings are consistent with the results of Croci et al. 
(2011), Xiang et al. (2018) and Gurdgiev and Ni (2023), which state that diverse boards create 
good governance. Also, the independent role of the Chairman from the CEO minimises the 
conflict between the shareholders and management. The third theme at the meso level is the 
impact of the organisational structure on influencing CEOs in their financing decisions. The 
researcher found that the organisational structure, if it is not complex, such as organic, 
15 
 
supports innovation in CEOs’ financing decisions, and the mechanistic structure, which is 
based on the level of hierarchies, supports less innovation and traditional financing. These 
results are aligned with the findings of Chaston (1997), Bai et al. (2017) and Su et al. (2019), 
which emphasise the significance of organic organisational structure as a factor to influence 
CEOs' decisions to respond timely by responding to the change in their business environment. 
The results of the final question of how CEOs of UK SMEs navigate the complexities of macro-
level factors, including government support and laws, when making Fintech financing 
decisions found that UK SMEs prefer to work within rules, laws, and regulations because that 
increases their confidence and protects their decisions within the laws. CEOs are aware of the 
economic situations, recessions and negative politics. CEOs align their decisions with the 
laws, consistent with the findings of Dwivedi et al., 2021; Ng and Pan, 2022 and also 
government policies and laws promote their adoption of Fintech, which is consistent with the 
findings of Chen et al. 2014; Kiwanuka, 2015; Rosavina & Rahadi, 2018; Ghazali & Yasuoka, 
2018; Owen,et al., 2019; Rosavina, et al. 2019; Goo and Heo, 2020; Ahern, 2021; Zarrouk et 
al., 2021; Makki and Alqahtani, 2022; Ng and Pan, 2022. However, CEOs expect the UK 
government to take considerable measures regarding the enhancement of Fintech, 
awareness of Fintech financing and further laws or regulations to ensure the confidence of 
their decisions in Fintech financing.  
This is the first empirical research on CEOs' decision-making for their Fintech financing 
preference. There are several studies that are closely related to the subject. For example, 
external sources of funding by SMEs in China by Wei, 2017, promoting SME finance in the 
context of the Fintech revolution: A case study of the UK's practice and regulation by Lu 
(2018), Fintech education and training in the UK by Sung, et al. (2019), awareness and 
perception of Fintech Financing of SMEs in Malaysia by Ghazali & Yasuoka (2018), the 
emergence of global Fintech market by Haddad & Hornuf (2019) and the real effects of Fintech 
lending on SMEs: evidence from loan applications by European Central Bank (2022). So, it 
contributes to the extant literature in Fintech financing, particularly regarding the CEO's 
16 
 
decision-making in SMEs. The emerging themes contributing to CEOs’ decisions to fund their 
businesses with Fintech financing are that it provides more financing options, such as 
crowdfunding, P2P lending, and Cryptocurrency; it also offers lower financing costs and has 
lower regulations. CEOs' decision-making supports the cognitive theory of decision-making to 
make rational decisions. CEOs establish business relationships and partnerships, work with 
ethics, morality, dedication, and hard work, and align their values and vision to achieve the 
organisational goals by developing and engaging their team. This contributes to the 
transformational theory of leadership (contribution to Bass & Riggio, 2006 and Baškarada et 
al., 2017). Their vision, values, family members, personal beliefs, experience, and risk-taking 
personality influence CEOs' decisions to accept Fintech. They work collectively with other 
board members to ensure integrity is reflected in their financing decisions. The study reveals 
that middle-aged CEOs are more risk takers to accept Fintech than younger CEOs. These 
findings oppose the literature that older people are less risk takers (Okun, 1976; Vroom & 
Pahl, 1971; Bellante & Green, 2004). This is because Fintech is comparatively new in England, 
and the older CEOs know more about Fintech financing due to experience. The study results 
contribute to the Agency theory of corporate governance (contribution to Samuel, 2013; 
Renders & Gaeremynck, 2012; Nguyen et al., 2020; Tibiletti et al., 2021) to the extent that 
CEOs work for the interest of their shareholders that reduce agency costs and bring expertise 
to the board due to the independent factor of non-executive directors. The findings contribute 
to the literature on the organisational structure, specifically Chaston (1997), Bai et al. (2017) 
and Su et al. (2019), to the extent that the organic structure of the organisation supports 
innovation for SMEs to accept Fintech financing. The findings are also consistent with the 
Pecking Order Theory and contribute to Degryse et al. (2009) that the SMEs prefer to use their 
internal funds in their first preference to meet their business funding needs. Further, CEOs 
prefer alternative funding through Fintech because of its fewer regulations, lower cost and 
more funding options. At the practical aspects of the contribution, the current study supports 
that CEOs follow and comply with the country's laws to avoid non-compliance. They expect 
support and knowledge through networking and more guidance from the government. The 
17 
 
study further informs government policymakers to encourage a fairer legal environment 
favouring SMEs' adoption and use of Fintech. 
 
1.3. Structure of the thesis 
The structure of this study includes the following sections.  
Chapter 1 provides a general introduction to this research, including a brief review of existing 
literature, research gaps and rationales, research aims and objectives, methodology, overview 
of findings, impact and the score of limitation and the structure of the thesis. Chapter 2 
discusses the literature review. The literature review has three parts. Part 1 includes the 
theoretical background of decision-making theories and models, leadership theories in 
decision-making, and financing theories. Part 2 includes Fintech in practice – history, recent 
development, the role of technology in Fintech, Fintech financing, government support and 
the challenges of Fintech in the economy. Part 3 includes the development of research 
questions supported by extensive literature to develop the conceptual framework. Chapter 3 
contains the data and the research methodology. The research methodology is based on the 
approach, methods and procedures. This section includes the data collection and analysis. 
Research ethics are explained in detail to maintain the confidentiality of information before, 
during and after the interview. Chapter 4 contains results within the emerged themes and sub-
themes based on the interview questions to CEOs to understand their decision making relating 
to Fintech financing in UK SMEs. Chapter 5 includes a general discussion of findings and 
recommendations. This chapter comprises the conceptual framework from the research 
results, supported by empirical evidence. Chapter 6 is a conclusive part to confirm the CEOs' 
preferences toward Fintech financing and the role of CEOs in SMEs when it comes to 
decision-making, e.g. where they stand, what contributes to their decisions when it comes to 
choosing between Fintech and traditional financing. It includes the summary of the findings 
and contribution to the knowledge, literature, practice, and policies with reflection and 
18 
 
limitations in addition to the future agenda for conducting research. Chapter 7 includes 
references, and Chapter 8 contains Appendices. 
     
2. Literature review  
The study deals with CEOs' decision-making regarding their financing preferences and factors 
influencing their Fintech financing decisions. The literature review is centred on CEOs' 
leadership, decision-making theories and models, Fintech development and its options for 
financing, and the considerable empirical evidence to support the development of the research 
framework. CEOs’ leadership is crucial to making important decisions because they are critical 
in managing and leading their organisation in SMEs (Kuntz et al., 2019; Alade & Windapo, 
2021; Barrett et al., 2021). The first section includes the theoretical background of decision-
making, leadership, and financing theories. These are significant in understanding CEOs' 
leadership styles and decision-making roles in SMEs in determining the suitable financing 
option. The second section includes Fintech's development, history, and various recently 
developed technology-enabled financing options to support funding needs. The literature 
review's final section includes key factors influencing CEOs' decision-making at the individual, 
organisational, and country levels in funding their business through FinTech. These factors 
are categorised as the CEOs’ micro, meso, and macro levels to explore how these factors 
navigate CEOs in their FinTech financing decisions. The current research uses three layers 
(micro, meso and macro) to identify CEOs’ factors. The concept of three layers is from the 
sociology literature. Sociology is a subject that aims to understand the people, culture, human 
interactions, forces, and factors that stimulate human behaviour in society (Thompson et al., 
2016; Spillman, 2020; Plummer, 2021). Sociologists divide society into three layers, namely 
micro, meso and macro (Bruhn & Rebach, 2007; Stebbins, 2020; Fillieule, 2023), to 
understand society (Johnson, 2008; Thompson et al., 2016). The micro level represents a 
subjective interpretation of individual characteristics and courses of action (Alexander et al., 
1987; Bruhn & Rebach, 2007; Bhaskar, 2020). That relates to human factors and the influence 
19 
 
of the other layers at meso and macro within which they act within the society (Alexander, et 
al., 1987). The researcher uses terms of three layers to understand CEOs’ micro, meso and 
macro factors. The micro factors include the CEOs’ characteristics, meso factors include 
networking, group, and all those forces around CEOs in organisations, and the macro factors 
fall external into meso and micro, such as the government policies that influence their 
decisions regarding their financing preferences. A research framework facilitates the conduct 
of research supported by the empirical evidence at the micro level, such as personal 
leadership traits, values, family, beliefs, education, experience, and risk-taking attitude and at 
the meso level, such as the board of directors and the organisational structure and at the 
macro level such as the government policies and support. The micro, meso, and macro-level 
factors further support them in making FinTech decisions in the needs of funds backed by 
leadership and decision-making theories.  
 
2.1. Theoretical Background 
2.1.1. 
Decision Making Theories and Models  
In literature, studies on decision-making often seek to describe the decision-making theories, 
models and practice, the kind of decisions, and the process of decision-making among other 
related topics. While addressing the issue of decision-making theories in the management and 
leadership of how CEOs utilise decision making models, various authors have different 
perspectives to differentiate the decision-making models from decision-making theories. The 
decision theory entails the study of the principles and algorithms used in making decisions 
(Ahmed & Omotunde, 2012). Even though there are many decision-making theories, however, 
the current study involves the identification of the values and uncertainties, among other 
factors, that navigate CEOs' decisions. Because making a decision in an organisation is one 
of the major attributes and a core skill of a leader (Ahmed & Omotunde, 2012; Kane, et al., 
2019). Decision-making in the management context describe the nature of identifying 
alternatives and picking the best among them (Ahmed & Omotunde, 2012; Herrera-Viedma, 
20 
 
et al., 2020). In the most organisations, the three levels of decision-making include strategic, 
tactical, and operational, and each level has different implications on the entire organisation 
(Lucey, 2004; Bello, et al., 2022; Laganà & Colapinto, 2022). Strategic decisions are made by 
senior executives such as CEOs, they are long-term and have an impact on the entire 
organisation (Recendes, et al., 2022). Tactical and operational decisions are shorter term 
(Bello, et al., 2022) and normally performed by lower management in the organisation. 
However, most studies on the decision-making theory and practice acknowledge that in the 
business context, it is mandatory for the senior executives to make numerous decisions, both 
simple and complex (Marchisotti, et al., 2018). The point herein is to show that decision making 
is perhaps the most critical management function in the organisation (Brunsson, 2019; Hums, 
et al., 2023; Fanelli, et al., 2023). Therefore, the only relevant decision theories closely aligned 
to this research are considered such as rational decision making (Gabor, 1976; Doyle, 1999; 
Certo, et al., 2008; Cabantous, et al., 2010; Acciarini, et al., 2021; Sáenz-Royo, et al., 2022), 
bounded rational theory (Turpin & Marais, 2004; Tarka, 2017; Gigerenzer, 2020; Morales 
Burgos, et al., 2020), the casual decision, game, evidential decision, Bayesian theories 
(Ahmed & Omotunde, 2012; Peterson, 2017; Icard, 2021; Ahmed, 2021; Harper, 2022), 
naturalistic model of decision making (Turpin & Marais, 2004; Boyes & O’Hare, 2011; 
Carmody-Bubb, 2023) and the cognitive theory of decision making (Wu, 2020; Andrade, 2020; 
Featherman, et al., 2021).  
The rational decision-making theory is one of the most common theories. The key aspect of 
this theory is that the decision-maker analyses the various alternatives from given scenarios 
before making a choice (Oliveira, 2007; Jonassen, 2012; Sáenz-Royo, et al., 2022). In this 
theory, scenarios are weighted by probabilities for decision makers to select the best 
alternative. In this theory, the rational and informed decision maker selects an alternative by 
following three basic steps: 1) Establishing the feasibility, 2) Determining the desirability and 
3) Combining both feasibility and desirability to select the best choice. 
21 
 
The rationality model is deemed somewhat traditional and a model that can be made more 
practical, and hence the emergence of the bounded rationality model (Grüne-Yanoff, 2007; 
Ballester & Hernández, 2012; Sáenz-Royo, et al., 2022). While bounded rationality is a model 
on its own, it is described herein as a refinement of the traditional rationality theory. The 
bounded rationality model of decision-making is based on the notion of satisficing – that is, 
selecting the alternatives that justify implicitly and explicitly the minimum criteria (Turpin & 
Marais, 2004). This model is also based on the observation (Geng, et al., 2022) in which the 
decision-makers are not always rational. As a refinement of the rationality model, the bonded 
rational loosens the assumption of rationality and makes the decision-making theory look more 
practical. 
The causal decision theory is founded on the principle of rational choice, meaning it is part of 
the rational model of decision-making (Ahmed & Omotunde, 2012; Opp, 2019). The rational 
model of decision-making is based on logic (Grandori, 2010; Kornberger, et al., 2019) and it 
is not essential for a CEO to know everything to make decisions. This model is founded on 
the neoclassical economic theory (Rizzo & Whitman, 2018). Based on this model, the 
decision-making process begins with intelligence – that is, finding the occasions for the 
decision-making. The process involves developing, inventing, and analysing the courses of 
actions followed by a review to select the best choice.  
The game theory is a mathematical study of strategic decision-making and that takes into 
account the conflict and cooperation between intelligent and rational decision makers (Tang, 
et al., 2019; Choi, et al., 2020; Leyton-Brown & Shoham, 2022). This theory is normally help 
to understand collective decisions (Rossi, 2014). The evidential decision theory asserts that 
the decision is chosen among the alternatives with the best outcome (Ahmed, 2021; Huttegger 
& Huttegger, 2023). Again, this can be seen as having some similarities with the bounded 
rationality model that seeks the best outcomes and a more pragmatic approach to decision 
making.  
22 
 
The Bayesian is a probability theory, often considered as an extension of logic that allowing 
reasoning with prepositions (Zhou, et al., 2020; Acree & Acree, 2021). The Bayesian theory is 
also associated with the risk management model (Ardia, 2008; Iberraken, et al., 2019; 
Martínez-Sánchez, et al., 2023). Risk Management models are used by CEOs in the SMEs to 
identify, measure, mitigate, monitor and report risks for better decision making (Zoghi, 2017; 
Crovini, et al., 2021). 
There is an interesting leadership model is termed as the naturalistic model of decision making 
which is normally focused on identifying, understanding, and then investigating all information 
available to decision makers in the natural context (Turpin & Marais, 2004; Boyes & O’Hare, 
2011; Carmody-Bubb, 2023). An example of decision-making approaches and models based 
on the naturalistic model is Klein (1998)’s Recognition-Primed Decision-Making Model that is 
founded on the capability and skills of decision-makers to identify a situation and set decision 
goals appropriate to that situation. It requires real-time decisions from CEOs in the dynamic 
changing business environment in the natural settings.  
The cognitive theory of decision-making is based on human individual perceptions, beliefs and 
knowledge as important factors to contribute to their decisions (Wu, 2020; Andrade, 2020; 
Featherman et al., 2021). In this theory, the decision making is a mental process, and it is 
based on the choosing the best or the preferred option from set of alternatives (Wang & Ruhe, 
2017). Decision making is fundamental part of human life in day-to-day routine. The economic, 
social, and technological factors play significant role for humans to make decisions in relation 
to their family, personal and professional decisions (Crozier & Ranyard, 1997; Sima, et al., 
2020), for example, education, careers, management of personal finance, development and 
deployment of resources and acceptance of technology. 
In the cognitive decision making, the behaviour and expected utility theories are common to 
make a rational choice from the set of actions. Expected utility theory depends on the rational 
decision making to seek the expected outcome of the decision (Ruggeri, et al., 2020). In this 
theory, decision makers’ personal attributes are key contributors for their decisions to choose 
23 
 
between risky, unpredictable or uncertain outcomes (Daniele, 2017). On the other hand, 
behaviour decision making theory provide insights of how people make judgements in reality 
under uncertain conditions  (Takemura, 2014) and decisions are closely linked to their belief 
and values (Slovic, et al., 1977; Teräs, et al., 2020). Decision makers in the cognitive process 
model, first identify the problem, make different choices by outweighing them, compare 
alternatives, and then make the final decision (Reehuckin, 2017). Factors such as expertise 
and personal beliefs influence the decision making (Dietrich, 2010; Corbett & Spinello, 2020; 
Van Laar, et al., 2020; Anastasiou & Garametsi, 2021). The same sentiments are evident by 
Stanovich & West (2008) and Acevedo & Krueger (2004) that expertise, cognitive favouritisms, 
beliefs, and personal preferences are common factors in human psychology to influence their 
decision-making capabilities. These decisions making theories are summarised below in the 
following diagram.  
24 
 
 
Figure 1 Decision Making Theories 
 
2.1.2. 
Leadership theories in decision-making  
Leadership and management are often discussed as separate concepts, each with different 
theories. Leadership is the process where a person (the leader) inspires others (the followers) 
to work together towards the achievement of a common goal (Kazeem, et al., 2014; Giddens, 
2018; Ciulla & Ciulla, 2020). While management is simply the process used to accomplish 
organisational goals. For the purpose of this literature review, the focus will not be on 
differentiating leadership and management, but examining how leaders and managers make 
25 
 
decisions paying attention to the various theories. In many studies, leadership theories in the 
context of decision-making have been outlined often highlighting how different types of 
decisions are facilitated by different leadership styles.  
In the theoretical concepts of leadership, it has been expressed that the leadership theories 
describe the leaders’ behaviour and their decision making as consequences of their leadership 
styles (Kazeem, et al., 2014). The leadership theories determine the leadership styles to guide 
the decision making. The behavioral theory of leadership decision making for leaders to make 
decisions are based on reasons and it relates to their beliefs, attitude or behaviour (Westaby, 
2005; Westaby, et al., 2010; Sahu, et al., 2020). In this theory, leaders adjust their behaviour 
based on the situation and according to the demand of their stakeholders as they need a 
reason to justify their decisions to them. The servant theory of leadership emphasises 
listening, empathy, and stewardship to serve the needs of followers (Greenleaf, 2002; Parris 
& Peachey, 2013; Bavik, 2020). This focuses on leaders ensuring financial decisions are made 
fairly to benefit various stakeholders. Authentic leadership prioritises building trusted 
relationships and leading by example with transparency and integrity (Iszatt-White, et al., 
2021; Turman, 2023). They foster transparency and accountability in financial matters. Ethical 
leadership involves demonstrating and promoting values-based conduct (Bavik, et al., 2018; 
Bai, et al., 2019; Bazerman, 2020). This provides an ethical framework for financial decisions. 
In the organisational context where group of people work to achieve the organisational goals, 
there are two types of leaders – called assigned and emergent (Simoff & Sudweeks, 2010; 
Petridou, et al., 2016; Hoch & Dulebohn, 2017; Hickman & Akdere, 2018). Assigned leaders 
are given top management positions, whereas emergent leaders emerge over time as more 
influential due to their behaviour and leadership characteristics (Northhouse, 2016; Hoch & 
Dulebohn, 2017; Hickman & Akdere, 2018). Assigned and emergent leaders have the potential 
to influence others’ attitudes and course of action within the organisation because their 
followers believe in their knowledge and follow their good role models. The theoretical 
consideration of CEOs’ leadership and their decision making has often been based on the 
26 
 
upper echelons theory (Waldman, et al., 2004; Hambrick, 2007; Bromiley & Rau, 2016; Ma, et 
al., 2022). The upper echelons theory indicates that the personality, attitude, experience and 
the background of top management play an integral part to contribute in their decisions in 
organisations (Hambrick, 2007; Altarawneh, et al., 2020; Ma, et al., 2022; White & 
Borgholthaus, 2022). 
The key theories of leadership styles are transactional and transformational leadership 
(Hoxha, 2019; Cho, et al., 2019; Ugwu & Okore, 2020; Purwanto, 2021). Within these theories, 
leadership styles are participative, delegative and autocratic (Ejimabo, 2015). Leadership 
styles have different implications on the decision-making process and models. The leaders in 
the participative leadership encourage and involve their subordinates or team to participate in 
the major decisions (Khassawneh & Elrehail, 2022) that increase the participants creativity (Li, 
et al., 2018). The leaders play the role of facilitator while the team is the responsible for 
outcome of their collective decisions. In the delegative leadership, leaders set directions or 
goals, believe in the skills of their team and delegate the tasks to them (Cismas, et al., 2016). 
Team have freedom to make their own decisions that foster the accountability and innnovation 
in the organisation (Ziegert & Dust, 2021; Djafar, 2023). In the  autocratic leadership, the 
leader makes authoritative decisions often associated with their complete control and 
ownership of the organisation in the making of decisions (Sievert, 2018; Tang & Tang, 2019; 
Alblooshi, et al., 2021). The leaders do not consult with or request for ideas from subordinates. 
This style of decision-making is often hailed as being effective in situations such as emergency 
because the decisions are made quickly. The naturalistic decision-making model can perhaps 
fit in here as the model means entails making decisions based on situations.  
In examining leadership of CEOs in decision-making, transformational leadership involves 
inspiring followers to adopt the organisation’s vision and goals while providing intellectual 
stimulation to enhance innovative thinking and individual development (Bass, 1985; Burns, 
1978). Transformational leaders exhibit charismatic behaviours that generate enthusiasm 
(Pawar, 2016; Chitiga, 2018; Tintoré, 2019) and commitment to collective objectives (Ribeiro, 
27 
 
et al., 2018; Sosik, et al., 2018; Faupel & Süß, 2019; Amankwaa, et al., 2019). They are role 
model of integrity, which builds trust and respect (Avolio & Yammarino, 2013; Khan, et al., 
2020; Liborius, 2021; Lee, et al., 2023). This leadership approach aligns well with financial 
decision-making that requires stakeholder buy-in, shared vision, and motivation to implement 
funding requirements. It is a broad-based perspective encompassing several facets and 
dimension of the process of leadership. Transformational leaders empower others (Koh, et al., 
2019) to participate in finance-related processes and contribute ideas (Men, 2014; Son, et al., 
2020). They encourage prudence, rational analysis, and objectivity in budgeting or funding 
requirements while appealing to emotions and values to gain support for decisions. These 
leaders help frame spending choices and investment trade-offs as opportunities for growth 
and advancement (Sashkin, 2004). By promoting collaboration (Jiang & Chen, 2018), 
transformational leaders can integrate diverse perspectives into financial planning to find 
optimal solutions (Bass and Riggio, 2006). However, potential downsides include followers 
depending too much on the leader’s vision or overemphasising ideology over practical 
financial considerations. The key characteristics of transformational leaders relevant to 
financing requirements include articulating a strategic vision to guide funding decisions. They 
outline a purposeful future state and link monetary priorities to achieving that vision. This 
visionary guidance clarifies how budgets, expenditures, and investments should advance 
organisational goals. Rather than getting mired in short-term constraints, the leader's vision 
compels forward momentum. Transformational leaders motivate their teams by linking 
funding-related decisions to long-term goals. They stimulate their sub-ordinates working in the 
financial department of the organisation with their positive and sound decisions specific for the 
benefits of their organisational long term financial plans. Monetary decisions are seen as more 
than numbers; they are innovative (Pieterse, et al., 2010; Khalili, 2016; Hui, et al., 2018) to 
make those decisions which have a significant impact on their organisation. Such brings 
accountability within the organisation through their team commitment across all stakeholders 
to the financing requirements of the organisation. Their charismatic personality and rational 
arguments at the top management encourage their team to work collective towards their 
28 
 
visionary and innovative decisions (Van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013; Thoroughgood & 
Sawyer, 2018; Hawkins, 2021; Carreiro & Oliveira, 2019).  
While the transactional leadership style, leaders decisions are based on the available 
information and that relies within the organisational rules and regulations without considering 
their personal factors to influence their decisions (Berkovich & Eyal, 2021). The main objective 
is to achieve the organisational target and a reward is offered by leaders to their followers in 
the exchange of such targets (Ma & Jiang, 2018; Kalsoom, et al., 2018; Baškarada, et al., 
2017).  Transactional leaders put practicality ahead of insight when it comes to money matters. 
Their concentration is on enhancing operational efficiency (Deichmann & Stam, 2015; Afsar, 
et al., 2017; Suong, et al., 2019), eliminating inefficiencies (McCarthy, et al., 2008; Liu, et al., 
2011; Wen, et al., 2019), and seek rewards (Ma & Jiang, 2018; Puni, et al., 2018; Nurlina, 
2022) by adopting proven financial methods financing within their organisational established 
policies and regulations. However, this approach can sometimes over-emphasize transactions 
(Jermias, et al., 2018; Robin & Sendjaya, 2019; Juliana, et al., 2021) themselves versus long-
term vision or a plan. Without inspiration, transactional leadership can struggle to gain 
commitment for decisions with diffuse or uncertain benefits. Their financial judgments are 
grounded in rationally assessing facts, projections, comparisons, and financial implications. 
This approach, which is evidence-based and analytical, seeks to accomplish highly 
compassionate monetary related financing decisions. Both transformational and transactional 
leadership styles help leaders to choose their funding preferences. That can lead them to 
make decisions within the decision making theories, the assumption is that the decision-
makers are intelligent and properly informed. Being an informed decision-maker means that 
there has been a prior process of gathering and analysing information. The following table 
includes the key comparision between both leadership styles.

From the definition of leadership given earlier on, it is clear that leaders are faced with 
situations that require them to make vital financing decisions that may bring change to the 
organisation. The literature presented herein discussed the models and theories of decision-
making with respect to CEOs' leadership styles. It has been expressed that leaders have one 
critical role – making decisions. How leaders make their decisions, solely depends on their 
leadership styles and information available to them. Therefore, the leadership theories provide 
useful lenses for connecting a leaders’ style to financing decisions. While transformational 
leadership inspires vision, innovation and motivation, transactional leadership levers concrete 
incentives and oversight. Other leaders are focused on ethics, stakeholder needs and 
transparency. In summary, within the leaders  styles, the leaders decision can be rational 
based on factual data (Uzonwanne, 2016; Groeneveld, et al., 2017; Gati, et al., 2019), or as 
per beliefs, attitude or perception as per cognitive theory of decision making (Wu, 2020; 
Andrade, 2020; Featherman, et al., 2021) and upper echelons theory (Hambrick, 2007; 
Altarawneh, et al., 2020; Ma, et al., 2022; White & Borgholthaus, 2022) or in the natural context 
(Turpin & Marais, 2004; Boyes & O’Hare, 2011; Carmody-Bubb, 2023) or these decisions can 
be based on the logic with reasoning (Zhou, et al., 2020; Acree & Acree, 2021). The fact that 
30 
 
the leaders deal with various factors and situations in and outside the organisation and adopt 
suitable leadership styles to make financing decisions.  
2.1.3. 
Financing Theories  
The concept of financing theory includes the study of various ways by which SMEs raise funds 
for their businesses. In the corporate finance, it is important for an organisation to understand 
how funds are raised and allocated to various projects, operations and organisational tasks 
while considering the risks factors. There are two types of sources of funds, internal and 
external for the SMEs (Abdulsaleh & Worthington, 2013; Allen, et al., 2013; Eniola, 2021). 
Internal sources of finance are cash that is retained from the business profit after paying 
interests, taxes and dividends, assets financing or owners’ own investment while the external 
sources include debt or equity financial contribution to the businesses (Watson & Head, 2007).  
External finances are normally classified into short-term (less than a year), medium (usually 
one to five years) or long-term (more than five years) debt or equity finance (Watson & Head, 
2007). Financing decisions for SMEs are crucial for their smooth run of the business by 
keeping in view the benefits of such funding for both business and shareholders. These 
decisions are based on what portion of debt and equity should be used to increase the value 
of organisation (Baker & Martin, 2011) and enhance the shareholders’ worth (Rappaport, 
1998; Hakimah, et al., 2019; Liu, et al., 2022).  
There are two main financing theories applicable to SMEs – the trade-off theory and the 
pecking order theory (López-Gracia & Sogorb-Mira, 2008; Kokeyeva, 2019; Agyei, et al., 2020; 
Hoang, et al., 2021). The trade-off theory implies that organisations set a target debt-to-value 
ratio and work towards it (Myers, 1984; Aidoo, et al., 2022; Nawir, et al., 2023). The value in 
this theory means the worth of the organisation. In other words, the organisation uses mixture 
of debt and equity by balancing the cost and the benefits of debt (Baker & Martin, 2011; 
Kontesa, 2015; Alan & Gaur, 2018). In the trade-off theory of financing, top management set 
target debt to value ratio of the organisation, take advantage of tax benefits over loan against 
the cost of financial distress. If the company increase its debt amount or percentage ratio 
31 
 
against equity, it gets more tax benefits. In simple words, they prefer debt over equity and 
believe that current value of the debt provides tax shield which ultimate increase the value of 
the organisation (Baker & Martin, 2011; Ezirim, et al., 2017). The pecking order theory is 
opposite to the trade-off theory where there is no exact target debt to value ratio (Agyei, et al., 
2020). In this theory, organisations prioritise their sources of funds, the first option is internal 
financing, then borrowing and the last option is equity (Myers, 1984; Serrasqueiro & Caetano, 
2015; Simatupang, et al., 2019). In the pecking order theory of financing, internal generated 
funds e.g. retained earnings of the business is more preferred option for SMEs over external 
funding, and so external debt over equity investment. In this case, SMEs prefer loans or 
external sources of finance if they lack internal funds (Baker, et al., 2020) and still not prefer 
external investors or any outsider equity to be invested in their businesses if there is no option. 
The empirical study on trade-off and pecking order theories has mixed results. For example, 
Newman et al. (2012) found that larger SMEs are more inclined to use external financing due 
to their lower cost of financial distress, and they also found a negative relationship between 
profitability level and debt financing, which supports the theory. Their results were also 
consistent with the Pecking Order theory, especially for profitable SMEs with sufficient internal 
retained funds. Profitable SMEs tend towards internal funding options that rely less on external 
debt or equity financing to support their business financial needs. The findings of Shyam-
Sunder & Myers (1999), Hussain & Matlay (2007), López-Gracia & Sogorb-Mira (2008), 
Degryse et al. (2009) and Godke Veiga & McCahery (2019) confirm that SMEs’ financing 
decisions support the pecking order theory. The smaller or growing SMEs with limited 
financing credit history prefer to fund their business with internal sources of funds such as 
retained earnings or personal finance. If they have limited resources, they will prefer external 
debt. The above findings oppose the results of Mukherjee & Mahakud (2012), Mazen (2012) 
and Moritz et al. (2015) that SMEs’ financing decisions support trade-off theory due to several 
factors, e.g. the economic recession where SMEs prefer to fund their businesses with external 
debt. In addition, some SMEs' financing decisions support both pecking-order and trade-off 
32 
 
theories, such as the findings of Hashemi (2013), Serrasqueiro & Nunes (2014), Michaelas et 
al. (1999) and Sjur et al. (2008).  
  
2.2. Fintech Development 
FinTech is technological innovation designed to provide the financial products and services 
(Legowo, et al., 2020; Lagna & Ravishankar, 2022; Xu, et al., 2023). The technology is 
designed to incorporate computer programs and the financial configurations to drive efficiency 
in the financial services. FinTech was developed to offer the needed competition to the 
traditional financial delivery methods. The technology and the financial aspects are combined 
to produce a revamped and leveraged financial model that ensures efficiency in the delivery 
of financial services. The emergence of FinTech is a financial crash 2008 (Puschmann, 2017; 
Alt, et al., 2018; Giglio, 2021; Gopal & Schnabl, 2022) that increased the lending opportunities 
to small businesses via alternative sources of finance such as P2P lending, crowdfunding and 
Cryptocurrency.  
FinTech has now become a game changer that took the traditional banking by storm during 
the period of reluctant to change after the global economic recession. The funding through 
Fintech is efficient due to digitalisation and the use of technology. According to Godfrey (2018) 
the Fintech is evolving and enhancing users experience with better and efficient business 
models. The evolution of Fintech industry to FinTech 3.0 is underpinned by the changes in the 
consumer mindset where they view the FinTech as a legitimate and resourceful alternative 
source of finance in the delivery of financial services (Arner, et al., 2015). However, it is not 
known when an economy is fully slumped, will FinTech survive. For example, in 2019, it was 
predicted that 2020 would be another big year for FinTech due to projected deals to issue 
number of digital banking licences, product expansions, open finance and increase in 
cybersecurity features  (KPMG, 2019). However, in the reality, the first quarter of 2020, the 
FinTech funding dropped to $6.1 billion since 2016 due to effects of Covid-19 (CBInsights, 
2020), the lowest in the history and in the last three years (Lloyd, 2020). That does not mean 
33 
 
that the SMEs funding through traditional banks have increased, in fact, that have also 
decreased to its lowest level in the history (Canocci, 2020). With 30% contraction in UK 
economy by the mid of 2020, banks like HSBC, RBS, Barclays and many others have already 
seen a sharp falls in their shares since 2008 financial crash (Mustoe, 2020) which in turn has 
increased opportunities for FinTech financing. The International Monetary Funds (IMF) and 
the World Bank have also endorsed the innovation in Finance due to spread of digital 
technology around the world. In 2018, IMF and World Bank have issued FinTech guides for 
policy makers where main elements of digital finance are encouraged, risks and threats are 
highlighted and FinTech initiatives around the world are encouraged (International Monetary 
Fund, 2018).   
 
2.2.1. 
History of Fintech and Evolution 
The use of technology in financial services can be traced back since 1965 when Giovanni 
Caselli invented pan telegraph to verify signatures in banking transaction (Zimmerman, 2016; 
Gray, 2022). The pan telegraph was the first Fintech, but it was much slower in speed to 
process 25 words in 108 seconds to complete the financial transaction (Teler, 2023). In 1866, 
the telegraph introduced the trans-Atlantic cable to provide infrastructure for financial services 
(Fari, 2016). Trans-Atlantic cable was much better in speed to process financial transactions 
due to its efficiency features and the scope of market. In late 1800s, goods were exchanged 
with credits (Bindseil, 2019).  
In 1918, Morse code system was introduced to connect all Reserve banks and to transfer 
funds in between them (Hockett, 2022; Sreekala, et al., 2023). Following the invention of credit 
cards in 1950 (Kahf & Mohomed, 2016), Quotron System was introduced in 1960 – a first ever 
electronic system (Novocin, 2022).  In 1993, financial technology was coined with the creation 
of Financial Services Technology Consortium (FSTC) (Schueffel, 2016). In 1998, internet 
banking was introduced in the United State (Furst, 2000) which further revolutionised the 
world’s financial system. The following the financial crash in 2009, the world evident the 
34 
 
release of Bitcoin Cryptocurrency as a result of financial innovation (DeMartino, 2018). 
However, the term FinTech was first used in 2014 (Anyfantaki, 2022). The following timeline 
chart further illustrate the whole evolution of FinTech since 1866.  
 
Figure 2: FinTech History and Evolution 
 
One of the most referred triggers of Fintech development was the financial crisis in 2007. The 
Financial crash of 2007-2010 almost paralysed the global banking system. A dark cloud was 
cast on the global financial market. Several banks were closed, Stock market nosedived 
considerably as well the property prices in the global economy slumped. The downfall of 
Lehman Brothers in 2008, a global banking giant sent the greatest shock waves in the global 
financial system. The financial crash was unpredicted and as a result of it hundreds of financial 
institutions in the USA and Europe were closed and some merged with stronger partners to 
keep them alive (Lybeck, 2011). The economy was much down as compared to 1930s 
depression and GDP fell 4.9 percent in the UK that affected businesses and individuals 
(Lybeck, 2011; Liuhto, 2020). The collapse of large financial institutions and the tremendous 
decline of international stock markets – together with the subsequent economic crisis has
