been in the centre of debate among financial policymakers, economists and the central bank. 
The profound regulation and economic issues after the crisis also catalysed the growth of 
FinTech to solve the economic problem (Allen, et al., 2014; Jakšič & Marinč, 2019; Yadav, 
2020). 
In 2019, the Covid-19 pandemic affected people and organisations around the world, and it 
ultimately unsettled the world economy. In the UK, Covid-19 affected more than the global 
financial crash because its GDP fell by 19.8% in early 2020 due to national lockdown (Office 
for National Statistics , 2023) and that brings the household expenditures down to over 20% 
as compared to 4.8% of the 2007 global financial crash (Office for National Statistics , 2023). 
During the Covid-19 pandemic the demand of Fintech applications increased to confirm the 
higher adoption of Fintech (Le, 2021; Khan, et al., 2021; Fu & Mishra, 2022; Tut, 2023). The 
UK Fintech investment has increased over the years £21.9 billion in 2021, at least 90% higher 
than the previous year 2020 which represents £2.2 billion (KPMG , 2023). However, in the first 
half of the 2023, these Fintech investment decreased to £4.6 billion due to high inflection, 
higher interest rate and war conflict between Russian and Ukraine (KPMG , 2023). The first 
half of the 2023 investment is still 52% higher than the 2020. The remarkable development 
which emerged recently are use of open banking, internet of things (IoT), artificial intelligence, 
biometric and buy now pay later. In the open banking individuals (people and businesses) 
share their financial data securely with third parties with their consent to compare improved 
financial services offered by banks (Ramdani, et al., 2020; van Zeeland & Pierson, 2021). 
Internet of things improve the customers’ experience e.g, the contactless payment where 
customers can pay bill using the mobile application without carrying the actual cash or bank 
cards (Kvartalnyi, 2023).  The use of Chatbot’s by financial institutions to answers customers 
queries without by break (Zoi, 2021), face recognition (Satuluri, 2021) and fingerprints are all 
artificial intelligence in FinTech to enhance customer’s experience (Kumar, et al., 2021). The 
biometric in FinTech is widely used to verification and identification of the person accessing 
the sensitive financial data or banking (Dubey, 2019). The commonly used biometrics methods 
36 
 
in Fintech to support the banking app or other financial services are the verification by the use 
of SMS, authentication by face or fingerprints, voice, and hand geometry (Dubey, 2019; 
Ganesh, 2021). Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) in Fintech has recently become popular in the UK 
with 17 million customers (Money Expert, 2023) to get short term finance and pay the full cost 
of the product and services in equal monthly instalments with zero interest rate (Koesworo, et 
al., 2019; Gerrans, et al., 2022; Guttman-Kenney, et al., 2023).  
 
2.2.2. 
Recent development of Fintech around the world 
The FinTech market has grown during the last decade around the world. Financial institutions 
have invested heavily to increase their market size, growth and to provide efficient services to 
the customers in this digital age. In 2010, more than 24 billion US dollars were invested in 
FinTech start up worldwide (Desai, 2015). In 2014, investment on FinTech business venture 
was 12.21 billion US dollars while in 2015 it was 11 billion US dollars which has resulted into 
total global growth of 201% (Nicoletti, 2017). In 2017, investment on FinTech start-up was 
around 27 billion US dollars, 18% more than of 2016 investments (Novack, 2018).  
In the Europe only, the remarkable growth of Fin-tech market took place in 2014, with Europe 
experiencing substantial growth - UK and Ireland were experiencing above 40% growth 
(Accenture, 2015). The massive progress in financial technology was marked in 2015, where 
profound progress has been recorded by companies, especially PayPal, First Data, and World 
Pay. In 2018, Stripe and Square are great examples of Fin-tech with amass valuations of $9.2 
and $18.9 Billion (Gefferie, 2018). Recently, triggered by little access to credits based on 
collateral securities and credit ratings, the FinTech innovative has moved to mitigate the 
financial obstacles faced by entities and individuals through lending via FinTech P2P system 
(Conner, 2013). The investment size and the global growth rate rely on the lifecycle of the 
FinTech evolution. A product, system or a process has normally four stages in its life cycle. In 
the introductory stage, the cost is higher, and the return is lower due to less customers, 
demand or public confidence. The growth stage is characterised by a good investment, sales 
37 
 
and customers. The maturity stage is more competitive stage due to stable and well-
established technology in the financial services while at the decline stage FinTech may have 
further innovation to attract new customers and retain existing market. FinTech initiatives 
around the world still not in its maturity stage (Nicoletti, 2017). In 2023, the Fintech has overall 
2% shares of the global financial services and it is estimated to reach 25% by 2030 (Boston 
Consulting Group, 2023). However, evidence from growth trend suggest that FinTech has 
become one of the main players in the financial world. In the recent years, the FinTech has 
developed a lot across the world such as in the UK and EU (Appendix 2), USA (Appendix 3), 
Asia (Appendix 4) and the rest of the world (Appendix 5).  
Organisations have unique financing needs to operate their business functions, the majority 
of which are not currently addressed by the traditional financial institutions, such as the banks. 
Due to such unique needs, many organisations are currently turning to the Fintech financing 
as a means of meeting such financial needs. Techniques, such as improved customer 
experience and efficient IT enabled models offer opportunities for FinTech - challenger banks, 
and other non-financial institutions to provide funds for businesses (EY, 2018).  
Forbes Magazine noted that the global banking industry are facing challenges from Fintech 
financing due to the growing usage and popularity of technologies, such as Blockchain as well 
as the dominance of millennial (Sorrentino, 2015). Fintech has significantly changed the way 
financial services are offered and consumed; through greater convenience and lower costs of 
operations (Arner, et al., 2015). The different technological innovations that have been central 
to the Fintech growth include cryptocurrencies, blockchain, crowdfunding, and peer-to-peer 
(P2P) lending amongst others. The Fintech industry has grown to be one of the most 
accessible for the businesses in the UK (EY, 2018) due to their ability to meet the unique 
needs of the organisations. Revolutions, especially the movement towards Fintech 3.0 has 
allowed new and established businesses to acquire financial products and services through 
Fintech. Due to this evolution, the Fintech financing is viewed as a resourceful and legitimate 
source of financing (Arner, et al., 2015). These financial services are delivered to the 
38 
 
businesses through the application and usage of technology. Catalini, et al. (2017) noted that 
Fintech refers to the movement towards decentralisation, disintermediation, as well as 
digitisation that is enabled by information technologies, such as peer-to-peer networking, 
machine learning, Blockchain technology, and Open APIs. The use of Fintech financing 
through P2P lending, Crowdfunding, Neobank and cryptocurrencies for the businesses are 
explored below.  
 
2.2.2.1. 
P2P Lending 
Peer to Peer lending is one of the applications of the FinTech to offer the digital financial 
solutions (Arjunwadkar, 2018; Hamarat & Broby, 2022; Gu, et al., 2022). These are online 
platforms that unite lenders and borrowers that would otherwise not exists through the 
traditional banking models. This innovative approach links up potential investors with 
individuals seeking finance needs (Mattarocci & Gibilaro, 2016; Yan & Zhou, 2023). The 
investor is assured on high returns with low risks being involved. The borrowers with good 
credit history can access credit at lower rates. The platforms have enablement that can 
analyse past loan history and the social media interaction patterns. In the UK, Zopa was the 
first Peer to Peer platform, introduced in 2005 (Coulter, 2018). Later, hundreds of other online 
platforms sprung up to have a share of the demand for the online lending needs by the 
customers in need of finance. These platforms offer affordable and convenient P2P 
connections to the customers in the UK financial market.  
In the process of P2P lending as illustrated below in the figure 3, in which borrowers and 
lenders first register to the P2P online platform. Borrowers are either individuals or companies 
who seek funds in terms of loan for their personal or business use while lenders are investors 
who seek a good deal to get return on their investment. Investors usually get interests on their 
principal loan amount. In the process of registration, borrowers have to provide their IDs, bank 
account and other personal information for their credit report. Borrowers are group of people 
who initiate the lending procedure by indicating the amount they want to borrow with 
39 
 
information of how much interest they will on the principal loan amount. On the other hand, 
lenders are group of people who choose borrowers and fund them loan.  
 
 
Figure 3: P2P Lending a conceptual process – An illustration 
 
Peer to Peer lending is a major source of funding for the SMEs in the UK. It is estimated that 
out of €2.9billion issued out through the online lending platforms in 2017, €1.9billion went 
towards the improvement of SME businesses in the UK (Mattarocci & Gibilaro, 2016). 
Therefore, the system is salient towards SME growth. In the current financial crises of 2020 
with uncertain future, the UK government is supporting SMEs with its new schemes of 
Coronavirus business interruption loan and bounce back loan in which P2P lenders are also 
eligible to get accreditation the British Business Bank. In fact, the British Business Bank 
already work with various P2P leaders to support government backed loan schemes for 
businesses and SMEs (Gaw, 2020). 
Race setter is an online P2P platform that was introduced in 2009 (Orca, 2018). It was 
developed on the concept of ‘'provision fund'' whereby internal insurance is maintained to 
cushion against borrower default.  The platform became operational in 2010. By the month of 
May 2017, over €2 billion loan amount had been processed through the platform and about 
400,000 lenders and borrowers had been connected. Peer to Peer lending ensures access to 
higher returns with lower risks on investment (Mach, et al., 2014). The risks in investment are 
spread through the provision of diversified and smaller units of loans advanced to the 
40 
 
borrowers. The investors earn interest faster since the period of loan repayment is shorter 
(Mohamed & Ali, 2019). The initial capital invested can easily be recovered on demand from 
the online platform. This online platform has the drawbacks such as the risk of default by the 
borrower hence loss in the capital if there is no insurance on the amount invested. The interest 
earned from the loan advanced are taxable hence reduces the margins realised. Borrowing 
may not be processed immediately since the customer data has to be analysed before 
approval of a loan request.  
Peer to peer (P2P) lending platforms act as intermediaries between the borrowers and the 
lenders (Arjunwadkar, 2018); thus, taking the previous roles of banks.  Since P2P is made up 
of a number of lenders, the lenders are able to pool their risks across the various SMEs. The 
first UK P2P lending platform Zopa started operations to connect individuals with lenders 
(Arjunwadkar, 2018). However, P2P lending expanded to businesses, especially SMEs in the 
UK. As of 2015, P2P financing of SMEs stood at £1.5 billion, which is equivalent to 6 per cent 
of all financing of the SMEs in the UK. P2P financing for the SMEs accounted for 22 per cent 
of all the new loans to small businesses in the UK. Zhang, et al., (2016) noted that the average 
P2P financing indicate that it is mostly focused on the small businesses market. The UK P2P 
sector is likely to grow in 2023 up to 1.6% to reach total value of £376.6 million (Gaw, 2023).   
The growth in the P2P SME financing has been driven by the various demand and supply 
factors in the UK. According to Atz & Bholat (2016) the argument that the supply side of the 
P2P SME financing in the UK may have witnessed the strong growth may be ideological, for 
instance, the lenders are focused on ways through which they can have control over the 
manner in which their financing is invested. Baeck, et al. (2014) noted that the most important 
motivation in the supply side of P2P SME financing is the financial gain as the survey indicated 
that 82 per cent of the P2P lenders offered the SME financing as a means of gaining financial 
returns on their investment.  
On the demand side, the most important motivation for the SMEs to use P2P financing is the 
competitiveness in the interest rates as well as the increased speed of access to the financing. 
41 
 
P2P also caters for a segment of the market that would remain unsatisfied (Baeck, et al., 
2014). A majority of the SMEs in the UK, especially the newer businesses cannot satisfy the 
conditions for traditional bank financing, for instance, the inability to provide collateral or even 
have long-term relationships with the banks. To such SMEs, P2P lending is the only alternative 
that they can use to get financing for their business activities.  
 
2.2.2.2. 
Crowdfunding  
Crowdfunding is a collective process where a group of individuals pools resources or funds 
through online sites to support an organisation or a project (Arjunwadkar, 2018; Bao, et al., 
2022). The resources and the people are mobilised to achieve a given objective such equity 
capital, donation, start-up capital etc. (Mohamed & Ali, 2019). Crowdfunding is also known as 
social media fundraising since the entire fund generation process occurs through an online 
platform (Lynn, et al., 2019).  
Crowdfunding process involves few steps to raise funds (Harrer, et al., 2023) and it usually 
involves a project (Arjunwadkar, 2018). A project can be a new idea, product, service, event 
or a campaign with a clear purpose and an outcome to raise funds or the project can be based 
on sourcing funds for existing businesses. Fundraiser, through crowd funding platforms, raise 
small amount of money from large number of peoples by using crowd funding FinTech 
platforms. The entire process is illustrated in the figure 4. 
 
 
Figure 4: Crowdfunding a conceptual process 
 
42 
 
The crowd funding differs from traditional financing because fundraises do not require to 
present a business plan or have to go through due diligence and credit checks to raise funds. 
In this process, fundraisers just create a new product, idea, service, event or any campaign 
which has a clear funding amount and a targeted specific date. Once it is decided that the 
specific amount to be raised, fundraises normally create a video or specific document with 
description about the project for the audience. Once the project is advertised to the crowd 
funding sites, then funds are raised from number of peoples or businesses around the world. 
These people or businesses represent the term ‘crowd’ in the crowd funding and they have 
their expectations, desires and independent decisions to finance the project. In the UK, 
crowdfunding sites such as Crowd cube, Kickstarter, seedrs, crowd funder, fundit.buzz are 
some of the commonly used crowdfunding platforms. These sites also charge their own 
commissions which is a percentage of the specific fund.  
While the crowdfunding market is still small in the UK, in terms of financing of the SMEs, there 
has been significant growth from the time of the financial crisis of 2008. Online platforms 
facilitated £3.2 billion of donations, investments and loans to the SMEs in the UK in 2015 
(Zhang, et al., 2016). The global crowdfunding market is growing continuously and it is 
projected for $1.41 billion in 2023 from $1.25 billion in 2022 (Fortune Business Insights, 2023).   
According to the UK’s Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), crowdfunding covers the various 
donation, equity and loan-based online models (FCA, 2023).  
The more commonly used crowdfunding for SMEs is equity based where the crowd funders 
invest to seek a potential return on the business shares in exchange of equity (Arjunwadkar, 
2018; Chu, et al., 2017). The main benefits of investors are the return on equity. Equity-based 
crowdfunding allows investors to be able to purchase shares in SMEs and other unlisted firms. 
The main motivation of the investors is to share the returns from the SMEs. Equity-based 
crowdfunding is mostly used by the SMEs in the UK (Wachira & Wachira, 2022; Erasmus, et 
al., 2022; Li, 2022) for capital expansion, start-up or seed capital; an indication of the breaking 
of the pecking order theory of funding preferences (Baeck, et al., 2014). The theory holds that 
43 
 
SMEs would traditionally prefer internal financing from family, friends and founders before the 
SMEs can even consider the use of external financing.  
Appendix 6 provides the details of different types of crowdfunding in the UK.  
 
2.2.2.3. 
Neobanks and Challenger Banks SME Financing 
Neobanks are Fintech driven technology (Banga, et al., 2023) or digital lenders who offer 
specialised financial services for the SMEs, especially since such SMEs are underserved by 
the incumbent traditional lenders (Lu, 2018). The reasons for the existing underservice of the 
SMEs is explained above under the Traditional SME financing subtopic. Despite the Neobanks 
being authorised by the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and regulated by the FCA, the 
Neobanks have changed the way SMEs in the UK use bank financing. Some prominent 
examples of Fintech Neobanks in the UK include Atom, Monzo, Starling, OneSavings, 
Aldermore and Tandem amongst others (Lu, 2018). Some of the neobanks in the UK have 
concentrated their services on offering specialised financing for the SMEs. Further, SMEs in 
the UK also use neobanks financing as such banks are customer-centric in terms of ethical 
culture adoption and fairness. Since the neobanks are mostly online-based, they have 
developed distinct distribution systems that allow them to reach many SMEs as opposed to 
the current practice of many traditional banks that still focus on the traditional brick and mortar 
branches.  
Even though the neobanks, just like the traditional banks, take deposits from the public and 
then lend the loans to the borrowers, they tend to offer tailored financing options to the SMEs 
(Erel & Inozemtsev, 2022), for instance, SME mortgage, invoice and asset finance (Lu, 2018).  
Taking the example of asset finance, the UK SMEs are able to gain financing for the purchase 
of a relevant asset and then make affordable monthly rents or payments. Further, invoice 
discounting offered by the neobanks to the SMEs in the UK allow the SMEs to maintain credit 
control as well as the collection facility (Lu, 2018).  
44 
 
Due to the online operations of the neobanks, there are lower costs of operations that are 
passed on to the SMEs in terms of competitive interest rates for the financing. The financiers, 
the neobanks, are also able to achieve higher profit margins due to the lower costs. Aldermore, 
one of the challengers/ neobanks in the UK, recorded 3% of the total asset financing to the 
SMEs in the UK in 2015 (Lu, 2018). While in 2020, The UK challenger Starling bank raised 
€44.3 million to support SMEs (Tucker, 2020). In 2023, UK neobank Revolut is the most 
funded bank of worth of $1.71 billion funds (Fintech Global, 2023).  
The significance of the digital-based neobanks to the SME financing in the UK is twofold. First, 
the neobanks play an important role in the channelling of credit from the depositors to the UK 
SMEs through bespoke loan services, such as commercial mortgages, invoice discounting, 
and asset financing; thus, providing the necessary financing for the operating cash and credit 
for the SMEs. Secondly, through the market niche, the neobanks have recorded significant 
growth and have become very profitable. As of 2014, the neobanks that were listed in the 
FTSE All-Share Banks Index outperformed the traditional lenders thus generating significant 
returns to the lenders (Lu, 2018). The specialised market target coupled with the cost-
effectiveness in their operations has enabled the neobanks to compete and even outperform 
the traditional banks.  
 
2.2.2.4. 
Cryptocurrency 
A cryptocurrency is a form of disruptive currency (Burniske, 2015) that involves encryption of 
information thread to represent a currency (Hafishina, et al., 2023; Lo, 2022; Valdez & 
Hernandez, 2022). It is a digital and decentralised currency (Nafis, et al., 2019). The currency 
is reviewed and monitored through a Peer to Peer network called Blockchain (Mohamed & Ali, 
2019). The blockchain is a digital ledger that ensures a secure and an easy mode of 
maintaining the transactional data, agreements and contracts (Lansiti & Lakhani, 2017) and 
generate cryptocurrencies (Arjunwadkar, 2018) . Cryptocurrency is formed through a 
cryptographic algorithm that is verifiable and stable through the mining process (Zhang & Xue, 
45 
 
2019). Examples of this currency include Bitcoin, ether, Litecoin etc. Companies in the UK are 
investing in blockchain following recognition by the parliament as a digital financial product 
(Monaghan, 2018). The currency is gaining popularity in the UK. The UK has immense 
potential of being the FinTech hub because of the advanced progress in the use of the digital 
financial product (Monaghan, 2018). 
Forbes noted that cryptocurrencies have a great potential for the UK SMEs to gain borderless 
financing access (Clarke, 2018). The expansion of blockchain has led to the SME financing 
on the condition of invoices ensuring that payments are prompt. The first successful initial coin 
offering (ICO) called Debitum was launched in September 2018; raising $17 million that would 
be available for the financing of SMEs. ICO entails the use of blockchain technology to create 
digital tokens by SMEs to investors (OECD , 2019). The digital tokens are then exchanged for 
first-generation cryptocurrencies, such as Bitcoin. ICOs are enabled through the use of 
Distributed Ledger Technologies (DLTs), such as blockchain that allows for the value 
exchange without the need for a trusted intermediary or central authority. The DLTs allows for 
improvement in efficiency due to the disintermediation.  
Although presently not used for the SME financing in the UK, there is a significant potential, 
especially the potential for profit-making. ICOs have the potential to create value through the 
potential network effects and efficiency gains enabled through blockchain technology. 
However, there is limited legal clarity in the use of cryptocurrencies in the UK; thus, can pose 
some risks to the SMEs that participate in the ICOs. In theory, the use of cryptocurrencies as 
an alternative Fintech financing model would lead to the democratisation of the SME financing, 
as well as distributing the control amongst the token holders and the SMEs; rather than 
concentrating the power of decision making in the hands of the traditional financiers for SMEs 
(OECD , 2019). Cryptocurrencies will also allow the SMEs in the UK to be able to diversify the 
financing options (Phạm, 2022) by appealing to the financiers in terms of the characteristics 
of the projects that the SME is engaged in as well as the profitability. Through the use of DLTs, 
46 
 
there would be significant efficiency gains from innovation and automation application thus 
further driving the disintermediation.  
In theory, the SMEs will share in the efficiencies with the investors; thus, leading to lower costs 
of financing as compared to the use of IPOs. Further, cryptocurrencies would also provide 
SMEs in the UK with access to a large pool of investors that increase the potential for the 
SMEs to gain funding. However, in practice, there would be some risks to the investors as the 
lack of central control or authority can expose such investors (OECD , 2019). ICOs have a 
significant potential to overcome some of the challenges that SMEs in the UK face at the initial 
stages coupled with the unwillingness of the SME owners to give away the equity in their firms 
at the early stages of the firm's existence. ICOs is an example of how cryptocurrencies can be 
used as an alternative financing model for the SMEs.  
The main benefits of cryptocurrencies in the financing of the SMEs in the UK include cost 
efficiencies in terms of the efficiency gains from the automation and use of blockchain, and 
the cost savings as a result of disintermediation (Miller, et al., 2023; Alshater, et al., 2022; 
OECD, 2019). The advantage is the inclusive SME financing through the unrestricted access 
to investors, active participation of the SMEs in the investor networks as well as the SME 
financing democratisation. The other benefits of cryptocurrencies in the SME financing are 
speed, liquidity and flexibility (Busayatananphon & Boonchieng, 2022; Harris, 2023; Daidai & 
Tamnine, 2023) through faster execution speed, almost immediate liquidity, as well as the 
ability of the SMEs to invest in a fraction of the token. The other potential benefits include 
direct access and diversity due to unlimited access to global investor pools (OECD , 2019). 
The other benefits that SMEs in the UK would gain from the use of cryptocurrencies in the 
financing include value creation through network effects monetisation as well as no dilution of 
rights or even the conferring of the entrepreneur’s ownership rights.  
Despite the existence of the potential benefits from the use of cryptocurrencies in the financing 
of SMEs, there are also a host of limitations. First, there is uncertainty with regards to the 
regulation. Since the regulatory framework for the ICOs is not clear in the UK, the SMEs and 
47 
 
other participants (investors) would be exposed to significant risks (Zetsche, et al., 2018). 
Regulatory uncertainty exists in the DLTs in terms of the enforceability of the contracts as the 
UK is yet to develop standard laws on Smart Contracts. Further, there is limited investor 
protection due to the volatility in the prices of the crypto-assets thus raising concerns about 
the suitability of the instruments to the investors. Finally, there is also a general lack of 
regulatory compliance and corporate governance. Regulatory reporting is significantly lacking 
and due to this, the large investors and stakeholders can have great space to manipulate the 
prices (OECD , 2019). Despite this, SMEs in the UK have increasingly been drawn to the use 
of crypto assets in the financing of their activities due to the lower costs, associated with the 
financing, the faster speeds as well as the limited requirement for long-term relationships 
before funding can be offered.  
 
2.2.2.5. 
The role of technology in Fintech  
The FinTech market is expanding rapidly throughout the world due to the increase in the usage 
of smartphones and the digital transformation of financial service organisations to innovate 
their systems (Anifa et al., 2022; Guo, 2023; Chin et al., 2023). FinTech has introduced new 
business models where digital platforms are used to meet the needs of customers or 
businesses. These platforms are technology-enabled, such as peer-to-peer, crowdfunding, 
and cryptocurrency. The world has invested a lot during the last decade in FinTech to meet 
users' needs. According to EY Global (2017), 48% of users worldwide, especially the young 
generation aged 25-34, prefer technology-enabled Fintech platforms to make payments and 
transfer money. The technological changes in the world economy have led to massive 
variations in structural financial management by different organisations. Technology has a 
significant impact on the economy and influences organisations’ financing decisions. That is 
because technology provides efficiency way of interaction to exchange of information between 
the financial providers and the companies (Chang, et al., 2012). The blockchain in finance is 
well known technology to confirm the secure monetary transactions for P2P and 
48 
 
cryptocurrency (Onyekwere, et al., 2023). It is based on machine learning, a branch of Artificial 
Intelligence (AI), where system learn from the raw data, perform specific tasks, and make 
decisions without minimal human involvement. In summary, the blockchain and technology 
embedded models facilitate FinTech financing. The main triggers of FinTech are the excessive 
use of technology in this digital world (Kasiewicz, et al., 2023; Langley & Leyshon, 2023; 
Rupeika-Apoga & Wendt, 2022) to deliver financial services (Natarajan, et al., 2017). Due to 
this technology enable features in Fintech, it reduces the high cost of funding by traditional 
banks has led SMEs to consider Fintech financing.  The challenges experienced during the 
global financial crisis, the highest inflation, Covid-19 disasters, and the highest inflation are 
addressed by technology enabled FinTech. For example, during the period of Coronavirus 
outbreak in the world, the use of social media has increased in the UK and Europe as 
compared to previous years (Johnson, 2020). Social media such as Twitter, Facebook, 
Instagram, LinkedIn and interactive platforms such as P2P and crowdfunding enable people 
interact online for their financial needs. In the recent report published by eMarketer (2018), 
there are estimated 2.55 billion users use social media and mobile applications and due to 
this, mobile commerce is estimated to reach $278.9 billion in 2018. The mobile e-commerce 
has reached to $2.2 trillion in 2023 to accumulate 60% of all e-commerce sales in the world 
(Buchholz, 2023).  
Keeping in view the threat of Fintech, the financial sectors such as banks have started to use 
software solutions offered by FinTech which are easily integrated by application program 
interfaces (API). Banks started to partner with FinTech companies without relying on heavy 
investments to overcome their existing hurdles and enhance their payment and lending 
innovation. The prominent examples are Barclays partnership with MarketInvoice and 
rainmaking, Lloyds with Nayaone, HSBC with monese, Natwest with Vodeno (Kumar & 
Chakraborty, 2022). Accepting new and modern technologies depends on several factors, 
including user-friendliness, easy access to funding and efficiency to influence users’ behaviour 
to use of Fintech platforms.   
49 
 
 
 
2.3. CEOs’ decision making on financing and the research 
framework development 
 
2.3.1. 
CEOs’ funding preference 
CEOs of organisations, including SMEs, often focus on achieving sustainable competitive 
advantage (Srivastava, et al., 2013; Kumar, 2016; Kahupi, et al., 2021; Quan, et al., 2021). 
Being the driving force of SMEs, CEOs' financing decisions are essential to their organisations’ 
success in the competitive market. The sustainable competitive advantage is a significant 
point of concern in SME financing (Sadalia, et al., 2020; Peprah & Ayaa, 2022), especially in 
the face of substitution and disruptive innovation (Ghemawat, 2006; Christensen, et al., 2015) 
due to FinTech (Chen & Li, 2019; Zhao, et al., 2019; Reyes-Mercado & Reyes-Mercado, 
2021). FinTech entails various technologies, such as big data analytics, blockchain 
technology, machine learning, open APIs, and peer-to-peer networking (Catalini, et al., 2017) 
to provide innovative financing solutions (Puschmann, 2017; Rajeswari & Vijai, 2021; Giglio, 
2021). Traditionally, SMEs seek financing solutions from financial institutions, for instance, 
using credit cards, bank loans, leasing, asset finance, and commercial mortgages. Alternative 
traditional finance includes grants and government schemes to support SMEs in the UK (EY, 
2018). However, many SMEs rely on their internal funds, such as savings (Hussain, et al., 
2006; Jiang, et al., 2014; Rao, et al., 2023), and many, at least 36%, seek finance from their 
directors and family members at their first instant before moving to traditional financial 
institutions (British Business Bank, 2021).  
In the competitive market, CEOs' financing preferences can be their strategic choice or the 
role of technology to influence their behaviour as to which mode of financing should be 
preferred for their organisations. For example, in the SMEs that traditionally relied on 
traditional financing by banks, the rise of FinTech in the financing space can adopt several 
possible strategic responses (Ghemawat, 2006). The response can be in the form of not 
50 
 
responding at all, where an organisation maintains the traditional financing options they have 
always used. Organisations which don’t respond to the change are less innovative (Chan, et 
al., 2019). The other strategic direction is combining financing from both traditional financing 
sources while adopting FinTech's new financing. Switching strategy, which entails the shift to 
the exclusive use of FinTech financing stimulates innovation (Trabucchi & Buganza, 2019). 
Closely linked to substitution is the concept of disruption (Kivimaa, et al., 2021). Christensen, 
et al. (2015) identified that disruption refers to the process when the new entrants with limited 
or fewer resources challenge the existing companies. The majority of CEOs face a dilemma 
about whether they can maintain the status quo or adopt radical changes to the financing 
(Christensen & Raynor, 2003). The main considerations are regarding the risk and potential 
opportunities in the new financing options available under FinTech. However, these strategic 
choices depend on the business life cycle (Madhani, 2014; La Rocca, et al., 2011; Johnson, 
et al., 2020), e.g. at the cycle of their business start-up, growth and maturity stage. The 
sources of funds for start-up businesses are particularly related to entrepreneurial finance 
(Alemany & Andreoli, 2018; Anwar, et al., 2020; Da Rin & Hellmann, 2020), where decisions 
can be based on borrowing from friends, family, crowdfunding, incubators, accelerators, 
business angels, venture capital and through private equity. Meanwhile, the existing 
businesses have challenges to grow and sustain in the competitive business environment. 
The sources of funds (appendix 1) for established businesses depend on the unique needs 
(Keary, 2020), strategic decisions (Johnson, et al., 2020), funding strategies (Shepherd, et al., 
2015) and many other reasons such as cost and efficiency of transactions. For example, SMEs 
use Fintech financing due to the efficiencies gains from the use of blockchain (Pizzi, et al., 
2021; Alshareef & Tunio, 2022; Bag, et al., 2023) and the cost savings as a result of 
disintermediation (OECD , 2019; Pasterny, 2021; Muda, et al., 2021). Since the banks are not 
involved in such financing, the costs for the SMEs are lower, thus leading to greater 
affordability of the funding. Another reason is that SMEs fear that using traditional bank loans 
would lead to a loss of control (Burgstaller & Wagner, 2015; Yazdanfar & Öhman, 2015; Jude 
& Adamou, 2018). In this sense, SMEs prefer to use alternative financing through Fintech to 
51 
 
ensure they maintain control of their businesses. Further, the use of FinTech financing offers 
unrestricted access to the investors for quick cash in flexible negotiated terms, which 
traditional financing does not offer (Fenwick, et al., 2018; OECD, 2019; Bollaert et al., 2021). 
However, despite the benefits of Fintech financing, the published corporate reports such as 
Small Business Market Report 2018-19 confirmed both funding through traditional banks and 
Fintech also increased over the years (British Business Bank, 2018) and gross lending to 
SMEs by banks increased every quarter up to 14 billion since 2014 to 167 billion in 2018 
(OECD, 2019). The current survey report suggests that the external funding for SMEs was 
higher during COVID-19 due to business support, and it significantly dropped to 30% in 2023 
from 59% in 2021 (British Business Bank, 2024) . According to the report by British Business 
Bank, SMEs' awareness of alternative external funding sources increased since 2012 such as 
P2P lending 24% to 53% in 2017 and crowdfunding 13% to 60% in 2017 (British Business 
Bank, 2018). The awareness for alternative sources of funds such business angels, 
marketplace lending, crowdfunding remained consistent in the past four years (British 
Business Bank, 2024).  
The technological changes have led organisations to move towards innovative funding 
options. Decision-makers such as CEOs have the capability to prefer technological innovation 
if it provides them with better facilities such as convenience, efficiency and sustainability for 
competitive advantage. With regards to the alternative sources of Financing via Fintech, 
Bruton, et al. (2015) undertook a study that investigated the new financing options available 
to SMEs and entrepreneurs to start and grow their ventures, such as P2P lending and 
crowdfunding, through a systematic approach framework. The research has often focused on 
the risks and opportunities of the technologies; for instance, Lindman, et al. (2017) explored 
the risks and opportunities of blockchain in payments. The research by Lindman, et al. (2017) 
explored risk and opportunities based on three sets of issues: technological design, 
organisational and competitive environment issues. The opportunities have been explored by 
the research undertaken by Lemieux & Lemieux (2016), who explored the value of blockchain 
52 
 
technology in preserving and creating trustworthy digital records. The trustworthiness of the 
records is an important consideration for the CEOs who explore the financing options for their 
SMEs. On the other hand, Chuen, et al. (2015) explored the elements of digital technologies 
through the use of the LASIC acronym (Low margin, Asset Light, Scalable, Innovative and 
Compliance easy) as the most essential principle for FinTech success. The research explored 
M-PESA (a payment solution developed by Safaricom PLC, a subsidiary of Vodafone) and 
Alibaba by examining how they aid financial inclusion (Chuen, et al, 2015).  
Systematic and rigorous research on FinTech is still at a very infant stage due to lacking a 
robust empirical and theoretical base. Most of the existing research has been undertaken by 
banks, government bodies and consultants, with the exception of a few researchers. However, 
the bulk of theoretically grounded research is found in crowdfunding, an important financial 
capital source for SMEs and other nascent entrepreneurs (Bruton, et al., 2015). There is a 
significant gap in the literature as most of the existing literature focuses on adopting e-banking 
as opposed to FinTech and the available financing models, such as crowdfunding, P2P and 
cryptocurrency. A number of research undertaken in the past explored the adoption of mobile 
banking due to the advancement in technology (Lule et al., 2012; Aboelmaged & Gebba, 2013; 
Ooi & Tan, 2016; Marakarkandy et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019). Much existing research focuses 
on different aspects of Fintech, including its benefits and its adoption due to a number of 
competitive advantages it offers. For example, research by Piyananda and Aluthge (2023) 
identifies the increasing trends in Fintech acceptance due to COVID-19, considering the 
opinions of CEOs. The outcome confirmed that a number of factors such as trust, Fintech 
features and awareness are key factors for the positive trend of Fintech in the market. A similar 
study identifies easy-to-use and perceived benefits as the main drivers influencing consumers 
towards Fintech (Singh et al., 2020; Nangin et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021). The other existing 
research have mixed results regarding the adoption of Fintech over traditional mainly because 
it offers the reduction of financial cost (Carlin, et al., 2017; He, et al., 2017; Najib, et al., 2021; 
Kumari & Devi, 2022; Ornelas & Pecora, 2022; Elliehausen & Hannon, 2023), saves time 
53 
 
(Setiawan, et al., 2021) and provide safety and trust (Hasan, et al., 2021). Emerging markets 
where Fintech is preferred mainly because of the higher financial literacy (Nugraha, et al., 
2022). Further, Fintech advancement poses risks to commercial banks due to its influence on 
CEOs to get alternative sources of funds (Vasiljeva & Lukanova, 2016). In the competitive 
market, CEOs' roles have become crucial for their businesses' competitive advantage and 
long-term sustainability by considering their financing preferences, which include opportunities 
and challenges, either through traditional financing routes or Fintech. The research question 
relating to the CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing choices in the changing economic 
and financial technology environment is still unexplored. Therefore, the first research question 
is proposed below: -  
Research Question 1: How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing choices in the 
context of a rapidly evolving economic and financial technology environment?  
 
2.3.2. 
Key micro factors in FinTech financing decisions making 
Research on SMEs have been undertaken for a number of decades, especially on the role of 
leadership in making important decisions in organisations. Literature from the early 80s to date 
have indicated that the success of a firm is highly dependent on the possession of top 
management skills and capabilities (Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Analoui & Karami, 2003; Ahn, 
et al., 2013) that reflects CEOs’ productivity in their decisions. Scholars such as Ahn, et al., 
(2013) have theorised that since the majority of SMEs are highly centralised, i.e., the CEO 
has a larger and more critical role in the making of various important decisions such as finding 
suitable funding sources. The CEOs’ decisions are based on their leadership styles (Hoxha, 
2019; Cho, et al., 2019; Ugwu & Okore, 2020; Purwanto, 2021) and underpinning leadership 
and decision-making theories to make financing decisions. As the leadership qualities of CEOs 
play a central role in the adoption or non-adoption of innovations, The main focus of the 
following section is to identify the closely related literature and the empirical evidence on 
CEOs' factors in navigating their decisions. 
54 
 
 
2.3.2.1. 
Personal Leadership Qualities  
Leadership is a process or attributes of multidimensional skills (Sadler, 2003; Shakeel, et al., 
2019; Benmira & Agboola, 2021; Kwiotkowska, et al., 2022) and a relationship between those 
who desire to lead and those who are willing to follow (Gallos, 2008; Tannenbaum & Schmidt, 
2017; Kouzes & Posner, 2023). There is substantial literature and empirical research focused 
on identifying the leadership qualities for CEOs to make essential decisions in different 
organisational situations. These attributes of CEOs are primarily personal and related to their 
honesty, integrity, inspiring others, good communication, creativity, reliance and vision 
(Hasan, 2019). According to Northhouse (2016), the personal traits of leaders are those 
characteristics which individuals possess to influence others to achieve organisational goals. 
For example, achieving financing preferences for the benefit of their organisations in the long 
run. Scholars also describe leadership as inborn qualities of individuals (Morkes, 2009; Singh, 
2012; Toh & Ruot, 2019), and due to those inborn attributes, individuals are comfortable to 
inspire and lead others in the organisation. In the organisation, the most common functions of 
leadership are establishing a vision, setting strategies, communicating goals with people, 
building teams, and motivating, aspiring and empowering followers (Northhouse, 2016; 
Sumayya, 2023). That also includes power as one of the personal characteristics of leaders 
to influence others within the organisation (Spence, et al., 2013). For example, CEOs have 
the power to make important funding decisions. According to Ross (2015), there are several 
personality dimensions of leadership, such as self-esteem, how people feel about themselves 
as leaders and their personal values to serve as a code of conduct to develop their leadership 
qualities. Individuals’ feelings about themselves are consistently dominant by their self-
confidence. Self-esteem is associated with leaders’ self-motivation and attributes such as 
attitudes, beliefs and actions (Ross, 2014; Axelrod, 2017; Anninos, 2018). In today’s world, 
organisations prefer to have people perform different tasks, such as managing and leading 
people and projects (Morkes, 2009; Leal Filho et al., 2020; Malhotra, 2021). These 
organisations, regardless of their size, offer training to improve their employees’ leadership 
55 
 
skills so they can make important decisions. That is evident from the UK Department of 
Education's current management and leadership apprenticeship scheme for management 
(Department of Education, 2020) to enhance the people's leadership skills in the organisation. 
Whereas CEOs’ personal leadership qualities are important factors in influencing their 
decision-making (Connie & Cull, 2018). Differentiation between different types of leaders and 
their leadership styles is essential to identify the contribution of their personal leadership traits 
to justify their financing preferences. This has been ostensible from the following empirical 
evidence, closely linked to the literature relating to the CEO's personal leadership qualities to 
influence their decisions to accept innovation and make financial decisions.  
Kaplan, et al. (2012) found that CEOs have leadership traits that include respect, efficiency, 
and development of people, personal integrity in their work, calmness, strategic visions, pro-
activeness, oral communication, and interpersonal and intellectual skills. These leadership 
traits contribute to their decisions and are positively linked to organisational success. Chen et 
al. (2013) identified CEOs' transformational leadership styles as personal qualities of 
dominance, self-confidence, desire to influence others and strong moral values. This is 
consistent with the charismatic leaders' qualities of being fighters, intellectuals, persuasive in 
communication, entrepreneurs, and strategic in having long-term visions (Avolio & 
Yammarino, 2013). However, it is opposed to the traits of transactional leadership, which are 
considered to have the qualities of being logical decision-makers, practical, directive and 
responsive to their followers (Anastasia, 2018). Nadkarni & Herrmann (2010) identified CEOs' 
personal 
characteristics 
include 
emotional 
stability, 
openness, 
extraversion, 
and 
conscientiousness. Haas & Speckbacher (2017) identified controlling as the dominant 
personality factor of CEO leadership. In the other empirical evidence by Huang et al. (2019), 
the researchers emphasised CEOs’ power as a leadership trait is positively linked to 
organisational innovation. On the other hand, Simsek et al. (2010) identified that the self-
esteem and emotional self-control traits of CEOs’ leadership contribute to their decisions.  
56 
 
To conclude above, the success of SMEs is largely dependent on leadership. According to 
Ibarra et al. (2020), CEOs make creative and innovative decisions in organisations as per their 
leadership styles. Every style has different effects on the culture and effectiveness of an 
organisation. Leadership affects employee morale and productivity and sets the organisation's 
tone. CEOs of SMEs must embrace leadership styles consistent with their company objectives 
and grasp the subtleties of the position. The existing literature support leadership traits as 
inborn qualities (Nadkarni & Herrmann, 2010; Simsek, et al., 2010; Kaplan, et al., 2012; Chen, 
et al., 2013; Han, et al., 2016; Haas & Speckbacher, 2017 Huang, et al., 2019; Ibarra et al., 
2020). CEOs' leadership traits are more related to their confidence, personality, leading teams, 
integrity, and many other attributes that influence their decisions and are linked to the 
organisation's performance. Mixed findings by leading researchers confirm that leadership 
traits are either positively linked to the organisation's performance or that some traits, such as 
the CEO’s power, are negatively associated with the firm’s performance. However, these 
leadership traits help CEOs to make entrepreneurial decisions (Simsek, et al., 2010), which 
leads to financial innovation (Block, et al., 2021). 
The main emphasis of the above empirical evidence and literature is that leadership traits are 
multi-dimensional qualities of CEOs and characteristics they acquire during their career. 
These traits contribute to their organisational decisions, including financing, and these are 
associated with their leadership styles.  
 
2.3.2.2. 
Personal Vision  
CEOs are responsible for making decisions, leading their executives’ teams, and ensuring the 
organisation achieves the set goals. CEOs align the organisation around set priorities and 
shared goals and use their vision to guide their decisions. The CEOs' characteristics and 
personalities influence the strategic decision-making process of an organisation. A vision is 
an idealised goal that an organisation’s CEO envisions to achieve in the future (Men, et al., 
2020). Based on upper echelons theory, an organisation's top management influences 
57 
 
financial and strategic decisions (Collins, 2020; Ji et al., 2023). As strategic and visionary 
leaders, CEOs are better positioned to understand the organisational strategic direction 
through their strategic vision and choices that reflect their characteristics and use their 
influence to guide the decision-making process. There is congruence in existing findings that 
top executives of organisations play significant roles in creating and communicating a clear 
vision to stakeholders and employees when making strategic decisions (Ji et al., 2023; Men 
et al., 2020). Therefore, CEOs use their visions to enable organisational change toward 
achieving strategic and financial goals. Much research has been conducted on the CEO’s 
vision and organisational performance. When a particular CEO has a vision, they will make 
decisions aligned with the strategic vision. The success of the organisation depends on the 
vision of its senior managers, aligning their vision with the organisational vision (Berg, 2015; 
Slåtten et al., 2021) that improves the employee's performance in work. According to Maran 
et al. (2022), leaders use their visions to influence employees as part of ensuing goal 
achievement. As such, CEOs who lead with vision and give employees autonomy are better 
placed to make effective decisions that shape the company. Evidence suggests that 
communication with a clear vision allows leaders to influence decision-making and set goals 
aligned with long-term strategic objectives (Collins, 2020; Maran et al., 2022). Prabhu and 
Srivastava (2023) associate transformational leaders who are visionary with better 
performance of SMEs. Transformational leaders communicate their vision to their 
subordinates and use that information to develop solutions faced by SMEs. These leaders can 
inspire employees, fostering a shared vision in an organisation. Based on empirical evidence, 
transformational CEOs communicate their vision, encourage their team to develop innovative 
solutions and mentor them to realise high-performance standards (Prabhu & Srivastava, 
2023). Therefore, a transformative leader’s vision is integral in promoting successful decision-
making, including strategic changes within SMEs (Ng & Sears, 2020; Prabhu & Srivastava, 
2023). The role of CEOs is to ensure the sustainability and competitiveness of organisations, 
which can be achieved by communicating their vision. Evidence suggests that CEOs have 
considerable influence and power within companies and are responsible for determining the 
58 
 
direction (Ji, et al., 2023). CEOs use their power during decision-making to motivate, inspire, 
and guide team members toward the set organisational goals. Unlike Men et al. (2020), Ji et 
al. (2023) established that although CEOs have power and influence, they use their position 
to communicate the organisation’s visions. Therefore, organisational vision is vital in guiding 
the decisions held by CEOs because they have a responsibility to different stakeholders.  
Several studies have explored the impact of CEOs' vision articulation on a firm's sustainability 
(Ashford, et al., 2018). CEOs are essential in SMEs since they lead their growth through 
strategic decision-making (Li, et al., 2020). The research by Lee et al. (2022) associates the 
CEO’s vision for CSR with better-performing SMEs. Cognitive CEOs will improve 
organisational performance and promote CSR activity (Li, et al., 2020). Better organisational 
performance will be achieved when the CEO has a clear vision. Yuanyuan et al. (2022) 
affirmed that a CEO articulating a strong vision goes for innovative strategies and takes 
calculated risks. Notably, this gives an SME a competitive advantage. The capabilities of a 
CEO enable a firm to achieve a competitive advantage (Huang et al., 2019; Shahab et al., 
2020). Friedman and Carmeli (2022) noted that although executives might struggle to form a 
coherent vision when a CEO has great implementation capacity, they can transform an 
organisation into a better position. The role of CEOs cannot be underestimated; they are seen 
as the drivers of an organisation’s success through their decisions. The CEO’s role is 
becoming complex and very demanding. Therefore, they should envision where they want to 
take their company (Citrin, et al., 2019). 
According to Neely et al. (2020), numerous studies have explored how executives’ traits affect 
their actions and how these influence an organisation’s performance. A visionary leader 
develops a good relationship with the employees in an organisation, which acts as a source 
of inspiration (Nwokedi, 2015). The personality traits of a CEO are essential in dictating the 
effectiveness of a company. CEOs better understand their traits to motivate employees and 
achieve organisational goals and objectives. Since vision is a vital element in CEOs’ 
leadership, a leader’s vision gives a clear and compelling picture of the future, and this acts 
as a source of inspiration for employees (Kurdyukov, 2023). Kurdyukov (2023) noted that 
59 
 
through a clear vision, employees have a better understanding of the purpose of the 
organisation they are working for and the strategic direction the organisation is taking. Preller 
et al. (2020) noted that effective vision communication guides a team in an organisation 
towards a common goal, a significant characteristic of effective leadership behaviour. A 
leader’s vision guides the allocation of resources in an organisation. A CEO with a clear vision 
allocates resources in a manner that leads to efficient and effective usage of resources. 
Visionary leadership leads to organisational agility (Yilmaz, 2023). All the above empirical 
studies have supported the idea that an organisation’s CEO’s vision influences decision-
making, leading to better organisational performance, employee motivation, innovation, and 
effective resource allocation. However, it is not known whether vision helps in their financing 
preferences.  
 
2.3.2.3. 
Values, Family and beliefs 
Values are general principles that guide human actions, in particular, provide direction to their 
attitudes, beliefs and behaviour (Connie & Cull, 2018; Duréndez, et al., 2023) and integrity 
(Liborius, 2021) in their decision-making (Schmoldt, et al., 2001; Pasewark & Riley, 2010; 
Sutherland, 2022). The empirical evidence to justify this claim is reflected through the closely 
related study of Löfving, et al. (2013). They found that personal values guide CEOs within the 
organisational culture. That organisational culture works on a set of beliefs to influence CEOs' 
ways of thinking and action, which ultimately influence their decision-making. Gorgievski, et 
al. (2011) concluded with similar findings that there is a positive relationship between small 
business owners’ values and their organisational success. The findings of Gorgievski, et al. 
(2011) and Löfving, et al. (2013) are aligned by the research of Berson, et al. (2008) who found 
that CEOs' values include self-direction of creativity, safety, independence in decision-making, 
kindness, and security values, and these values provide them with a framework or self-code 
of conduct in their roles within firms. The results of the study support that the value and 
creativity of CEOs result in positive outcomes for the organisation, such as the adoption of 
innovation.  
60 
 
Further, CEOs with high values also create an innovative and supportive culture in the 
organisation. Yang & Lee (2019) investigated the inhibitors and enablers of the adoption of 
crowdfunding by SMEs. The research findings confirmed that the values are important and 
critical for the success of the innovation or the technology acceptance. The empirical evidence 
indicates that inhibitors such as complexity, operational costs as well and reputational risks 
have a negative impact on the value alignment of the CEO towards the adoption of 
crowdfunding in the financing of SMEs. The findings are similar and supported by Ryu (2018) 
research on Fintech users with regard to the impact on the adoption and the continuance 
usage of Fintech financing. Further, Gannon and Hieker (2022) also found that the internal 
stakeholders in Fintech SMEs are believed to focus on environmental, social, and governance 
for long-term financial stability, attract investors, and align with their values. In addition, 
Gannon and Hieker (2022) found that the participants wanted to be part of a firm that shared 
their values. A significant study by Vitt in 2004 confirms that individual financial decision-
making involves personal values (Vitt, 2004). Therefore, personal values play a crucial role in 
shaping an individual’s corporate decisions; however, due to limited research, it is not known 
whether values could be one of the micro factors navigating CEOs in their Fintech decisions, 
on which the current study will focus.     
The involvement of family members reflects the family’s strategic decision-making but is less 
explored in the literature (Buchanan, et al., 2022). There are mixed findings in the literature 
regarding the impacts of family members on the productivity of SMEs. For example, some 
researchers found that involving family members in the management of SMEs increases firms' 
performance while others found that relying on family members instead of professional 
managers could lead to inefficiencies in decision-making (Buchanan, et al., 2022). According 
to Irdiana et al. (2022), factors such as the presence of a reference group, family, and role and 
status are among individuals’ considerations in taking a loan. The higher influence of family 
members and reference groups increases an individual’s decision to take a loan (Irdiana, et 
al., 2022). Jonathan (2021) conducted a study and found that family background, financial 
61 
 
technology, perception of risk, and financial literacy influence investment decisions among 
millennials. Specifically, family background had positive effects on investment decisions. In 
this study, CEOs who shared their firm’s information with family members would be more likely 
to incorporate family members’ decisions when making Fintech decisions. Anderson & Reeb 
(2003) found that their family has an impact on the CEOs, and there is a positive association 
between the family CEOs and the financial performance of companies. The findings of 
Anderson & Reeb (2003) were supported by Chrisman, et al. (2004) research on small private 
US family and non-family-run businesses. Chrisman et al. (2004) found that family involvement 
in businesses positively impacts the firm's ability to achieve its economic and non-economic 
goals. The empirical evidence and literature signifying the role of the family.  
CEOs' roles, particularly behaviour and confidence level, impact their corporate decision-
making (Malmendier & Tate, 2015), including their religious belief (Baxamusa & Jalal, 2016). 
The prior research by Osoba (2003) confirms the significance of the beliefs on human actions. 
Osoba (2003) concluded that risk-avoiders people attend more churches than risk-takers. The 
results of Osoba (2003) are consistent with research by Hui & Hilary (2008) who found that 
firms in the most religious areas are risk avoiders in their decision-making. In the study of 
CEOs' religious affiliation to influence their decision-making, Baxamusa & Jalal (2016) 
indicated a solid religious implicate of CEOs in their decision-making. Results of the study 
further confirmed CEOs' actions and decisions due to beliefs impact the firm’s performance.  
In the causal study conducted by Setiawan et al. (2021), user attitude is influenced by the 
social factor which is the most critical factor in predicting Fintech adoption. There are three 
approaches to influencing an individual’s behaviours to social influence, including (a) 
internalisation, (b) identification, and (d) compliance (Alkhwaldi, et al., 2022). Internalisation 
and identification amend an individual’s beliefs based on subjective norms or social status. 
Compliance involves an individual’s amending their belief. In the context of this study, social 
factors guide CEOs in their Fintech financing decision-making, which can contribute to their 
beliefs about preferring Fintech financing. The FinTech solutions focus on factors directly 
62 
 
associated with CEOs' personal beliefs when making the funding choice. For example, a study 
by Mascarenhas et al. (2021) regarding the benefits and risks of using Fintech financing 
options can be based on technological innovation to provide perceived benefits and risks that 
could influence their personal beliefs. Mascarenhas et al. (2021) assessed four diverse types 
of perceived risks associated with the personal beliefs of the consumers: Legal, financial, 
operational risks, and security. The researcher stated that FinTech monetary losses are 
inversely related to continued use intent due to the inadequate operation of the financial 
transaction structure, money laundering, ethical risk, and excessive transaction charges 
related to the cost of early adoption. Given that the FinTech sector has no market precedents, 
the absence of regulation involving monetary losses or safety concerns might cause consumer 
worry or distrust (Mascarenhas et al., 2021). Gupta et al. (2023) stated that perceived beliefs, 
such as the trust of the consumer, affect the intention to adopt FinTech solutions. Fintech 
usage is connected with a relatively high risk of loss (in terms of privacy, personal data, and 
transactions), thus enhancing the perceived risk (Mascarenhas et al., 2021). In summary, 
personal belief becomes more robust if there is less risk and more benefits associated with 
technological innovation (Mascarenhas, et al., 2021; Abdul-Rahim, et al., 2022) that can also 
influence perception or attitude (Setiawan, et al., 2021) to use Fintech.  
The empirical evidence above confirms that religious beliefs influence human behaviour and 
organisational corporate decisions. These corporate decisions are related to financing 
decisions such as funding through Fintech, debt, equity, or other sources of finance - 
diversification or investment (Baxamusa & Jalal, 2016). The current study will focus on drawing 
questions to identify the contribution of the belief and its impact on CEOs' perception or attitude 
to use FinTech because the prior research doesn’t confirm the personal beliefs' impact on the 
SMEs making Fintech financing decisions. It is also not known whether religious beliefs 
negatively or positively impact the organisation. 
To conclude above, values, family support, and beliefs held views are important factors that 
significantly impact CEOs' decision-making. These factors have greater significance for CEOs 
63 
 
to shape decisions in organisations. Stronger, more resilient businesses are always created 
by CEOs who diligently match their values with the objectives of their enterprises (Astrachan, 
et al., 2020). When CEOs' values align with the organisational goal and vision, it fosters a 
culture of integrity and purposeful decisions. Furthermore, a CEO's approach to decision-
making depends on their support from family support and beliefs. There are a number of 
findings to confirm that values (Vitt, 2004 Berson, et al., 2008; Gorgievski, et al., 2011; Löfving, 
et al., 2013; Ryu, 2018;  Yang & Lee, 2019; Gannon and Hieker, 2022), family (Anderson & 
Reeb, 2003; Chrisman et al., 2004; Jonathan, 2021; Irdiana et al., 2022) and beliefs (Osoba, 
2003; Hui & Hilary, 2008; Baxamusa & Jalal, 2016; Mascarenhas, et al., 2021; Abdul-Rahim, 
et al., 2022) navigate CEOs in their decisions. However, it is not known whether all these 
factors contribute to CEOs’ decisions regarding their financing preferences. 
 
2.3.2.4. 
Educational level  
Financial literacy contributes to effective decisions making (Kumar, et al., 2023; Duréndez, et 
al., 2023). Several scholars have studied the impact of CEOs' educational backgrounds on 
effective decision-making. Education has been explored to measure the individual’s 
competence and knowledge base (Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Hitt & Tyler, 1991; Lassnigg, 
2017; Bauman & Lucy, 2021). The prior research indicated that the CEOs with higher levels 
of professional and academic education as well as those with business degrees are generally 
expected to generate a more significant and wide range of creative solutions when faced with 
challenges thus, the level of education has been linked with firm performance (Norburn & 
Birley, 1988; Berberoglu, 2018; Mehralian & Nazari, 2018) and the innovation levels (Bantel & 
Jackson, 1989; Shanker, et al., 2017; Demircioglu & Audretsch, 2017; Lenihan, et al., 2019).  
Empirical evidence suggests that education is a significant factor for CEOs to help in their 
decisions and for their organisation. For example, Garnier (1982) found that CEOs and 
managers with high educational achievements are better at decision-making. Even though the 
research was undertaken when the FinTech industry was not developed, the views resonate 
64 
 
with the FinTech industry, especially concerning the view that better-educated CEOs are more 
open-minded to adopting new technologies. Jorissen et al. (2002) found that education 
significantly predicts a person’s values, cognitive preferences, and perceptions. Soriano & 
Castrogiovanni (2012) argued that education plays a vital role in the survival of a business. 
Further, the high levels of CEO education indicate a higher ability of the CEO to develop 
various strategic decisions and engage the company in innovation, such as innovative 
financing options as well as the development of strategic options (Xiaowei & Zhang, 2010). A 
prominent study by Loukil & Yousfi (2022) found that CEOs’ educational background and the 
type of academic degree are significant for them to bring innovation to their organisations. 
Education brings expertise to CEOs to make decisions, which are not limited to financing 
preferences but in many business areas. For example, Ali et al. (2022) found that education 
is a CEO’s attribute that influences their organisation’s performance and contributes to their 
financial decisions. The findings of Ali et al. (2022) are supported by the study of Gupta et al. 
(2021), who have similar results that CEOs’ education is a contributing trait to their decisions. 
These decisions are related to the investment (Gupta et al., 2021; Ali et al., 2022). In their 
study, Herdinata et al. (2019) explore the decision-making in SMEs and how it impacts the 
adoption of Fintech financing. They found that organisational productivity, especially the 
educational achievement and levels of leadership of the SMEs, plays a significant role in 
adopting Fintech. Herdinata et al. (2019) thus point to the need to develop the quality of human 
resources regarding training and other programs to ensure greater success in adopting 
Fintech financing by SMEs.  The findings of Herdinata et al. (2019) were consistent with 
(Duréndez, et al., 2023), who emphasised the CEOs’ literacy to implement technological 
innovation.  
To summarise, the CEOs in the organisations come from a wide range of educational 
backgrounds, which reflects the variety of routes to leadership style. Some CEOs of SMEs are 
self-made business people (O’Brien, 2009) who pave their routes to success with unwavering 
perseverance and real-world experience (Jokinen, 2023). Some have advanced degrees from 
65 
 
prestigious universities and are knowledgeable to make decisions relating to their 
organisations’ financing preferences. It is important to remember that education certainly plays 
a role in CEOs’ decisions (Bhagat, et al., 2010; Hmieleski, et al., 2015; Bai, et al., 2020). 
However, the influence of education on CEOs’ decisions as leaders is shaped by several 
elements, for example, the industry environment (Chen, et al., 2019; Lin, et al., 2019; Cortes 
& Herrmann, 2020) in which the CEO works is crucial, first and foremost. Advanced degrees 
can greatly benefit some industries where highly specialised knowledge is required, but 
flexibility and practical experience are valued more highly in others. SME CEOs balance their 
natural abilities and educational backgrounds while customising their leadership style to meet 
the financing needs of their company. Although education has many benefits, only one 
contributing micro factor of the complex puzzle defines a CEO’s decision-making in their SME 
environment. Considerable existing literature support that education level support CEOs to 
make decisions (Garnier, 1982; Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Hitt & Tyler, 1991; Soriano & 
Castrogiovanni, 2012, Lassnigg, 2017; Herdinata et al., 2019; Bauman & Lucy, 2021; Gupta 
et al., 2021; Ali, et al., 2022; Loukil & Yousfi, 2022; Kumar, et al., 2023; Duréndez, et al., 2023).  
Undoubtedly, education improves humans' skills and abilities to contribute to SMEs' adoption 
of financial technology. However, the literature lacks clarity regarding the significance of 
education or financial awareness for navigating SMEs’ CEOs in their financing decisions.  
 
2.3.2.5. 
Experience  
Research has indicated that professional experience contributes effectively to the decision-
makers (Alkhwaldi et al., 2022; Duréndez et al., 2023). Experience is crucial in determining a 
CEO's efficacy, especially in SMEs. CEOs with experience and industry-specific knowledge 
are frequently better suited to lead their companies toward success (Ng’ora, et al., 2022). The 
assumption is that senior managers who have professional experience place greater 
emphasis on making better decisions than those who lack professional experience. Peni 
(2012) argued that firms can significantly benefit from the experience based on the long tenure 
66 
 
of the CEO, especially if the CEO has been in the same position for an extended period. Such 
long tenure allows the CEO to develop more comprehensive and richer knowledge about the 
company (Finkelstein, 1992; Peni, 2012). Extending on the work by Peni (2012), the 
experience contributes as an integral part of the CEO's understanding of the funding needs of 
the SME as well as dealing with the challenges and opportunities in the competitive market. 
The high levels of CEO experience indicate a higher ability of the CEO to develop various 
strategic decisions and engage the company in innovation (Xiaowei & Zhang, 2010).  
CEOs make critical strategic decisions regarding the firm's direction (Ahn, et al., 2013). Daily 
& Dalton (1992) found that the CEOs of SMEs face more significant challenges and have more 
opportunities to make decisions than large firms, such as multinational companies. Further, 
CEOs of SMEs can have greater scope with experience than CEOs of large companies (Daily 
& Dalton, 1992). However, the research by Hambrick & Mason (1984) found that CEOs tend 
to make decisions that favour them based on their experience. A study by Zheng & Piao (2019) 
explored FinTech financing decisions from P2P and found that the experience of the CEO's 
human capital plays an essential role in adopting Fintech financing. The results suggest that 
new ventures and SMEs can use multiple ways based on the experience of the CEO to get 
financing through Fintech financing. Similarly, Mohd Thas Thaker (2019) found that CEO 
experience indicates greater knowledge that influences their behavioural intention to use 
crowdfunding. A research study by Moritz et al. (2015) found that the CEO's experience 
reduces the informational asymmetries between the financiers and the SME, thus increasing 
the adoption and the use of crowdfunding in SME financing.  
Therefore, experienced CEOs have the capacity to make well-informed decisions in 
organisations (Gordon & Martin, 2019; Woo, 2019; García-Pérez-de-Lema, et al., 2021). With 
the help of their extensive experience, they can confidently traverse challenging business 
environments, seeing opportunities and dangers in a wider scope than less experienced 
CEOs. They often possess a more sophisticated strategic vision as well, enabling them to 
meet the funding goals for their businesses that are reasonable and doable. Their ability to 
67 
 
successfully reduce risks is even more important. CEOs with experience can see possible 
problems and create backup plans (Ballaro, et al., 2021). Since they have faced comparable 
difficulties previously, they can apply the knowledge they have gained to steer clear of costly 
blunders. More evidence of the beneficial relationship between CEO’s experience and SME 
success has come from recent studies. These results highlight the importance of learning from 
past mistakes to improve leadership effectiveness (Ng’ora, et al., 2022). Leading researchers 
explored the phenomenon of CEOs’ experience as a contributing factor in their decisions 
(Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Xiaowei & Zhang, 2010; Peni, 2012; Ahn, et al., 2013; Moritz et 
al., 2015; Zheng & Piao, 2019; Mohd Thas Thaker, 2019). Different outcomes from these 
research confirm that experience improve the CEOs’ ability to make important decisions for 
the organisational growth and sustainability. Essentially, experience continues to be a key 
factor in SMEs' success, proving the proverb that "wisdom comes with time" to be true. The 
above empirical evidence confirm experience as a contributing factor to navigate CEOs in their 
decisions.   
 
2.3.2.6. 
Risk Appetite  
Risk taking is one of the factors that CEOs possess as an ability or feature to perform their 
managerial work (Shapira, 1997; Benischke, et al., 2019), such as financing decisions. The 
risk in decision theory distinguishes between certain and uncertain situations (Yoe, 2019). In 
certain situations, managers are aware of outcomes compared to uncertain situations where 
the probability of a desired outcome is unknown. For example, flipping a fair coin has known 
outcomes (heads or tails) and their probabilities, except the final result is uncertain. Therefore, 
psychologists consider individuals’ personality includes dynamics of risk perception and the 
risk taking appetite (Wallach, et al., 1964; Hampson, et al., 2006; De Bortoli, et al., 2019; 
Akhtar & Das, 2020; Dhellemmes, et al., 2021).  
There are few empirical studies on the risk-taking attitude as a factor for CEOs to influence 
their decision-making, ultimately affecting the firms’ performance. Cain & McKeon (2016) 
investigated the sample of 4012 CEOs from firms listed on the S&P 1500 index and found a 
68 
 
significant association between CEOs' risk-taking attitude and the firm’s overall risk. A risk-
taking attitude also positively impacts the firms’ value to complete high-value projects. 
Attitudes are internal human mental processes that assist people in choosing the best position 
as per their understanding of different situations (Hillson & Murray-Webster, 2007).  The 
findings of this research are supported by Cronqvist, et al. (2012), who found that CEOs who 
never take loans in their personal life are low-risk takers and have less or no leverage for the 
firms they work for. 
Risk-taking is also related to the confidence level of the CEOs, which may lead CEOs to 
become overconfident in their career. Overconfidence in decision-making is one of the factors 
that CEOs possess in their belief and has a positive relation with their risk-taking attitude 
(Glaser & Weber, 2007). Overconfidence is a quality in individuals’ personalities that puts 
weight on their judgements (Gray & Carlisle, 2013). Many studies establish that overconfident 
people are high-risk takers (Bernardo & Welch, 2001; Glaser & Weber, 2007; Sheffer & 
Loewen, 2019; Burkhard et al., 2023). An empirical study by Galasso & Simcoe (2011) on 627 
CEOs from 290 US firms from 1980 to 1994 found that overconfident CEOs are more 
innovative in accepting technology. Overconfident CEOs think their likelihood of failure in 
making major organisational decisions is less due to their risk-taking approach and wealth of 
experience. Due to this, their organisations benefit from being more involved in innovative 
projects and initiatives.   
Unquestionably, taking risks is ingrained in the personality of CEOs who embrace risk 
frequently (Salehi, et al., 2022; Ma, et al., 2022; Ingersoll, 2023) and play a key role in 
expanding their businesses. When others might only see barriers, these visionary leaders 
have the natural capacity to identify opportunities and are eager to take decisive action to 
make these opportunities a reality. However, the careful balance between taking risks and 
managing them determines whether such activities are successful. The significant 
consequences of CEOs’ risk-taking behaviour on the expansion and viability of SMEs are 
shown by research. CEOs who take measured risks provide their companies with creative 
69 
 
energy and push the envelope of feasibility (Benischke, et al., 2019). CEOs encourage 
innovative thinking among their staff members (Bagheri, et al., 2022), creating a climate 
supporting disruptive ideas' growth. This innovative culture promotes exponential growth and 
helps SMEs quickly pivot and adapt to changing market conditions. However, there is a danger 
associated with taking chances. In order to make sure that the risks they take are well-
considered and in line with the firm's goals. The empirical evidence supports the significance 
of a risk-taking personality in navigating CEOs in their decisions (Bernardo & Welch, 2001;
