Glaser & Weber, 2007; Galasso & Simcoe, 2011; Cronqvist, et al., 2012; Cain & McKeon, 
2016; Sheffer & Loewen, 2019; Burkhard et al., 2023). However, it is yet to explore whether it 
plays an important part in adopting FinTech and what contributes to CEOs becoming risk-
takers or avoiders in their financing decisions.  
The earlier literature indicates that dimensions of personal leadership (section 2.3.2.1), vision 
(section 2.3.2.2), values, family, beliefs (section 2.3.2.3), education (section 2.3.2.4), 
experience (section 2.3.2.5), and the risk-taking attitude (section 2.3.2.6) are micro factors. 
These factors are CEOs' own characteristics, which work as input for CEOs to contribute to 
their decision-making. The question relating to how CEOs in UK SMEs perceive the influence 
of micro factors to navigate their decisions regarding Fintech financing is unexplored. 
Therefore, after reviewing literature, the proposed second research question is: - 
Research Question 2:  How do CEOs of UK SMEs perceive the influence of micro-level 
factors, such as personal leadership traits, vision and risk-taking attitude on their Fintech 
financing decisions 
 
2.3.3. 
Meso factors in FinTech financing decisions making 
There are a number of meso factors external to CEOs to influence them in their FinTech 
decisions, such as professional networking, the board of directors in SMEs, and the 
organisational structure.  
70 
 
2.3.3.1. 
Professional Networking 
Professional networking is an essential resource for CEOs' technical knowledge in SMEs 
(West & Noel, 2009; Hmieleski et al., 2015; Aaboen et al., 2016; Margherita, 2022). It functions 
as a potent instrument that can provide access to a wide range of relationships, resources, 
and possibilities that may substantially impact these companies' expansion and prosperity 
(Adomako, et al., 2021). Networking offers significant advantages to SMEs, highlighting its 
critical role in overcoming the unique obstacles that these businesses frequently encounter. 
With professional networking, SME CEOs can access an extensive reservoir of information 
and experience, which is one of its main benefits, and that helps them to make financing 
decisions (Kvasov, et al., 2021). They can access many best practices and insights that can 
aid them in navigating the challenging business environment by establishing connections with 
other entrepreneurs, business leaders, experts and possible mentors. Additionally, these 
networks can help the SME community develop a culture of continuous improvement (Sigala 
& Chalkiti, 2015; Darwin, 2017) by facilitating the exchange of creative ideas and answers to 
shared difficulties. 
CEOs of SMEs create strategic alliances and partnerships through networking (Vanags, et al., 
2018; Agostini & Nosella, 2019; Vătămănescu, et al., 2022). These collaborations enhance 
the knowledge of executives working in the SMEs to make informed decisions, such as 
creating joint ventures and approaching more accessible markets for capital expansion. 
Furthermore, conferences, networking events, and industry get-togethers give SME CEOs a 
stage to present their goods and services to prospective customers and investors (Adomako, 
et al., 2021). Professional networks can provide good support to CEOs in their organisations. 
Peer-to-peer experience-sharing and advice-giving might facilitate SME CEOs' ability to 
address challenges like personnel acquisition, regulatory obstacles, and finance limitations. 
Furthermore, belonging to a support network can help entrepreneurs feel less alone, positively 
affecting their emotional and mental health. Extant literature has indicated that professional 
network involvement is significant in the CEOs’ strategy-setting regarding the expansion of 
71 
 
financing sources. The general assumption in the knowledge is crucial in the imperfect market 
for the senior leadership of SMEs to make financing decisions (Brahmana, et al., 2022). CEOs 
work with people in their professional networks to develop formal financing strategies. SMEs 
can gain massive benefits from the professional networks of the CEOs because they provide 
them with appropriate knowledge and information critical for meeting funding needs. 
The empirical study by Wang & Tu (2016) focused on professional networks by exploring the 
element of herding behaviour in informing the decisions of CEOs in the adoption of Fintech 
financing by SMEs. The study considered the online peer-to-peer (P2P) lending platform, 
explicitly considering the herding behaviour and the rationality of the herding behaviour in 
making decisions regarding adopting Fintech financing. Wang & Tu (2016) study indicated 
that herding behaviour leads to a significant increase in the take-up of the Fintech financing 
by the SMEs. To support Wang & Tu (2016), the findings of Xiaowei & Zhang (2010) confirm 
that the high quality and levels of professional networks of CEOs enhance their ability to 
develop various strategies that allow organisations to engage in innovation as well as the 
adoption of innovative financing options. The study found that CEOs can make significant and 
strategic decisions regarding the direction of funding based purely on the information gained 
from professional networks. The findings of the empirical evidence from Eze, et al. (2018) 
have similar results that CEOs and managers of SMEs seek and use relevant funding 
information through their professional networking to guide their decision-making regarding 
technology adoption. The involvement in networks has received greater attention in recent 
years (Aaboen, et al., 2016; Minai, et al., 2012; Al-Busaidi & Olfman, 2017; Margherita, 2022) 
due to the sharing of knowledge in the global marketplace and for the benefit of the business 
(Zizah, et al., 2007; Carmeli & Paulus, 2015). Rather than relying solely on traditional financing 
methods, globalisation has led to developing new financing models in the international market, 
such as FinTech financing. The networks’ involvement is highly linked to social media and 
technology, especially accessibility and ease of use to influence people’s behaviour (Jaradat 
& Faqih, 2014) and provides a competitive advantage to organisations (Jorissen, et al., 2002). 
72 
 
Existing studies do not confirm that CEOs' involvement in professional networking can 
contribute to their Fintech financing decisions. However, the empirical evidence demonstrates 
that increased CEOs’ involvement in professional networking has been seen as a significant 
predictor of a greater need for information for their decisions to achieve financial innovation 
(Langeland, 2007; Wainwright, 2015; Gruin & Knaack, 2020; Chang et al., 2022). 
 
2.3.3.2. 
Board of Directors 
Within SMEs, the board of directors is crucial in shaping the financial decision-making 
processes of the CEO. Empirical studies have emphasised the significance of the board's 
makeup and level of engagement (Topak, 2011; Samuel, 2013; Godos-Díez, et al., 2018; 
Thottoli, et al., 2019; Agarwal, 2020). An active and diverse board can significantly impact 
SMEs' governance and strategic decision-making (Zheng, et al., 2022). Fundamentally, the 
board serves as an essential internal check and balance system. It serves as a safety net, 
guaranteeing that the CEO and other leadership team members conduct themselves honestly 
and responsibly. Because resources and operations are frequently deeply entwined in SMEs, 
it is essential to have this monitoring because it makes it easier for choices to lack the 
appropriate checks and balances (Zheng, et al., 2022). The actively involved board members 
contribute many skills, industrial expertise, and varied viewpoints to the group. This variety of 
views encourages lively debates and more thorough decision-making procedures. A well-
organised board also helps to push the business on the right path and avert any problems by 
offering the CEO supervision, mentorship, and counselling. A board of directors is a group of 
experienced people who provide leadership, strategic direction and overall governance to their 
organisation (Calder, 2008; Stamm, 2019; Dicke & Ott, 2023). Directors perform their duties 
by following the company’s constitution, which includes decision-making as per their ability in 
the company's best interest (Companies House, 2019). The board of directors' structure varies 
as per the constitution and the organisation's size. The board rely on the information it receives 
from the CEO (Boggs, 2006) to set and implement its plans for effective governance (Croci, 
73 
 
2018). Therefore, the communication is vital for board success; the CEO mainly acts as a 
bridge to connect the board to the management by providing all information relating to day-to-
day operations or short-term goals through board meetings with board members (Price, 2018). 
The term ‘Governance’ is widely used in research, corporation websites, governance 
standards and literature. However, there is no single common definition of governance. It is 
described as a mechanism to guide company actions  (Nordberg, 2011), a system to direct 
and control companies (Financial Reporting Council, 2014), a process to exercise power 
(Lynn, et al., 2001), and a relationship between company management, a board, shareholders 
or stakeholders (OECD , 2004; Price, 2018). Corporate governance requires a difference 
between the board members and the executive management (Price, 2018) where board 
members don’t perform daily duties except the organisational management. 
In the UK, the evaluation of governance traces its roots in a sequence of corporate collapses 
and scandals in the late 1980s. In 1992, the Cadbury Report highlighted significant 
governance issues, which laid down the basis of the corporate governance code in 1998 
(Financial Reporting Council, 2010). It is a mandatory framework for the sustainable business 
success of listed companies on the London Stock Exchange to provide effective rights and 
accountability to shareholders. Even though there is no mandatory corporate governance 
framework for SMEs in the UK except regulatory obligations from Companies House. 
However, SMEs follow general practices of governance codes or similar frameworks as an 
inspiration to achieve good governance (Institute of Directors, 2017).  However, the board 
works for the interest of the company’s stakeholders by defining the organisation’s mission, 
developing plans (Leardini, et al., 2013) and working for the organisation's long-term survival 
by satisfying its stakeholders’ needs (Rolstadås, 1995). The board controls, monitors and 
leads the organisation internally and maintains compliance with external regulations to 
achieve accountability, transparency, quality or improvements. Therefore, the composition of 
the board and its governance framework are significant factors for SMEs to accept FinTech.   
74 
 
Within the board of directors, non-executive directors are individuals appointed to heighten 
the performance of the board and the company (Dunne & Morris, 2008) with their impartial 
and independent decisions (Institute of Directors, 2018). The roles of non-executive directors 
are very distinctive in SMEs because they are independent of the company management and 
hold considerable experience and knowledge to perform their duties (Cummings, 2018).  
To understand the role of non-executive directors, it is essential to understand the following 
three fundamental aspects of the Board as proposed by Dunne & Morris in 2008.  
 
 
Figure 5: Three fundamental aspects of the high-performance board, Source: (Dunne & Morris, 2008) 
 
According to the above figure, Dunne & Morris (2008) proposed that the high-performance 
board members know the board's purpose, the processes through which it performs its 
functions, and a good composition of people to bring expertise and decision-making qualities. 
These three fundamental aspects of the board achieve proper governance for the 
organisation. Another reason to appoint non-executive directors to the organisation is that UK 
corporate governance and unlisted organisation corporate governance codes suggest that 
there should be an independent element to the board for impartial decisions, which is suitable 
for effective governance.  
Due to the above reasons, non-executive directors in SMEs are appointed to bring expertise 
to the board to help other executive directors develop, deploy, and implement strategies for 
the company's long-term success. It is also worth mentioning here that non-executive directors 
75 
 
have the same legal obligations, duties and liabilities as their executive colleagues on the 
board per the Companies House Act 2006. Therefore, non-executive directors’ role is crucial 
to the organisation with their independent decisions because they perform their duties as 
agreed at the time of their appointment and by the law to ensure their actions add value to the 
board on its excellent governance (Dunne & Morris, 2008). Non-executive directors bring their 
personal qualities to the board, such as honesty, creativity, leadership, impartiality, 
communication styles, aptitude, flexibility (Dunne & Morris, 2008), influence, and strategic 
thinking skills. This helps the organisation not only to bring outside experience to the board 
(NHE, 2019) but also SMEs prefer to have them to add credibility and commercial introduction 
to their businesses (Hurley, 2010). Therefore, including non-executive directors on the board 
is also one of the factors that affect the adoption of FinTech. 
Evidence from the prior research confirms the significance of the board of directors and its 
influence on the CEO’s decisions and the firm’s performance. For example, Thottoli et al. 
(2019) found that the board of directors’ roles are essential in directing and controlling the 
company, and they impact the overall performance. The researchers further confirm that the 
board of directors influences CEOs, which results in the reduction of agency costs. The 
arrangement of the board creates little control of the shareholders in the management, which 
decreases the agency cost (Katti & Raithatha, 2018; Baysinger & Butler, 2019; Vitolla, et al., 
2020; Uyar, et al., 2021; Kyere & Ausloos, 2021). An important finding of Samuel (2013) 
confirms that the large board size brings more expertise and financial stability to the 
organisation because the influence of such a board on CEOs has a more significant impact. 
The large board size is suitable for governance because it enhances the organisation’s value 
(Samuel, 2013). The findings of Samuel (2013) are opposed to the results of Topak (2011), 
who found no link between the firm's financial performance and the board's size. However, 
evidence from previous research found a negative relationship between the board size and 
the financial performance of the firms (Florackis, 2005; Irina & Nadezhda, 2009; Nanka-Bruce, 
2011). The results of the empirical research are inconclusive regarding how the board affects 
76 
 
CEOs in their decision-making, especially in the case of UK SMEs. This is because the board 
structure in different organisations varies, including its impact and the flow of information 
between the board, the CEOs, and the shareholders for CEOs to make financing decisions.   
 
2.3.3.3. 
Organisational Structure  
Organisations are groups of individuals interconnected to the external environment and have 
structured and coordinated systems to achieve goals (Daft, 2008; Mills, 2011; Sessa & 
London, 2015; Baltaci, 2017; Valeri, 2021). Groups of people are linked to each other as social 
entities; they work together to design, develop, and follow rules and regulations to achieve 
specific tasks in line with their purpose. Top Managers of the organisation intentionally create 
the structure into different units and departments, allocate their resources and ensure that the 
structure is fit for managing the business operations (Daft, 2008; Daft, 2015; Valeri, 2021). 
The organisation's structure helps to organise the organisational activities, and employees are 
differentiated from each other based on their skills to perform those activities (Freeman, 1999). 
Thus, the operations are based on the pattern of activities within those structures that 
members perform to achieve the organisational goals (Aquinas, 2010).  
It is essential to understand the organisational structure because if it is responsive to the 
external environment, it can work more proactively towards accepting financing innovation 
such as FinTech. The rationale of the structural design is based on the assumption that 
structure has its purpose and usually relies on the hands of individuals who govern the 
organisation to create differentiation and a line of authority between workforce so that that 
they work for the organisation (Dow, 1988) and bring innovation (Zammuto & O'CONNOR, 
2018). These differentiation processes create distinctive coordination between operational 
and managerial roles in different units and departments based on their high level of expertise 
per their job description (Janićijević, 2013). Therefore, the organisation's structure balances 
its external and internal environment through its distinctive features of job design, the line of 
77 
 
authority and the differentiated units, and the coordination between them and the external 
environment.  
Suppose the structure is designed with little interaction between people and the external 
environment. In that case, these types of organisations find it hard to accept change or to 
accept change or technology. It is necessary to understand different types of SME 
organisational structures. Top management typically follows two kinds of models to design the 
organisational structure. These are mechanistic and organic organisation structure models 
(Mills, 2011; Ahmady, et al., 2016; Parthasarthy & Sethi, 2018). These structures are 
illustrated below as 
 
 
Figure 6: Mechanistic and Organic models of Organisation design, Source: (Weaver, 2019) 
 
In the mechanistic structure, the organisation is divided into different departments with a clear 
line of control from the top management or departmental line managers. Information flows 
from the bottom of the structure to the top management for decision-making. Therefore, these 
organisations are considered more challenging to accept new changes, innovation, or 
technology (Daft, 2008; Valeri, 2021). The information flows from the top managers to the 
bottom at the departmental line managers as orders or instructions to perform specific tasks. 
In these structures, organisational work is divided into smaller pieces at the departmental level 
and allocated to employees who are specialised and closely supervised by their top 
management to perform their jobs (Nieuwenhuizen, et al., 2012). These organisations are less 
78 
 
risk takers to accept change and have less networking between employees across different 
departments, and control of tasks is centralised at the organisation's top (Daft, et al., 2010). 
In the organic structure, the organisation group of people work collectively flexibly with a high 
span of control and in a high centralisation environment to accept innovation with their shared 
set of values and knowledge to accomplish the specific task (Nieuwenhuizen, et al., 2012). 
Top management of SMEs set up this organisational structure to create a high level of 
networking, quick problem solving, promote flexibility (Aquinas, 2010) and decentralised 
decision making (Daft, 2008; Valeri, 2021). The workforce within these types of structure 
encourages teamwork, employee autonomy, new ideas, creativity, entrepreneurship and 
innovation to compete efficiently in the business environment (Daft, et al., 2010).  
The impact of the organisational culture and types of organisational structures are significantly 
essential factors for SMEs' Top managers in decision-making to accept financial innovation, 
i.e. FinTech. This is because CEOs need timely information for their decision-making (Ada & 
Ghaffarzadeh, 2015; Wang, et al., 2016; Porter & Nohria, 2018; Alsmady, 2018; Liem & Hien, 
2020). If the structure is complex, getting information from lower management takes time to 
make timely decisions. All data and information go through different units and departments, 
while organisations with organic structures and CEOs get data and information directly from 
their employees. The decision-making by CEOs is less productive if the organisation is 
structured in such a way that it has many departmental levels (Carzo & Yanouzas, 1969). At 
each departmental level, there is a senior manager, followed by an executive team, to process 
the information for the top manager (Ou, et al., 2014; Child, 2015; Burke, 2022). In such 
structures, managers don’t have sufficient time to solve the problem and prefer to delegate 
their decision-making authority to their subordinates. The top managers of organisations with 
fewer levels of administrative departments have a wide span of supervision to control and 
process the information for effective decision-making that has an overall impact on the 
organisational performance (Chaston, 1997; Claver-Cortés, et al., 2012; Tran & Tian, 2013; 
Child, 2015; Ahmady, et al., 2016; Bai, et al., 2017; Dewar & Hage, 2018; Su, et al., 2019). A 
79 
 
similar study by Meltzer & Salter (1962) identified that more hierarchical levels in the 
organisational structure impact the CEO's productivity. In their research, Chehade & Vigneron 
(2007) found a positive relationship between banks with a decentralised structure and the 
SME's information transparency. This is because SMEs need funding from various sources, 
including FinTech platforms (Temelkov, et al., 2018; Dai, 2020; Sanga & Aziakpono, 2023). 
Meltzer & Salter (1962) further concluded that SMEs face more credit restraints due to their 
age, size and structure than banks with complex hierarchical organisational structures. The 
success of SMEs is essential to the economy (Prasanna et al., 2019; Gherghina et al., 2020; 
Epede & Wang, 2022), and depends on their organisational structure (Seetharaman, 2020; 
Rodrigues, et al., 2021). SMEs possess the adaptability to implement diverse organisational 
frameworks, which can profoundly impact the efficacy of their CEOs and, in turn, their 
organisations' overall achievements. According to Gherghina et al. (2020), organisational 
structure significantly affects SMEs’ performance. This emphasises the significance of 
matching the organisation's design to its strategic goals. In organisational structures, 
employees are assigned to perform specific tasks based on their specialised knowledge or 
roles. This helps CEOs to be more productive by enabling them to concentrate on their primary 
skills and expedite departmental decision-making (Gherghina, et al., 2020). A divisional or 
matrix organisation can support a multi-dimensional approach to business, where many 
product lines or geographical areas function semi-independently. Although this can foster 
creativity and adaptability, CEOs may need varied skills to navigate the associated 
complexities. 
Evidence from the literature suggests that there are several factors including people and 
forces around CEOs such as professional networking (Xiaowei & Zhang, 2010; Minai, et al., 
2012; Jaradat & Faqih, 2014; Eze, et al., 2018), board of directors (Dunne & Morris, 2008; 
Leardini, et al., 2013; Cummings, 2018) and the organisational structure (Carzo & Yanouzas, 
1969; Meltzer & Salter, 1962; Chaston, 1997; Chehade & Vigneron, 2007; Su, et al., 2019; 
Muthukannan, et al., 2020). These factors are meso - falls external to CEOs' characteristics 
80 
 
(micro) and have a direct link to CEOs as top-level executives in the organisational hierarchy 
and board members. Meso factors significantly contribute to CEOs' decision-making; however, 
no prior research confirms that these factors navigate CEOs in their FinTech financing 
decisions. Therefore, the third question is proposed as  
Research Question 3: How do CEOs of UK SMEs make their decisions on Fintech financing 
through professional networking, board of directors and organisational structure contexts at 
the meso level?  
 
2.3.4. 
Macro factors in FinTech financing decisions making 
Similarly, macro factors such as government policies and support pose that adopting new and 
risky practices in the organisation bound CEOs to some extent by law, politics or government 
initiatives to make effective decisions (Chen et al. 2014; Kiwanuka, 2015; Hu et al., 2019; 
Ahern, 2021; Ng and Pan, 2022). UK political system and policymakers fully support the 
FinTech revolution and understand its importance for economic growth. Therefore, the 
government stands its position to the UK FinTech as a global capital of FinTech. The UK 
government published its policy paper on the Fintech Sector Strategy in March 2008 to meet 
the needs of the FinTech sector by removing barriers from entry to the growth of FinTech 
firms, supporting international expansions and investments, stability of FinTech after Brexit 
and encouraging the FinTech benefits for everyone by promoting literacy, digital economy and 
financial innovation. Numerous government programmes, policies, and tax systems are 
operating to deal with digital currencies and peer-to-peer lending to FinTech (HM Treasury, 
2016). In December 2019, HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) published a policy paper to 
describe its position on cryptocurrency and provide guidance on paying income tax and 
national insurance contributions on cryptocurrency (HM Revenue & Customs, 2019).  
In the UK, the Financial Conduct Authority also started to establish a relationship with financial 
services sectors, including FinTech companies, to support the better use of technology, which 
ultimately benefits the public and ensures compliance with international financial standards. 
81 
 
Agility and an improved customer experience are critical success factors for FinTech 
(Capgemini, 2018). Due to government support, FinTech has raised nearly $110 billion since 
2009, as per Capgemini’s World FinTech Report 2018 (Capgemini, 2018), and it is growing 
with more success and initiatives worldwide. The global investments in FinTech in 2015 were 
$26.8 billion, which was higher than $19.9 billion in 2014, and hereafter it increased by 11.9% 
in 2016 (Fintech Global, 2018). The global investment in Fintech was $111.8 billion in 2018, 
120% higher than the total investment of $50.8 billion in 2017 (KPMG, 2019) and $23 billion 
in the first half of 2023 (S&P Global, 2023). Despite the heavy investment in Fintech, 
technology-enabled financial services have their limitations and challenges, too; for example, 
there are key challenges that IT can be compromised. The major challenges for Fintech to 
date are dealing with the issues of privacy, cybercrimes, and non-compliance with industry 
regulations (Naz et al., 2022; Kravchuk & Lutsyshyn, 2022). These threats are IT systems 
capabilities to manage the number of users worldwide and data security issues. To underpin 
security issues, cyberattacks have become very common these days and are a key threat to 
the sector. The IMF working paper highlighted that Cyber-attacks on Fintech firms have 
increased with an estimated $1450 million in losses due to fraud since 2013 (Bouveret, 2018). 
Apart from the government policy papers or guidance mentioned above, there are no specific 
UK FinTech regulations. However, there are few laws to prevent Fraud or abuse of financial 
transactions, such as the Financial Services and Market Act 2000, the Fraud Act 2006, the 
Theft Act 1968, and Money Laundering. FinTech companies are subject to these statutory 
frameworks to deal with financial crimes. Any company that wishes to undertake regulated 
provisions in the UK is subject to authorisation from the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). 
Therefore, the financing preferences of CEOs are significantly influenced by government 
support, the threat of IT and legislation, especially in the quickly changing FinTech sector. 
These regulations may serve as impediments to growth opportunities or as catalysts that make 
it easier to obtain funding. Examining government actions and the regulatory environment is 
essential to understanding their impact. In a FinTech-dominated era, governments throughout 
the globe are being forced to review their regulatory frameworks. Some have adopted a 
82 
 
progressive approach, creating an atmosphere of trust that allows SMEs to apply for loans 
through alternative lending sources and digital platforms (Cumming, et al., 2023). These 
governments have frequently accelerated financial inclusion efforts, supported FinTech firms, 
and expedited regulatory procedures, giving SMEs better access to various funding sources. 
Conversely, SMEs may find it easier to secure funding and carry out their expansion plans if 
there are onerous laws or an absence of helpful programs. Fintech innovations can be 
impeded by high regulatory barriers, compliance burdens, and a reluctance to accept them, 
which can apply to SMEs needing financing. 
Empirical research indicates that government support, user-friendly regulations, and policies 
are essential to SMEs' acceptance of Fintech financing. Several scholars have explored the 
impacts of government support in creating credibility for Fintech products, such as peer-to-
peer (P2P) lending, neobanks, crowdfunding, and others. Yee‐Loong Chong et al. (2010) 
found that government support is the biggest driver of Fintech adoption since the government 
significantly improve the reliability and credibility of the Fintech products, which makes them 
more acceptable to consumers. Government support positively influences the adoption of 
Fintech among customers (Kiwanuka, 2015). Governments must focus on developing and 
formulating appropriate policies to support SMEs' uptake of Fintech financing (Kiwanuka, 
2015). Regarding government support, Hu et al. (2019) have similar results closely related to 
Kiwanuka (2015) and Yee‐Loong Chong et al. (2010) that it increases the adoption of Fintech 
financing, especially P2P and crowdfunding. Support from the government is critical to building 
trust in SMEs to use Fintech (Marakarkandy et al., 2017), and it increases the adoption of 
Fintech (Hu et al., 2019). Marakarkandy et al. (2017) also undertook a study and emphasised 
that the government is critical for building the trust of SMEs in Fintech products. A similar result 
by Rosavina et al. (2019) and Chen et al. (2014) indicated that the government's role is 
significant in building trust between customers and lenders. The study found that trust is one 
factor that affects SMEs' use of P2P lending platforms (Rosavina, et al., 2019). According to 
Chen et al. (2014), governments, intermediaries and P2P lenders play an essential and 
83 
 
concerted role in ensuring the development of trust by providing high-quality information and 
adequate security information about the lenders to improve the uptake. Rosavina & Rahadi 
(2018) have also explored the issue of trust and the role of government regulation. They noted 
that P2P lending players enhance SMEs' access to financing, especially those with limited 
access to traditional funding. They found that the preference of SMEs for P2P financing is 
based on several factors - regulatory measures and the government can focus on easing the 
factors to ensure greater uptake of P2P lending by SMEs.  
In the study to identify the competitive strategies and their fitness to ensure the FinTech 
platforms' performance, Ng and Pan (2022) found that CEOs make Fintech decisions that 
conform to government regulations. According to Zarrouk et al. (2021), financial barriers, 
regulatory environment, and legal issues can negatively impact the initiation and growth of 
Fintech ventures. According to Ahern (2021), regulatory burden has cost and influences 
decisions. Entrepreneurs seeking to invest in Fintech innovations in Europe should 
understand the potential impacts of domestic laws and the European Union’s regulations for 
each member state (Ahern, 2021). Makki and Alqahtani (2022) conducted a study to evaluate 
critical factors that enable Fintech innovations. Zarrouk et al. (2021) have similar findings that 
regulation is among the favourable factors that support and promote Fintech. According to 
Goo and Heo (2020), excessive regulations often impact the innovative ability of the firm and 
the ability to compete in the market. They reported that regulations often hinder FinTech firms' 
business. Therefore, Fintech regulations are essential in guiding the adoption and running of 
Fintech innovations. The government's role in building trust in FinTech also requires creating 
awareness and perception of FinTech in SMEs. For example, the findings of the study by 
Ghazali & Yasuoka (2018) suggested that external environmental factors, such as the 
government's role, are critical in enhancing the awareness levels of SMEs for P2P and 
crowdfunding opportunities. They also indicated a positive relationship between financial 
literacy and the success of entrepreneurs for SME growth and business expansion. The study 
findings indicate that SMEs have low awareness of Fintech financing models such as P2P and 
84 
 
crowdfunding. The study suggests that there is an opportunity as well as a need to engage in 
the raising of the awareness of the SMEs with regards to the use and adoption of Fintech 
financing in the financing of their activities Similarly, the findings of the Owen et al. (2019) 
study confirm that the successful adoption of financing strategies in SMEs is emerging in the 
UK due to the combination of escalator funding, collaboration and bootstrapping that are all 
supported by the government's interventions.  
Empirical evidence (Chen, et al. 2014; Kiwanuka, 2015; Rosavina & Rahadi, 2018; Ghazali & 
Yasuoka, 2018; Owen, et al., 2019; Rosavina, et al. 2019; Goo and Heo, 2020; Ahern, 2021; 
Dwivedi et al., 2021; Zarrouk et al., 2021; Makki and Alqahtani, 2022; Ng and Pan, 2022) 
suggest that the macro factors such as the government regulations, policies and support are 
linked to the greater levels of adoption of Fintech financing. These factors fall external to 
CEOs' micro and meso levels to influence their decisions. However, the complexities of 
government support and laws as contributing factors to navigate CEOs in their Fintech 
financing decisions are still unexplored. Therefore, the research question 4 is proposed as  
Research Question 4: How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate the complexities of macro-level 
factors, including government support and laws, when making Fintech financing decisions? 
 
2.3.5. 
The Research Framework  
In the development of the research framework, it is important to understand that humans make 
judgments in daily life based on a combination of cognitive processes (Newen, 2017; 
Blanchette & Richards, 2010; Pisano & Sozzo, 2020) and environmental factors (Harding, 
1998; Nahavandi, 2019; De Groot, 2019). Decision-making is generally integral to human 
existence, affecting our actions and determining our future. Individuals make daily decisions, 
ranging from trivial decisions such as what to wear or consume to those with far-reaching 
personal and professional consequences. Understanding how we make decisions and 
influencing factors is essential for numerous disciplines, including psychology, management, 
85 
 
and organisational behaviour (Sahu et al., 2020). Factors such as personal leadership, vision, 
values, family, beliefs, education, experience and risk-taking attitude, professional networking, 
organisational structure, governance, government laws, policies, and further the use of 
decision-making models and tools greatly influence decision-making depending on the setting. 
According to Morelli et al. (2022), everyday decision-making is frequently influenced by 
heuristics, cognitive biases, and emotions. Heuristics are mental shortcuts or rules of thumb 
that people use to facilitate decision-making, resulting in accurate but occasionally erroneous 
decisions (Raue & Scholl, 2018; Shah, et al., 2018; Gigerenzer, et al., 2022; Pathak, et al., 
2023). Individuals may, for instance, rely on the availability heuristic, basing their decisions on 
readily accessible information instead of all pertinent factors. Additionally, cognitive fallacies 
play a role in daily decision-making (Juárez Ramos, 2018; Laureiro‐Martínez & Brusoni, 2018; 
Newell, et al., 2022). Confirmation bias, for instance, causes people to search for information 
confirming their beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence. This bias can influence decision-
making by limiting the contemplation of alternative options or points of view. Emotions also 
influence daily decision-making (Taffler, 2018; Sheppard & Levy, 2019; Sattar, et al., 2020; 
Goodell, et al., 2023). According to studies, emotions can influence decision-making by 
altering risk perceptions and preferences (Zhao, 2006; Foo, 2011; Tang, 2016; Diener, et al., 
2020). Positive emotions, for instance, may encourage individuals to take more risks, whereas 
negative emotions may induce risk aversion. Therefore, the decision-making of CEOs heavily 
relies on the cognitive theories of decision-making to make rational decisions. As the research 
is based on professional decisions in SMEs where the CEOs play a leading role to manage 
and lead the organisation, their professional decisions do not solely rely on their job 
descriptions. They consult with family, friends, and people in their personal and professional 
networks about what they do in their professional jobs and make decisions in the best interest 
of their organisations. Their professional decision-making typically entails more intricate 
processes and considerations. Expertise, education, and experience all play a role in making 
informed decisions. According to Gati and Kulcsar (2021), such professionals rely heavily on 
analytical decision-making models and instruments when making choices.  
86 
 
Decision-making models and tools are frequently used in SMEs to facilitate structured and 
systematic decision-making. These models, such as rational decision-making, risk 
management, and funds management, seek to reduce subjectivity and bias by providing a 
logical framework to assess alternatives, weigh risks, and choose the best course of action. 
Decision-making tools, such as decision matrices and cost-benefit analysis, assist CEOs in 
evaluating and comparing various funding alternatives to make informed judgments. The study 
aims to explore CEOs' decision making in Fintech Financing and how they perceive the 
influence of micro, meso, and macro factors in their Fintech financial decisions. For these 
reasons, the four main research questions are proposed as  
 
How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing choices in the context of a rapidly 
evolving economic and financial technology environment?  
 
How do CEOs of UK SMEs perceive the influence of micro-level factors, such as 
personal leadership traits, vision and risk-taking attitude on their Fintech financing 
decisions? 
 
How do CEOs of UK SMEs make their decisions on Fintech financing through 
professional networking, board of directors and organisational structure contexts at the 
meso level?  
 
How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate the complexities of macro-level factors, including 
government support and laws, when making Fintech financing decisions? 
The following research framework is drawn based on the empirical evidence of CEOs' 
factors as an input to their financing decision.   
87

3. Data and Research Methodology 
 
3.1. Introduction 
Research is the art of seeking new facts through a process or an academic activity (Kothari, 
2007) to generate new knowledge (Gerrish & Lathlean, 2015). Planning the research design 
concerning any feasible route, such as design by questions, methods of analysis or data 
collection, is an important part of the success of any research (Braun & Clarke, 2013). The 
research design includes a plan, process, or way of knowing by which we develop common 
sense to understand the problem (Leavy, 2017). This involves identifying philosophical 
paradigms (Khatri, 2020), and using suitable and valid research techniques to collect and 
analyse data (Rahi, 2017; Allemang et al., 2022). The process provides a useful model to 
guide in planning and executing the research to produce valid results. Each stage in the 
research process is linked to the other stage and has a specific role in formulating the research 
methodology. The methodology is a term to cover all steps involved in the research (Van 
Meter, 1994). The researcher illustrates the whole process with a unique approach to ensure 
that the effectiveness of the study remains consistent throughout the research methodology. 
The concept of the research approach is based on the criteria for the research and the 
participants to ensure the research will remain within the parameters to answer the research 
objectives. The criteria for the research include the researcher's position within the research 
philosophies, the participants’ criteria, and the choice of the research methodology as a 
structured approach to collecting, analysing, and interpreting data within the research context 
and ethical constraints to complete the thesis. It includes the criteria for the participants ensure 
that the research produces purposeful results, identifies limitations and gaps to produce 
generalised results, and allows future development of the existing research. The remaining 
stages of the research are analysis through data collection, findings as results and discussion 
to reach a conclusion. All this information that emerges from the collection of data to analysis 
89 
 
and results are based on the participants’ criteria. If anything changes in the participant criteria, 
the results will emerge differently. These boundaries as criteria are set to guide the whole 
research process as illustrated below: -  
 
Figure 8 The Research Process (Author’s own Illustration) 
 
The purpose of the research is to explore CEOs’ decision-making in Fintech Financing. The 
research process is illustrated in the shape of the research tree; in general, the tree grows 
from the seed to produce its intended result; the purpose of the research within the research 
process is to make valid and reliable results. 
 
3.1.1. 
Establishing the research design  
The first point of the research tree is to establish a criteria for the research design which is 
based on identifying the researcher’s position in the research philosophies and rationales for 
90 
 
choosing of the appropriate research methodology as an approach, fit for purpose, to conduct 
the whole research.  
 
3.1.1.1. 
Research Philosophies  
The research process is based on underlying research philosophies to understand research 
assumptions about the nature of reality and the researcher’s position, approach and stance 
towards that reality. Research philosophies are beliefs that guide the planning, design and 
execution of the research (Tamminen & Poucher, 2020; Porot & Mandelbaum, 2021; HR & 
Aithal, 2022). These philosophies help the researcher identify his position during the research. 
Not all philosophical paradigms apply to every type of research; they have different goals and 
natures to define the problem and guide the researcher in developing the research process. 
In each research philosophy, there are several philosophical assumptions, such as the 
researcher’s stance towards the nature of reality (ontology), sources and the construction of 
the knowledge (epistemology), the role of values and beliefs in the research (axiology), the 
research language (rhetoric), and choice of the suitable methods (Creswell, 2007; Creswell & 
Poth, 2016). In the following section, the researcher critically reflects on research philosophies 
with rationales to choose the most suitable research philosophies for this research.    
The research philosophy explores the CEOs’ decision-making in Fintech Financing and how 
they perceive the influence of micro, meso and macro level factors to navigate their financing 
choices. CEOs are human beings, and they have their thoughts, experiences, educational 
backgrounds, family, networks, and friends within their social context. They have leadership 
qualities and decision-making power and work for their SMEs under contract or employment. 
Decision-making is a mental state of human beings considering all those factors in and around 
CEOs. This also includes the reality of their existence in society to reflect in their decisions 
due to social environment (Bruch & Feinberg, 2017) or social structure (Archer, 1995; Archer, 
1982; Gorski, 2013). Human identities and decision-making differ and vary from one situation 
to another; therefore, exploring the reality of how they think and work may bring multiple views 
about their perceptions of Fintech financing. Thus, the constructivism paradigm is more 
91 
 
suitable for exploring the reality of the phenomenon of CEOs' financing-related decision-
making. In constructivism, the assumption is that realities are multiple (Pale & Lilford, 2011) 
because individuals construct the meanings of the realities they experience through their 
cognitive process (Young & Collin, 2004). Decision-making is one of the basic cognitive 
processes of human behaviour (Newell & Bröder, 2008) as per the cognitive theory (Wu, 2020; 
Andrade, 2020; Featherman et al., 2021), where CEOs deal with various situations in their 
professional practice to choose the best financing option. Constructivism is a more focused 
individualistic approach (Young & Collin, 2004), which is more aligned with this research 
because CEOs are vital decision-makers and construct knowledge through their subjective 
meanings of the phenomenon (Putnam & Banghart, 2017; Bleiker et al., 2019; Ugwu et al., 
2021). The reason for choosing the constructivism paradigm is that the researcher does not 
know what is happening in the CEO's mind when making decisions. It helps to identify how 
CEOs work in the social context (Schneider, 1985) and construct their knowledge through their 
individual minds (Crotty, 1998) to address problems when dealing with various troubles or 
situations in complex organisational environments. For example, it may be possible that SMEs 
try to finance their business monetary needs through bank loans but are rejected, and they 
decide to use FinTech. This situation or the context of their decision-making may differ for 
other SME CEOs, who may be inclined more towards de-regulations or dislike heavy 
paperwork to comply with banking regulations. Therefore, the researcher’s use of the 
constructivism paradigm helps identify participants' views on their financing decisions. This 
approach is the basic stance in the dynamic environment where multiple realities exist, and 
the researcher has his views and articulates CEOs' context to answer the research objectives.  
In the constructivism paradigm, the researcher’s stance involves fixing his ontological and 
epistemological positions to the context, which will ultimately determine the contents of the 
research to address research objectives. Ontology is a study of reality whose existence is 
confirmed by explicit theories, views, opinions or thoughts (Porta, 2008; Baskerville et al., 
2020; Krzanowski & Polak, 2022). It concerns the nature of reality and our understanding of 
92 
 
its existence. Therefore, the researcher needs to work within its ontological parameters. The 
ontological position enables the researcher to capture clear, understandable, secure and 
convincing accounts of context and experiences of the situations where understanding occurs 
(Hamilton & Pinnegar, 2009). This study deals with the ontology that is dynamic and 
changeable by human beings. CEOs who are participants in this study have different points 
of view, interpretations, experiences, opinions, and thoughts while making decisions using 
FinTech. It is because CEOs, as human beings, have their own identity in society and in the 
organisations in which they work. They have their perceptions about reality and the 
circumstances in which they make decisions. The factors of education, experience, social or 
professional networking, organisational structure and culture, including its rules and 
regulations, and the country's laws shape their decision-making. Therefore, the ontological 
stance of this study is relativism due to the subjectivity (Kusch, 2020; Krol, 2021; Merlo & 
Pravato, 2021) that CEOs have their views and perceptions in their social structure to make 
decisions. The point here is that several factors influence human decisions, including their 
personal experiences and identities within organisational culture and society. Therefore, their 
subjective stance due to their unique leadership qualities, experience, knowledge, values, 
beliefs, networking, people around them, friends or family or other influential factors such as 
government policies and the organisation, its culture and governance shape CEOs' decisions 
for their financing choice. This brings multiple realities to the phenomenon of understanding 
the CEOs’ decision-making.   
The other philosophical paradigm such as positivism is not a suitable paradigm for this study 
because it tests the hypothesis where the researcher observes the situation and predicts the 
research (Dubé & Paré, 2003; Piekkari & Welch, 2018; Park et al., 2020). In this approach, 
the knowledge is tested by scientific methods (Braun & Clarke, 2013), and the researcher's 
role is independent but limited to the observation during the collection, testing and 
interpretation of data (Hassard, 1995). In other words, it is all about the discovery of 
knowledge. Similarly, the post-positivist approach is mainly suitable to explore the reality of 
93 
 
CEOs' Fintech-related decision-making phenomenon by admitting that empirical observations 
alone could not capture the full reality (Salzmann-Erikson, 2024), which is accompanied by 
many layers and complexities. It mainly seeks details of shortcomings left in strict positivism 
(Clark, 1998; Groff, 2004; Petroski, 2011). Meanwhile, pragmatism emphasises that realities 
are external and have multiple meanings due to the practical implications of actions in a 
particular context (Creswell, 2007; Morgan, 2014; Ivanova et al., 2019). In this kind of 
philosophical approach, many approaches, mixed or multiple methods, are used to collect and 
analyse data, particularly those research in which events happened in the past, in social or 
political contexts (Creswell, 2007). The philosophical approach to realism relates to the 
scientific assumptions of discovering the truth that already exists anywhere around the world 
(Braun & Clarke, 2013; Gronda & Gronda, 2020) but is independent of human understandings 
and beliefs (Callaghan & Mitchell, 2023; Willis, 2023). Idealism suggests that reality is based 
on ideas in the mind (Foster, 2021).  
When it comes to the criteria for the validity of the knowledge, the researcher needs to define 
its epistemological position. Epistemology is a study of criteria or philosophy to constitute valid 
knowledge (Johnson & Duberley, 2000; Bonaccorsi, 2022; Younus & Zaidan, 2022). 
Epistemology deals with the ways of knowing and the researcher’s belief system about the 
knowledge's origin, interpretations, meanings and sources (Klenke, et al., 2016). In the 
epistemological position of the researcher, the most common question is to identify the 
relationship between the researcher and the topic of the study to make it clear that the 
subjective stance of the researcher may influence the research. However, in this case, the 
researcher is not working in the FinTech industry but is an ordinary researcher interested in 
understanding SME financing using FinTech. The epistemological stance is social 
constructionism, meaning knowledge is constructed through social practices (Young & Collin, 
2004; Cohen et al., 2004; Stead, 2004) or social interactions (Mir & Watson, 2000). In social 
constructionism, social and cultural practices such as interaction or conversations with people 
help individuals to construct their knowledge (Richardson et al. 2009). In this study, the CEOs 
94 
 
interact with friends and family at the micro level and gain education or experience within their 
social environment. At the meso level, they work in their respective organisations, which 
includes people to make their organisational culture and further establish their professional 
networking in and outside their organisations. At the macro level, they socially interact within 
their country's social environment, where they learn and gain knowledge to make their own 
decisions within the country's political system, laws and regulations. That leads towards the 
relationship between self at the micro level to socially construct meanings of their decisions 
coupled with influencing factors at the meso and macro levels. This philosophical stance of 
social constructivism enables the researcher to delve deep into how CEOs make Fintech 
decisions. This is particularly significant as it considers their engagement with people as a 
form of social practice, thereby providing a comprehensive understanding of their decision-
making processes. As a researcher, it is imperative to maintain an ethical and collaborative 
approach with the participants. This ensures a respectful and considerate understanding of 
their perceptions of similar research questions. Social constructivism, in this context, enables 
the researcher to identify how CEOs construct their knowledge of a particular phenomenon 
through social interaction and experience, which will uncover many truths (Stead, 2004), 
particularly due to the participants’ subjective perceptions of their financing decisions.  
In summary, the constructivism paradigm helps the researcher to know participants’ subjective 
perceptions and decision-making of their financing preferences. The ontological and the 
epistemological stances influence the work due to the researcher's subjectivity, beliefs, values 
and approaches to methodology. The researcher's ontological approach towards the research 
philosophical assumptions is relativism with a social constructivism epistemological stance to 
explore the research phenomenon. 
 
3.1.1.2. 
Methodology  
Research methodology is an approach that provides the researcher with a way (Sileyew, 
2019) to establish a plan, procedure, or direction (Creswell, 2014) to conduct the research. 
95 
 
Research methodologies include methods and techniques (Dubey & Kothari, 2022) to collect 
data consistent with the philosophical assumptions to accomplish research objectives. These 
methods focus on different aspects of reality to examine the rich understanding of the 
phenomena through gathering and collection of the data (Miller & Tsang, 2010). Methods are 
rules, techniques or tools to collect evidence (Hesse-Biber, 2016). Research methods are 
classified into qualitative or quantitative and linked to the inductive and deductive reasoning 
approach, depending on the research design, research questions, and prior knowledge and 
theory in the subject area.  
It is essential for a researcher to identify the most suitable research methodology per the 
research tree process to establish the criteria for the research, including following the 
appropriate research methodology. In the current research, what influences or affects CEOs' 
decision-making is unknown, and there are limited academic theories and evidence on 
FinTech financing. Therefore, the qualitative research method is more suitable to examine the 
research questions. In qualitative research, the researcher's approach to writing is inductive. 
The inductive approach of reasoning in the qualitative research method is suitable for getting 
detailed insights if there is no or little existing theory or literature about the problem (Pope, et 
al., 2000; Domegan & Fleming, 2007; Hayes, 2005; Ketokivi & Mantere, 2010; Bendassolli, 
2013; LUKENCHUK, 2017; Kyngäs, 2020; Zeithaml et al., 2020). It includes subjective 
reasoning and focusing on underlying assumptions that reality is constructed through the 
social phenomenon. The inductive approach is also called bottom-up (Hayes, 2005), 
explanatory and reverse to the deductive approach (Tuomela, 1981; Zalaghi & Khazaei, 2016; 
Okoli, 2023). The deductive reasoning approach is unsuitable for qualitative research but is 
more appropriate for quantitative research where the process starts with the research 
theoretical framework, the creation of hypotheses, and then data collection, developing 
themes (Tracy, 2020) and testing the theory (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).  
There are many research methods to address the research questions, such as an experiment, 
case study, survey, action research, grounded theory, ethnography, phenomenological and 
96 
 
narrative research inquiry which provides a framework for design, structure, philosophical 
assumptions and approach to meet the research objectives (Morse, 2016; Muurlink, 2018; 
Creswell & Creswell, 2018; Ng, et al., 2018; Tracy, 2020; Yin, 2013; Maulina, et al., 2022; 
Holloway & Galvin, 2023). In the current research, while using qualitative methods, the 
researcher’s philosophical assumptions are primarily based on constructivism to understand 
the subjective meanings of CEOs’ decision-making relating to their Fintech financing decision 
making. In the process, the researcher gathers data through interviews, including participants' 
views and opinions, to establish the meaning of the research phenomenon (Creswell & 
Creswell, 2018). Considering inductive reasoning and its consistency with philosophical 
paradigms, the researcher establishes a grounded approach as a suitable method within 
qualitative research to collect and analyse the data. This is because the current research deals 
with CEOs who hold professional positions at the highest level of their organisational 
hierarchies to make critical decisions. Considering their senior executive positions, it is argued 
that their decisions are primarily strategic with a long-term impact on their entire organisation 
(Recendes, et al., 2022) that includes power and politics as dominant paradigms to influence 
their decisions (Eisenhardt & Zbaracki, 1992). For example, the senior executives and non-
executives on the organisational board can have conflicting objectives to reach a consensus 
on their decisions. That is because strategic decisions' power and politics paradigm views 
organisations as political systems where decision-makers have conflicting objectives 
(Eisenhardt & Zbaracki, 1992). Power dynamics are crucial, and political manoeuvring 
influences individuals' decisions (Eisenhardt & Zbaracki, 1992). Similarly, CEOs’ distinctive 
leadership characteristics distinguish them from other senior offices in organisations and help 
them compete with their competitors by responding to changes in their dynamic business 
environment. They also consider their country's political system and laws in their financing 
decisions. The context of financing preferences requires detailed insights to get the true 
meanings and the actual cause of CEOs' decision-making by investigating their subjective 
stances within the complex organisational systems and dynamics of their business 
environment. Therefore, the ground approach is more suitable for this research. The grounded 
97 
 
approach in this qualitative research helps the researcher establish the unique practice used 
in grounded theory research; however, it does not focus on the grounded theory itself (Gligor, 
et al., 2016). The grounded approach allows flexibility to use an iterative process of data 
collection and analysis (Gligor, et al., 2016) as per the nature of the research to engage 
actively with participants of how they create meaning within their context (Charmaz, 2000; 
Charmaz, 2006; Charmaz, 2017). The data collection and analysis process in the grounded 
approach allows participants to freely express their opinions, experiences and views through 
interviews, which are analysed by the researcher through coding, leading to the development 
of themes and patterns (Gligor, et al., 2016). The main emphasis of this approach is that the 
data is grounded in the participants (Creswell & Creswell, 2018; Cullen & Brennan, 2021) to 
gain an in-depth understanding of a particular phenomenon (Beck, 2021). Getting views of 
CEOs through similar research questions in the grounded approach enables the researcher 
to identify multiple realities (Stead, 2004) within the social constructionism paradigm (Young 
& Collin, 2004; Cohen et al., 2004; Stead, 2004). Such studies are exploratory in the qualitative 
research context (Ponelis, 2015), which leads to revealing or unpacking the experiences of 
CEOs about their financing preferences and the results into more generalised conclusions to 
meet the research purpose (Yin, 2014). 
The rationale for not considering other methods is that if the researcher considers the 
experiment research, that requires consideration of hypotheses - the causes and effects of 
dependent and independent variables to test theories (Srinagesh, 2011). Experiments are 
usually conducted in the lab or natural environment and have different design features, 
feasibilities, and complexity regarding the cost and time of academic research. Experiments 
are further classified into true and quasi-experiments and follow deductive reasoning. Survey 
methods are primarily quantitative (Nardi, 2018), but they can be qualitative (Braun, et al., 
2021) - exploratory and descriptive, and the data is collected through responses from the 
selected participants (Check & Schutt, 2012). Survey results may limit the participants’ views 
with close-ended questions (Story & Tait, 2019) which does not fit the project. The current 
98 
 
research does not include the researcher and practitioner working together in a field to solve 
a problem, as the action research strategy suggests. It is also called a field experiment 
(Krishnaswamy, et al., 2009) to learn truths relating to the research objectives. This research 
also does not include the researcher's experience as a philosophical approach criterion 
mentioned earlier. Then, it focuses on the stories of an individual’s experience (Given, 2008) 
as per the framework of the Narrative research methods. Similarly, case study methods are 
considered powerful qualitative research tools (Gerring, 2016) but do not fit because it is 
mostly focused on single or multiple cases (Hancock, et al., 2021) or events (Mfinanga, et al., 
2019) and these methods are not aligned with the current research philosophical paradigm. 
Similarly, the phenomenology research strategy aligns more with humans' lived experiences 
(Salazar et al., 2015) of a particular situation. This is more suitable if the research requires 
identifying and learning participants' live experiences in an actual situation (Van Manen, 2017; 
Williams, 2021). Further, the current research does not fall into ethnographic research 
because the researcher has to become a part of a group or a culture to whom they practically 
observe in the social context through observations (Lichtman, 2011). Therefore, the grounded 
approach (Gligor, et al., 2016) to understand the subjective meanings (Charmaz, 2000; 
Charmaz, K., 2006; Charmaz, 2017) of CEOs’ decision-making is more aligned with this study. 
This approach fits well with the philosophical paradigm of social constructionism (Young & 
Collin, 2004; Cohen et al., 2004; Stead, 2004). In the grounded approach, the researcher 
adopted an open approach (Bryant, 2017) in its questions to allow participants to provide 
detailed insights into their decision-making relating to Fintech financing. The point herein is 
that CEOs have various situations in which they make financing decisions. Due to the varied 
nature of humans and influential factors at the micro, meso and macro, the results may bring 
multiple realities (Stead, 2004) to explore CEOs’ decision making in Fintech Financing. 
 
99 
 
3.1.1.3. 
Interviews as Data Collection Method 
Data collection depends on the research methodological approach. There are two sources of 
data: primary and secondary. The primary data is collected directly from people by using 
questionnaires, surveys, and interviews, while the secondary sources are existing 
publications, reports, books and libraries (Ajayi, 2017; Largan & Morris, 2019; Sileyew, 2019; 
Watkins, 2022). As semi-structured interviews encourage two-way communication and open-
ended discussions (Lavrakas, 2008), it allows the interviewer to talk about sensitive issues 
and questions in detail (Rabionet, 2011). The researcher collected the data from CEOs by 
using semi-structured interviews as a primary source of information, which facilitates the 
researcher to collect qualitative data that can be compared to future and previous data to 
produce an accurate research outcome (Draper & Swift, 2011).  
During the interviewing process, participants were asked open-ended questions after 
consenting to participate in the study. The research questions are related to their financing 
practices and how they make FinTech financing decisions at the micro, meso, and macro 
levels. They were asked to answer the questions based on their views, opinions, and 
experiences and were also asked to highlight any concerns they may have had regarding the 
financing method. Data from interviews support the researcher in providing detailed beliefs 
and opinions of the CEOs' factors in their decision-making. The participants are given 
sufficient time to explore questions and express their opinions and views. The first interview 
was conducted in February 2021, with a follow-up to three participants’ interviews for the pilot 
study by 27th April 2021. During that time, the researcher participated in research training, 
aligned interview questions and analysed the pilot data. Due to COVID, The remaining 
interviews were conducted between 20th October 2022 and 3rd August 2023. The data from 
CEOs was gathered only once to complete the research in six years. It is essential to mention 
that the fitness for interview questions and ethical issues are important aspects of conducting 
good interviews. Ethical issues in interviewing include the safety of participants in a protected 
100 
 
environment, data protection requirements, a guarantee of participants’ anonymity and non-
traceability, and privacy issues.   
 
3.1.2. 
Establishing the participant criteria  
In this research, the availability of the potential participants and the study’s selection criteria 
are considered during the research design phase. The criterion sampling is used in this 
research which perfectly fits with the qualitative methods of the research (Barusch, et al., 
2011). According to Palinkas et al. (2015), the selection of the participants for the interview is 
based on the predetermined criteria that help the researcher identify the potential participants 
and their characteristics. The views, assumptions, and opinions of selected participants are 
according to the sampling criteria to generate more generalised outcomes (Patton, 2015). The 
criterion sampling includes only working CEOs of UK SMEs in the non-financial sector who 
use FinTech or have experience or knowledge of FinTech. Other criteria include that the 
SMEs' age should be older than a year and the company size should not be less than 2 people. 
The rationale for selecting SMEs aged more than 1 year is that new companies always face 
credit problems due to their age, and they may shift their financing preferences towards 
FinTech. Two-person criteria will restrict sole traders and encourage participants’ involvement 
that may have the board for collective decisions from limited companies. There are no 
restrictions on the CEO's age, experience or education, as these are the micro-level factors 
that this research establishes to achieve its research objectives.  
In qualitative research, a sufficient sample size matters a lot to reach a reliable and valid 
research outcome (Burmeister & Aitken, 2012; Marshall et al., 2013; Cleary et al., 2014; 
Malterud et al., 2016). In grounded research, the ideal size is a data saturation for value-added 
insights to conclude (Glaser & Strauss, 1967; Thomson, 2010, COI, 2016; Sebele-Mpofu, 
2020). Therefore, the researcher's stance is to continue gathering data to reach data 
saturation for valuable findings and conclusions. The current research reaches the sufficiency 
of data at the 20th participant. Even though the researcher does not have gender criteria in 
101 
 
this study. However, six females and fourteen males participated in the research as a fair 
representation of the sample to provide diverse views, opinions and perceptions of their 
financing decisions. The research design facilitates the researcher's analysis and examination 
of the actions and opinions of the individuals given in response to the interview questions to 
generate patterns, themes, and accurate outcomes. 
 
3.1.3. 
Data Collection: Participant Recruitment  
Most of the SMEs incorporated in the UK have their profiles available on the internet, such as 
incorporation information in the Companies House and their CEOs' profiles on LinkedIn, 
membership, or federation platforms. CEOs’ profiles are not readily available and accessible 
by the researcher through the Internet. At least 250 profiles on LinkedIn, companies and other 
professional membership websites were searched, and the invitation was sent. The invitation 
includes the participant recruitment criteria so CEOs can self-assess themselves if they meet 
the criteria to participate in the study. 60 CEOs responded, and further information was sent 
to ensure they met the research criteria. The reason for sending more invitations was to 
choose the eligible participants in the study. Many of the 250 contacted participants did not 
respond. At the same time, a few refused to join the research as they were either busy or had 
no interest in the study. The researcher also felt that the invitation letter did not get a response 
due to the current COVID-19 pandemic, where most CEOs have started to work from home, 
and gatekeepers do not provide access to their CEOs. The eligible participants were selected. 
The research also obtained consent from each participant, in which the purpose of the study 
and the importance of their participation were explained clearly.  
The researcher emailed an interview guide to the participants (Appendix 11). The interview 
guide included the necessary project and ethics details and an outline of the research the 
researcher would like to cover during the interview. The participants were advised to go 
through the guide to understand the purpose of the study and how their participation is helpful 
and meaningful for the researcher in generating generalised outcomes. Participants needed 
102 
 
to review the guide and confirm their participation before the interview. A few participants 
reviewed and agreed to the details of the guide and the participation in interviews in their 
working schedules.  
3.1.4. 
Participant demographic information 
Before the interview, the participants were given an interview guide. The participant 
information sheet (Appendix 10) records useful information about participants, including their 
SMEs, to help the researcher shortlist against the participant recruitment criteria and gain 
sufficient information for the demographic data analysis. It is to be noted that the data in the 
participant information sheet is processed in line with the ethical and legislative data protection 
requirements, where it was mentioned to CEOs that no data will be used to identify the person 
or the organisation and that confidentiality is maintained. Therefore, the information that the 
researcher gets through the participant information sheet is not sent to the third party to 
validate, for example, the identity of CEOs or their organisation, turnover and number of staff, 
CEOs’ age, qualifications or experience in SMEs and Fintech which is against the data 
protection clauses to keep the information confidential. Further data collection through 
participant information does not constitute any agreement between the researcher and the 
participant to validate the information either by the researcher or the third party on behalf of 
the researcher. Therefore, the demographic data only represents the information gained from 
CEOs based on their true faith and knowledge, which is considered correct and helps to justify 
their fair participation in the research. Microsoft Excel sheet records the participant 
demographic information to statistically represent them in tables as percentages. This 
information includes gender, age group, education level, experience as CEO and experience 
within the industry, knowledge and understanding of using Fintech, further sector, turnover, 
and number of directors in the organisation. The following table provides the representation of 
the demographic data.

3.1.5. 
Virtual Conduct of Interviews   
The researcher conducted the interviews virtually because of two potential reasons. One is 
that it was necessary to take the precautions and maintain the social distancing due to the 
current pandemic of Coronavirus (World Health Organization, 2020). Secondly, the CEOs of 
the SMEs in the UK were distributed across the country and it was not feasible for every 
participant to physically join the interview session due to the government guidance in addition 
to precautionary measurement taken the researcher. Therefore, the researcher opted for 
Zoom, which is a video communication application, providing chat and video telephony 
services to the consumers. The multiple formats of communication are supported in Zoom 
which include video chatting, instant messaging, texting and meeting and participant can 
securely join Zoom with user ID and password (Gal, 2020). Recording of interviews in the 
qualitative research is preferred but not essential (King & Horrocks, 2010). However, Zoom 
also has features of recording meeting (Zoom, 2020). This recording feature in Zoom facilitates 
the researcher to record the answers of each participant which can be formulated, analysed 
and compared to the opinions of other participants to generate the outcomes of the research.  
Zoom also captures the participants’ gestures and body language during the interviews, which 
is an extra feature over audio/telephone interviews; the video determines the accuracy of the 
information being gathered from the participants. According to Iserson, et al. (2019) the video 
recording without consent from both parties is unethical and illegal. Therefore, before 
suggesting joining the Zoom meeting, the researcher took the consent from each participant 
to confirm the recording of the video session while highlighting its importance to the research 
outcomes. It was also ensured that participants had an uninterrupted internet facility so that 
they could join the meeting on time.  Zoom has provided efficient, convenient and cost-
effective methods to conduct the virtual meetings, which are feasible for everyone and 
recording facility made it more convenient for the researcher to collect the data easily 
(Archibald, et al., 2019).  There are many other video conferencing tools to conduct interviews, 
one of such applications is Skype with improved features of video meeting, but for this 
106 
 
research, the Zoom was selected because it provides a great reliability and the quality of 
streaming video. Therefore, using Zoom and taking consent from each participant is suitable 
for collecting the online data in this research.  
3.1.6. 
Pilot Study  
Pilot study is very important for any kind of research either quantitative or qualitative. It helps 
the researcher to identify problems at the early stage of the research such as revision and 
modification of research questions before the broader plan is implemented and support the 
researcher to get some skills (Gorman & Clayton, 2003). The pilot study was beneficial for this 
research. It aimed to gain insights, understanding, and descriptive and exploratory responses 
from CEOs regarding their decisions relating to FinTech financing. 
Through obtaining information directly from the CEOs, the acquired answers were based on 
numerous years of experience in their respective fields and first-hand observations made by 
them on the financing. The answers led to a detailed understanding of the SME financing 
through FinTech using different methods. The answers and subsequent analysis were 
imperative for the study. Other reasons to conduct pilot study was that qualitative methods to 
be time-consuming and highly specialise job for the researcher to arrange participants, 
conduct interviews and then use Nvivo software to analyse their interview transcripts. Pilot 
study helped the researcher to ensure the fitness of interview questions, research 
philosophical assumptions and the preparation of the main study. The researcher also uses 
the pilot study to identify any gaps and limitations in the design and enable the researcher to 
improve. The following steps were used to conduct the pilot study
