In the pilot study, the researcher determined four main interview questions to cover topics of 
the study. In order to encourage participants to explore their views, the questions design 
features include all elements of purposeful and open-ended questions. The semi structure 
interview guide was developed as an interview framework, based on phases suggested by 
(Kallio, et al., 2016) to increase the questions effectiveness and participant involvement and 
motivation to the research.  In the pilot study, three participants were selected, only those who 
meet the participant recruitment criteria. Zoom was used to conduct interviews during Feb-
April 2021. The researcher provided details about the aims, objectives of the research and the 
scope of the interview and seek informed consent from participants.  
In the phase of piloting the interview, the researcher ensured that confidentiality of information 
is maintained that includes ensuring that the personal data is protected while using Zoom as 
per Data Protection Act. This was also confirmed to the participant before the start of the 
interview. The ideal interviewing time varies, depends on the research questions and the 
participant interest (Gillham, 2007). However, the researcher ensured that the interviewing 
timing last sufficient to get detailed insights, keeping in view the important time of CEOs and 
their other commitments. During the interview, the audio of the online meetings was 
transcribed and notations were made in a transcript with the visual cues which were recorded 
to evaluate their meanings next to the spoken words and to further understand the recordings 
(Keeney, et al., 2011). The data was analysed by using Nvivo software after the transcription 
was reviewed by the researcher. The responses gathered during interviews were not termed 
as right or wrong answers because opinions were objective and based on personal 
experience, yet the answers were used as guidance for the researcher to make conclusions 
and organise them according to the identified themes (Geist, 2010). 
The lessons learnt from the pilot study helped the researcher to plan better to organise timely 
interviews, re-ordering the interview questions, test Zoom meetings and the researchers’ skills 
requirement to conduct the better interview and the proficiency level to use Nvivo. In the major 
study, open-ended questions are used with an improved interview guide. The time allocation 
108 
 
would be 50 minutes. There would not be any change in the participant recruitment criteria 
and Zoom meeting software to capture meetings.     
 
3.1.7. 
Reflexivity and the researcher role during the interview 
Since the research is based on the collection of the primary data through semi structured 
interviews to achieve the research objectives. The researcher felt the complexity at different 
stages in exploring how various factors navigate CEOs in their financing choices at the micro, 
meso, and macro levels. For example, understanding the subjective stance of CEOs in their 
organisational context, where they faced various challenges in making financing decisions, 
required a deep understanding of their organisational environmental factors and, more 
importantly, the governance issues. The researcher reviewed different literature on 
management and leadership, power, and politics in governance and legal documents relating 
to SME incorporation and senior officers' roles on the board to understand the context of 
CEOs’ decision-making. The researcher maintained the learning log throughout the project to 
reflect upon the positive experience and impact on the research outcome. The purpose of 
maintaining the log of learning was to gain a deep awareness of the study's literature to make 
meaningful findings in the project. According to Waterhouse et al. (2020), there is always a 
probability of obtaining biased responses from the participants during interviews, which has 
the potential to make the outcomes redundant if the responses are not honest. To eliminate 
the biased responses from the participants, the researcher’s role was to maintain ethical 
discussion in a friendly and comfortable environment throughout the interview. During the 
interview process, there was the possibility that the participant could belong to different 
ethnicities, religions, or colours. Hence, the researcher ensured that his questions promoted 
equality by avoiding discriminatory terms or unethical language. Ethical reflexivity promotes 
good relationships between parties involved in the research (Clarke, et al., 2016) and 
maintains moral accountability (Cunliffe & Ivaldi, 2021). The discussion was made relevant 
and ethical as much as possible so that hatred and biased responses from the participants 
109 
 
could be eliminated. Further, various strategies were used to avoid personal discussions with 
participants, which could compromise the confidentiality of participants, and their associated 
parties' personal information. In the constructionism paradigm, in-depth interviews were 
essential to allow participants with assumptions to explore their subjective stances of what 
they feel, think and experience (Marvasti, 2019) in their social world (Archer, 2007). Therefore, 
the researcher appreciated the participants' opinions by allowing maximum time. These 
strategies also included adjusting the research questions without changing the context of the 
actual research questions so the participants could understand and respond accordingly.   
 
 
3.2. Analysis 
The purpose of this study was to explore how micro, meso, and macro factors influence the 
way CEOs make Fintech financial decisions. The researcher focused on four research 
questions to guide this study. Interviews with CEOs were conducted to collect data for the 
study. After recording the interviews, the researcher transcribed each interview of the 
participants into a Microsoft Word document. The researcher used alphanumeric codes to 
name the transcripts based on the sequence of interviews. For example, the first transcript for 
the first interview participant was named as “P1”. After transcribing all interviews, the 
researcher gave each participant a copy of the transcript to confirm the accuracy of the 
collected data. All the transcripts were accurate, as there were no participants who indicated 
changes to the transcripts. After confirmation of the transcripts, the researcher read each 
transcript several times to develop his understanding of each response and the research 
question. This is called the conceptualisation as per the analysis step of the research tree. 
Conceptualisation is a process of understanding the texture meanings (Ravitch & Carl, 2019; 
Palacios, et al., 2021) of the interview transcript to familiarise oneself with the research data.  
The researcher began data analysis by importing all the transcripts into Nvivo software. Nvivo 
was used to record interview transcripts. Nvivo was developed by QSR International, and it is 
110 
 
one of the most popular software for business, psychology, sociology, and other disciplines, 
especially for qualitative research. Nvivo helps the researcher store, organise, and analyse 
data using in-built programming through codes and the development of themes. Nvivo's main 
aim is to facilitate the researcher to organise and analysis of the data. Once a project is created 
in Nvivo, it can create, explore, share, and import documents. 
The researcher followed the six steps outlined in the literature for conducting thematic analysis  
(Byrne, 2022; Campbell et al., 2021; Xu & Zammit, 2020). The first step of thematic analysis 
is data familiarisation (Byrne, 2022). The researcher utilised the first step to gain a 
comprehensive understanding of the participants’ responses by reading and re-reading the 
transcripts. It is important for the researcher to understand the true meaning of CEOs’ 
responses in the interview data to reflect the correct understanding of the phenomenon. The 
researcher related the participants' responses to the research questions and highlighted the 
phrases and some words that were linked to the research questions while reading the 
transcripts. The researcher used the features of Nvivo software to highlight the phases and 
some words relating to the research questions.  
The second step of thematic analysis is the generation of initial codes (Byrne, 2022; Campbell 
et al., 2021; Xu & Zammit, 2020). Coding is important to analyse data and helps the researcher 
work towards achieving the research aims. After understanding the participants' responses 
comprehensively, the researcher started developing codes relevant to answering the research 
question. The gathered data of transcripts were coded using Nvivo (Bazeley & Jackson, 2013), 
and a backup copy was created to ensure that no data was lost until thoroughly utilised for the 
study (Stuckey, 2014).  The copies were stored on a separate device and placed in a protective 
location to prevent misuse, while in addition to electronic copies, hard copies were also made. 
The researcher developed the initial codes by labelling and organising data into meaningful 
groups using Nvivo software. Specifically, the researcher identified a chunk of words relevant 
to answering the research question and highlighted them within Nvivo. The researcher created 
nodes, which are coding categories, in Nvivo to store and organise the generated codes. The 
111 
 
researcher conducted the coding process systematically, meaning he read through each 
transcript at a time and highlighted the chunks of words that were relevant to answering the 
research questions. While developing codes, the researcher kept on revisiting the research 
questions to ensure that the initially developed codes were relevant to each question. For 
example, for the first research question (What are the financing preferences for UK SMEs in 
the changing economic and financial technology environment?), the researcher looked for 
phrases and words that expressed CEOs' preferences or non-preferences to Fintech and 
traditional financing. Examples of the codes that were generated for the first research question 
include prefer, secure, more efficient, cheap, and easy access to funds. These codes 
represented the rationale given by most CEOs for preferred Fintech.   
In the grounded approach, the data analysis starts with open coding to identify, name and 
categorise the phenomenon found in the data (Gligor, et al., 2016).  The researcher used an 
open coding technique to develop codes based on the participants' responses without the 
biases and preconceptions of the researcher (Vollstedt & Rezat, 2019). The researcher used 
an open coding technique to avoid making assumptions about the objective reality of the 
CEOs’ experiences. It is important to mention here that the researcher did not develop the 
codes based on his knowledge, preconceptions or available literature knowledge related to 
each research question. The open coding technique enabled the identification of emergent 
themes objectively, unlike pre-determined themes in priori coding. 
The third step in the thematic analysis was generating the initial themes (Byrne, 2022; 
Campbell et al., 2021; Xu & Zammit, 2020).  The researcher used the third step to develop 
initial themes by combining and sorting codes into initial themes and identified the meanings 
and relationships between the initial codes (Campbell, et al., 2021). The researcher conducted 
the third process by grouping and regrouping the codes that were developed to generate initial 
themes. Just like the code-developing process, the researcher read the sentences containing 
the codes and identified the relationships with the research questions. The researcher 
revisited the research questions every time to combine initially developed codes to develop 
112 
 
initial themes. The Nvivo software enabled the researcher to organise the initial codes and 
themes that were developed. For example, for each identified code, the software enabled the 
researcher to link codes to their respective themes. The researcher was able to see all codes 
for each theme using the NVivo software.    
The fourth step in the thematic analysis is reviewing the generated themes (Byrne, 2022; 
Campbell et al., 2021; Xu & Zammit, 2020). The researcher used the fourth step to refine the 
initial themes which were developed in step three. In the fourth step, the researcher made 
some other considerations and activities while refining themes. For example, the researcher 
ensured there was sufficient data to support themes. The researcher retained the themes that 
were supported by more than two participants. The researcher also collapsed overlapping 
themes in the fourth step. Further, the researcher checked for coherence and consistency of 
themes.  
The fifth step in the thematic analysis is defining and naming themes (Byrne, 2022; Campbell 
et al., 2021; Xu & Zammit, 2020). The researcher utilised the fifth step to finalise the themes 
that were relevant to answering the research questions. In the fifth step, the researcher 
ensured that the name of the themes succinctly captured the essence of the theme in 
answering the research question. According to Byrne (2022), the fifth step involves expressing 
each theme in relation to data and research questions. In addition, the researcher used the 
fifth step to support each theme with the participants verbatim. Specifically, the researcher 
linked the identified themes with participants' responses that contained codes and phrases 
supporting the theme. The last step in thematic analysis is producing a report. The last step 
involves presenting a concise account of the data and themes by writing compelling arguments 
that answer the research question (Campbell et al., 2021). The researcher utilised the last 
step to write research results about the study. The report contained the research questions 
and the themes that emerged to support each question. In the report of results, the researcher 
explained the themes and linked them with participants' responses and research questions.

3.3. Research Ethics 
3.3.1. 
Participant Anonymity  
Anonymity or confidentiality is an ethical practice to protect the identity of the research 
participants during the phases of data collection, analysis and reporting (Allen, 2017). The 
literature suggested that the participants must have an assurance that their confidentiality or 
anonymity is maintained by the researcher during the interview process (King & Horrocks, 
2010). The anonymity in the research refers to the process in which the participants of the 
research are completely unknown (Clark-Gordon, et al., 2019). The anonymity helps to protect 
the identities of the participants who agree to take part in the research interview, which also 
helps in the development of the participant’s trust in the researcher (Robinson, 2014). The 
steps to safeguarding the participant's identity provide them with an opportunity to respond 
more openly and freely when the interview questions are asked (Beatty, et al., 2014).  During 
the interview process, especially over the Internet, the confidentiality or anonymity of the 
participant is not fully ensured unless several steps are taken by the researcher because the 
115 
 
Internet or network service providers always have access to conversations between parties. 
Therefore, the researcher took several steps to protect the confidentiality and anonymity of 
the participant, such as using a random user ID and the removal of headers and tags during 
messaging over the internet. That assured the highest level of confidentiality to the participant. 
Therefore, in this way, the privacy of the participants was maintained in the research along 
with creating a comfortable, friendly environment so that the discussion flowed smoothly. 
 
3.3.2. 
Ethical Standards  
In the grounded approach, the ethical consideration includes maintaining confidentiality and 
obtaining informed consent from the participants (Gligor, et al., 2016). This research is a 
personal activity that requires hard work, dedication, and the highest standards to maintain its 
integrity and validity. This integrity requires following rules, regulations, industry codes and 
applicable laws. The current research involves the researcher and participants and includes 
the organisations where participants work, the institution where this study is undertaken, and 
all those connected persons who are directly or indirectly involved in the research. Therefore, 
the researcher tries to ensure that the current research should not harm any parties and should 
follow rules of ethics at each stage of the research process. If the rules of ethics are not 
followed, then the outcome of the research may damage people in a psychological or 
materialistic way. It will also damage the credibility of the research. Therefore, ethics are an 
integral part of every research study and, as such, were the foremost task of importance before 
beginning the research into the topic and approaching the selected participants. This 
qualitative research has its unique characteristics as an open-ended character, unlike 
quantitative research, where the hypothesis is tested. It also has its limitations because the 
researcher will not have control over the people who may misuse the publication of the 
findings. The researcher’s ethical stance in this research is that of a deontologist. This is to 
ensure that the researcher will focus on his action during the research process and take all 
those reasonable steps to assess the likelihood and the severity of the harm that this current 
116 
 
research may bring to the participants and involved parties. The School Research Ethical 
Panel (SREP) approved the research under the terms of Anglia Research University's 
Research Ethics Policy Ver 1.7 (Appendix 9). Therefore, the researcher understood and 
complied with the University's highest standard of research ethics (ARU, 2021), the code of 
practice for the research (UKRIO, 2009) and the Data Protection Act 2018 regulations to 
confirm the research integrity. That also ensures the participants’ privacy, confidentiality, and 
anonymity.  
The considerations undertaken for this study were regarding maintaining ethics involving 
voluntary participation in the study, obtaining their consent and then their views through 
interviews for the analysis, and drawing conclusions to meet the aims and objectives of the 
study (Ponterotto, 2010). Maintaining the participants' anonymity and confidentiality was of the 
utmost importance while ensuring there was no risk of harm to them, for which the ethical 
considerations, policies and research guidelines were followed to reduce risk. During the 
virtual interviews, privacy was the key issue, and it was solved with unique coding. Privacy 
properties were adjusted so no one could listen and have access to the electronic recording.  
To maintain the highest level of integrity and the confidentially in the study, the consent forms 
(Appendix 10) were distributed to the participants through email so they could go through the 
form and respond appropriately. They were then asked to return the form with their signature, 
signalling their agreement to participate in the study (Connelly, 2014). To further ensure that 
the research was conducted ethically, all the participants were treated equally regardless of 
their characteristics to ensure no bias. No identifying information was asked from the 
participants that could potentially risk their anonymity, and as such, no private data was shared 
in the findings and discussion of the research study (Hallowell & Gambatese, 2010). 
Confidentiality was ensured throughout the interview process, even before and after the 
research, with steps to ensure participants did not disclose their personal information. Audio 
and video recordings were stored in data storage, and a backup was created on the secure 
server. Both physical and virtual copies were protected in a safe location for unauthorised 
117 
 
access. The researcher further took steps and did not share any data with other academic 
colleagues. The researcher also maintained the participants’ anonymisation by replacing their 
names with random invented names and using their initials instead of full names to record 
data. As mentioned before, the researcher took all reasonable steps to identify the likelihood 
and impact of the research that is also valid for the storage, backup, and security of the data 
in computers and networks. The researcher used drop box premium for the entire research. 
Dropbox is a file hosting service to store, synchronise and share files. Dropbox files are 
encrypted using 256-bit Advanced Encryption Standard (AES). At the same time, the 
application creates a secure tunnel protected by 128-bit or higher Advanced Encryption 
Standard (AES) encryption and Two-step verification for an extra layer of security. That also 
meets the ISO ISO/IEC 27001 security arrangement to store and backup the data. The 
researcher will store data for at least five years before it is securely discarded. That ensures 
that the highest ethical standards were followed throughout the research.  
 
 
118 
 
4. Results  
The findings of the research include interviews with twenty CEOs of UK SMEs to gain detailed 
insights about Fintech financing decision-making. The semi-structured interviews were 
conducted, meaning the questions' sequence and phrasing were not followed strictly (Kallio, 
et al., 2016). Despite the benefits of Fintech financing to provide improved and innovative 
financial service delivery as a disruption for traditional financing, the factors influencing CEOs 
to make such decisions are not explored. Therefore, this study aims to understand the CEOs' 
decision making relating to Fintech financing in UK SMEs. The results are based on the 
following four main research questions based on the research objectives to achieve the 
research aim. 
Research Question 1: How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing choices in the 
context of a rapidly evolving economic and financial technology environment?  
Research Question 2: How do CEOs of UK SMEs perceive the influence of micro-level 
factors, such as personal leadership traits, vision and risk-taking attitude on their Fintech 
financing decisions? 
Research Question 3: How do CEOs of UK SMEs make their decisions on Fintech financing 
through professional networking, board of directors and organisational structure contexts at 
the meso level?  
Research Question 4: How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate the complexities of macro-level 
factors, including government support and laws, when making Fintech financing decisions? 
4.1. How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate their financing choices in 
the context of a rapidly evolving economic and financial 
technology environment? 
The first research question was used to explore the perceptions of CEOs regarding their 
financing choices at the UK SMEs. Three themes supported the first research question are (a) 
CEOs in the UK prefer Fintech to traditional financing, (b) Increasing Demand and Banking 
119 
 
Challenges Necessitate Fintech Financing Options, and (c) limited awareness about Fintech 
options. 
4.1.1. 
The CEOs in the UK prefer Fintech to Traditional Financing 
Consumers are more willing to adopt or use digital services that offer them added value, such 
as time-saving, improved efficiency, and less effort (Daragmeh, et al., 2021). Information 
technology is one of the significant drivers due to its benefits, such as the adoption of mobile 
technology (Hassan, et al., 2022). Consumers prefer technology-enabled services that provide 
efficiency or more benefits at a lower cost and with less complexity to meet their needs. In this 
study, the CEOs in the UK indicated their preference for Fintech over traditional financing 
options. The rationale for the preference includes that Fintech provides more options, cheap 
financing options and fewer regulations. For example, the preference for Fintech is a key 
interest of UK CEOs in SMEs, although Fintech is relatively new to them in the current market. 
The statistics from the sample confirm that 70% of UK CEOs from SMEs prefer Fintech 
financing to traditional financing, while 15% are into traditional financing. 15% of CEOs don’t 
want to prefer FinTech to traditional or traditional to FinTech, and they prefer both. When we 
further narrow down statistics concerning the male and female CEOs’ preferences, 83% of 
female CEOs prefer FinTech financing and 17% prefer traditional financing. The statistics from 
male CEOs confirm the highest preference for FinTech financing. 57% of male CEOs prefer 
FinTech as opposed to 14%, while 29% don’t want to prefer any method of financing; they 
prefer both for the benefit of their organisations.  
A series of questions were asked to CEOs, starting with “What are your financing preferences 
– traditional or fintech financing?” and follow-up questions about why and the fitness of their 
preference in the current economic and digital age. The results bring three sub-themes 
regarding their FinTech financing preference, as illustrated in the following diagram.  
120

4.1.1.1. 
Fintech Provided More Financing Options 
Fintech offers diverse financial options, such as insurance and payment systems, and scalable 
services across the globe. There are six Fintech business models, including (a) insurance 
124 
 
services, (b) payments, (c) crowdfunding, (d) lending, (e) capital markets, and (f) wealth 
management (Suryono, et al., 2020). Each helps businesses adapt to guidelines efficiently 
and resourcefully (Kumari & Devi, 2022). As a technological modernisation in the financial 
industry, Fintech provides alternative funding with more options. Patterns that supported the 
first sub-theme, the UK CEOs’ preference of Fintech over traditional financing, include more 
options, cryptocurrency, crowdfunding, GoFundMe, Kickstarter, and more investors. These 
electronic platforms increase convenience and access to financial services because of their 
reliability, easy accessibility, and high speed (Kumari & Devi, 2022). Not only this, but due to 
accessibility features, these funds are accessible to clients in different parts of the globe. For 
example, P11's rationale for FinTech preference is that it is associated with more financing 
options they can use to acquire funds needed to run their business. According to P11, Fintech 
has options like crowdfunding, where their SME can quickly get money. Other Fintech options 
identified by P11 include GoFundMe, Indiegoo, and Seedrs. P11 identified the Fintech 
financing options by stating,  
We found Seedrs, GoFundMe, and Indiegogo have many opportunities for 
our business. 
P11 supported the first sub-theme by stating,  
I support emerging artists and illustrators. They know it is a difficult time for 
them to survive. Our team goes through their artwork. If we need money to 
order equipment for mass printing, then we will go for crowdfunding.  
Similar to P11, P5 mentioned that Fintech has many financing options to meet the financing 
needs of their business. P5 stated the use of Fintech - crowdfunding by stating,  
I have crowdfunding experience. We raised money for our business using 
crowdfunding. We know how important it is for a business to have more 
financing options for the project.

We fund our project with crowdfunding. It is a good option for investors to 
be involved in such a project, which gives them a good return.

4.1.1.2. 
Fintech is Associated with Lower Costs 
The second sub-theme that supported the theme of UK CEOs’ preference for Fintech over 
traditional financing was that Fintech options are associated with low costs. It is to be 
mentioned here that the price value is a consumer’s cognitive characteristic whereby they 
decide to purchase a product based on the cost incurred and the associated benefits   (Najib, 
et al., 2021).  Fintech transactions are far more convenient, affordable, and secure than other 
funding (Yan, et al., 2021). Therefore, the cost is a significant factor influencing consumers’ 
decision to adopt Fintech, and CEOs would prefer financing options at a lower cost. This 
study's patterns supporting the second sub-theme include low interest rates, low cost, good 
profit, and cheaper rates. Cheaper rates signify lower transaction and annual fees with low-
interest rates to protect from the higher prices caused by inflation (Sager, 2021). When asked 
why they prefer Fintech to traditional financing options, P11 provided his opinion that Fintech 
is associated with low cost and is profitable for their SME. P11 stated 
It is a low cost and gives us a good profit.

high-interest rate by banks pushed us to get funds through Fintech.  
In addition, P5 supported the theme that banks have high-interest rates on loans during the 
period of high inflation and further of opinion that Fintech is a cheap alternative funding source.

4.1.1.3. 
Fintech is Associated with Lower Regulations  
A third sub-theme that supported the theme of UK CEOs’ Fintech financing preference was 
that Fintech is associated with lower regulations. In the UK, there is no specific regulatory 
framework for Fintech companies subject to financial regulations (Giglio, 2021). However, 
Fintech firms are regulated like other companies based on the regulated services they provide 
(Giglio, 2021). The CEOs of SMEs confirmed the absence of specific regulations governing 
Fintech financing by arguing that there are lower regulations associated with Fintech as 
compared to traditional financing options. The patterns supporting the third sub-theme include 
no strict regulations, fewer regulations, fair trading standards, lending criteria, and payment 
flexibility.

4.2. How do CEOs of UK SMEs perceive the influence of micro-
level factors, such as personal leadership traits, vision and 
risk-taking attitude, on their Fintech financing decisions? 
 
The second research question was used to explore the micro factors such as CEOs’ personal 
leadership traits, vision, and risk-taking attitude to navigate their Fintech financing decisions 
at the UK SMEs. The major themes that supported the research question include (a) 
leadership traits to guide FinTech decisions, (b) using vision to help Fintech decisions, (c) 
using values to guide decision-making, (d) incorporating family members’ perspectives in 
making Fintech decisions (e) using personal beliefs to reinforce Fintech decision-making, (f) 
use educational knowledge to improve Fintech decision making, (g) experience and (h) risk 
taker or avoider approach to make Fintech decisions  
4.2.1. 
Leadership Traits 
Leadership in the organisation's context refers to the senior officers holding the key positions 
to manage, organise and lead staff and contractors. They perform their day-to-day work to 
fulfil their responsibilities. CEOs, presidents, directors, managers and supervisors are the 
most prominent positions. As a multidimensional term with multiple meanings of leadership, 
personal leadership in this research refers to CEOs' traits to make FinTech financing 
decisions. The CEOs’ role in the organisation is not as easy as described in their job 
responsibilities. They have to make complex decisions, and they stand out for being more 
decisive by inspiring their team members, with great impact and reliability to drive performance 
in their organisations (Botelho, et al., 2017). When leaders make decisions, their internal 
thoughts, intelligence and feelings greatly influence their effectiveness (Bryant & Kazan, 
2012). Inner behaviour individuals relate their leadership, emotions, skills, knowledge, 
understanding and habits (Scouller, 2011).   
CEOs as leaders take responsibility for their actions and understand the consequences of 
their decisions. Their decisions in the organisations are not only to perform their roles up to a 
136 
 
certain level to achieve the organisational objectives, but they also consider their feelings and 
thoughts at the individual level. Being leaders of their organisations enables CEOs to be more 
engaged, cooperative and dedicated to their team (Bryant & Kazan, 2012), which improves 
their work productivity (Farcht, 2007) and creates a meaningful impact on the organisation's 
performance (King, et al., 2011). Over the years, scholars have built extensive literature on 
leadership, particularly on the leadership of CEOs, who are known to substantially impact 
organisational performance (Nohria & Khurana, 2010). CEOs act as change agents and 
introduce new approaches to achieve the organisational vision. Several pieces of literature 
support the leadership traits as trusting, focusing on the matters (Anderson, 2012), taking 
action in critical situations (Kruse, 2019), being practical to solve problems  (Grint, 2010), and 
leading change (Bird, et al., 2013) and work with integrity, courage and vision (Al-Azami, 
2019). So what does the CEO’s leadership include, especially at the personal level, and how 
do they make informed decisions at the organisational level? The emerging themes have 
concluded a detailed insight into CEOs' perception of their thoughts and opinions of what they 
think of their leadership and its link to financial decisions. P10 emphasis is more focused on

4.2.2. 
Use Vision to Guide Fintech Decisions 
The development and advancement of Fintech financing options do not only depend on 
regulations to grow but also on user perspectives and knowledge (Setiawan, et al., 2021). 
Thus, user perspectives, such as vision, beliefs, and values, can influence how individuals 
make Fintech financial decisions. According to Klynveld Peat Marwick Goerdeler (2023), 
clarity of vision comes first in the 10 characteristics of a Fintech leader. Therefore, vision is a 
crucial attribute that organisational leaders should demonstrate for the successful running of 
the business. Fintech CEOs should have concrete long-term visions instead of focusing on 
objectives (Klynveld Peat Marwick Goerdeler, 2023). CEOs integrate their personal and firm 
vision while making Fintech decisions to ensure the firm's long-term focus. Most participants 
reported that they use their visions to guide Fintech financing decisions. Vision provides a 
clear picture of what an organisation wants to achieve in the future, and the CEOs play a

fintech financing.

4.2.5. 
Using Personal Beliefs Reinforces Fintech Decision-Making 
The benefits and risks of using Fintech financing options can be based on personal beliefs, 
both negative and positive (Mascarenhas, et al., 2021). Based on Net Valence Model (NVM), 
if perceived benefits are more significant than perceived risks, potential adopters are more 
likely to engage in the activity or adopt new technology (Abdul-Rahim, et al., 2022). The main 
components of the NVM model are perceived (a) benefits, (b) risk, (c) and net valence. 
Perceived benefits are expected positive impacts of the change, while perceived risks are 
148 
 
anticipated negative consequences. Net valence is the difference between expected positive 
and negative utilities (Abdul-Rahim, et al., 2022). The perceived benefits, risk, and net valence 
influence the behavioural intentions of individuals while making decisions (Figure-14). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 14 - Net Valence Model 
 
In this study, perceived benefits, risk, and net valence of CEOs influence their beliefs and 
behavioural intentions, which, in turn, affect how they make Fintech decisions. For example, 
CEOs who believe that Fintech financing options are associated with more benefits than risk 
had a positive net valence and could prefer Fintech options based on the perceived benefits. 
On the contrary, CEOs who believed that there are more risks than benefits associated with 
Fintech had negative net valence and that would change their belief to prefer Fintech over 
traditional financing.  
The most participants of this study reported that their personal beliefs reinforce how they make 
their Fintech decisions. For example, P11 indicated that caring for others and optimism are 
characteristics of his belief that influence Fintech decision-making.  
My habits of caring for others and an optimistic about my decision affect my 
Fintech financing decisions.

4.2.8. 
Risk approach to make Fintech decisions  
The main drivers of CEOs' actions and organisational outcomes include CEO risk-taking and 
financial literacy (Duréndez, et al., 2023). Risk-taking refers to the CEO's willingness to devote 
resources to exploit opportunities or engage in behaviours with undetermined habits 
(Duréndez, et al., 2023). Financial literacy refers to the financial knowledge needed to make 
decisions (Duréndez, et al., 2023). Therefore, risk-taking and financial literacy affect how 
CEOs make organisational decisions and results, including Fintech decisions and outcomes. 
As the risk is associated with the probability and the level of impact. It is an event that may 
occur. Suppose the probability of occurrence could be within the range of 0-100%. 
Alternatively, these values can be expressed between 0 to 1. In the normal distribution, if the 
event range is higher than 0.5 and less than 1, it is considered likely to happen.  In the risk 
management framework, likelihood and the consequences are framed with numbers and their 
interpretation to accept, tolerate, or avoid risk. The high risk is associated with high 
154 
 
consequences; in return, it could be a high return or total loss or damage to the business. The 
CEOs were asked to provide their risk-taking and risk-avoider approach in the interview. The 
risk-taking approach means the CEO takes risks with a probability of higher than 0.5 in the 
standard distribution scale and higher consequences per the risk management framework. 
The risk avoidance approach means the CEO avoids a higher risk of more than 0.5 on the 
average distribution level, which can turn into a significant incident and, as a result, can cause 
poor performance of the company or related negative consequences.  
In this research, 55% of participants confirmed that they are risk takers, of which 25% are 
females and 30% are males. While 45% of participants avoid taking significant risks and 
establish them as risk avoiders, out of which 5% are females, and 40% are males. It is also 
noted that 10% of participants confirmed that they assess the situation and anticipate higher 
return before they make decisions.  
For example, P18 has an opinion that his risk approach depends on the level of reward, such 
as  
It depends on how good the reward is of the risk . The high risk , high risk 
means higher reward , but if the reward is worth the risk , then I might do . 
That's the thing. 
A similar answer was from participant P7 as of P18, whose focus was a higher return but less 
negative consequences on the organisation. P7 argues  
I think I am a risk avoider, but sometimes I also take risk if the return is 
good and not much financial implications.  
To justify risk avoider approach, P19 provided a comprehensive opinion to justify his risk 
avoider approach, which is mainly associated with the return, market uncertainty, regulations 
and history of cryptocurrency downfall in the financial crash

the risk would be minimal.  
P19's perception about the crypto market is volatile, so decisions relating to FinTech are risky. 
P11 risk avoider approach confirms that risky decisions are not secure and, therefore, “not 
stable”. P14 described that  
Risk avoider approach helped me to make good and right decisions for the 
benefits of my company.  
CEO P9 works in a care organisation which is responsible for providing care to vulnerable 
people, so he is a risk-avoider, not particularly because of his own leadership or decision-
making but because of the people around him or the work they perform in the organisation. 
P9 believes the risk avoider approach is good to avoid uncertainty. While P9 provided a 
considerable argument for his approach of being a risk avoider  
You don’t have to be a risk-taker if your clients are vulnerable. 
156 
 
Vulnerable people are disabled; they can be of any age or even young adult with or without 
disability. Vulnerable people also include people who need care, support, and protection due 
to many factors, such as age, disability, abuse, and neglect. That suggests that the risk-taking 
and avoider approach is directly or indirectly affected or influenced by the types of 
organisations and sectors.   
In this research, the risk-taker CEO ratio is 10% higher than the risk avoider. Some provided 
helpful rationale and insights into why they think of being risk-takers. For example, P2 has an 
opinion that  
I always go for uncertain situations, maybe mostly because my beliefs help 
me to have confidence to have decisions. I think if you know the probability 
of outcome, it is very easy for you to make Fintech decisions. But it 
something is so new like Fintech finance is, you really don’t know the 
outcome and you remain unsure whether you will raise that particular 
amount through P2P landing or even crowd cube. And you know once you 
have funds it is good for your organisation. 
Despite the known risk factors and consequences, risk-taker CEOs take risks to get a good 
return and avoid taking a complex route to earning money through other traditional lending 
forums.  
P3 is a risk taker because of the opinion that it has a “positive outcome”. P8's risk-taking 
approach to funding the business through FinTech could be because of “strict regulations and 
lenders criteria of traditional financing”.  The results further confirm that CEOs' risk-taking or 
avoiding approaches positively impact the company’s performance as per CEOs' perceptions 
and opinions. CEOs with a 55% risk-taking approach felt the positive outcome of their 
decisions on the organisation. Further, 45% of avoider CEOs also have a positive impact on 
their decisions on the company's performance. The results indicate that CEOs have different 
perceptions about their risk approach linked to the return. The risky financing decisions may 
result in high returns. Still, the outcome or the process to bring that outcome may have those 
157 
 
consequences on the organisation, which CEOs avoid in their financing decisions. This also 
confirms that CEOs are potent entities in organisations, and they make careful decisions, 
assess the situation or the project, evaluate the risk factors and then make the decisions. The 
risk approach is also associated with gender, age group, and education level. For example, 
younger CEOs are 5% more risk-takers than avoiders. The results further suggest that CEOs' 
risk approach implies them to make careful decisions and ensure the positive impact of their 
organisation.  
 
 
 
4.3. How do CEOs of UK SMEs make their decisions on Fintech 
financing through professional networking, board of directors 
and organisational structure contexts at the meso level? 
 
The third research question explored the significance of meso factors such as professional 
networking, board of directors, and organisational structure to CEOs' Fintech financing 
decisions at UK SMEs. Three major themes supporting the third research question emerged. 
The first theme was incorporating professional advice and contributions, and the second was 
consulting the board while making Fintech decisions. The third theme was the effects of 
organisational structure on FinTech decisions. Each of the themes is discussed below. 
4.3.1. 
Incorporating Advice and Contributions from Other 
Professionals 
This study presumed that CEOs in professional organisations with professional connections 
with individuals in the Fintech sector would consult them while making Fintech decisions more 
than those with limited connections. Most participants reported that their professional 
networking influences how they make Fintech decisions. The networking individuals identified 
include investors, friends, professionals from other fields, and workmates. For example, P10 
works in an SME that has a network of people such as investors, accounts and staff. Their 
158 
 
SME considers the reporting from these entities to be feedback to help them make Fintech 
decisions. P10 stated,  
Our investors provide us with feedback, accountants share profitability 
reports, and staff help with their suggestions. They all affect my Fintech 
decisions.  
Also, P13 acknowledged that his administrative team managing e-platforms. E-platforms has 
a team to discuss the main issues that contribute to his Fintech decisions. P13 stated,  
My administration team manages e platform, and they contribute to my 
Fintech financing decision. 
P14 reported that they discuss Fintech options with workmate friends, and their opinions 
influence how he makes Fintech decisions. P14 stated,  
 
We discuss Fintech options and their opinions are always helpful.  
In addition, P6 consults other professionals in the same situations and believes such 
discussions are crucial for financing decisions. P6 supported his argument by stating,  
Yes, but help you in any situation that could be the scene-making situation 
that you see it is important to consult with professional people involved in 
it. 
P7 acknowledged that sharing information with people from experience backgrounds, 
managers, directors, and board members influences his Fintech financing decisions. P7 
supported his argument by stating,  
When we shared information and listened to each other that may influence 
my Fintech decision. Yes, when we share information with each other and 
discuss different aspects of the business that contribute to my Fintech.  
In addition, P8 reported learning from professional networks and using that knowledge to guide 
making Fintech financial decisions. P8 indicated that his professional networks include senior 
159 
 
managers and supervisors in the company and membership in a professional organisation. 
P1 also acknowledged the influence of professional networks in making Fintech decisions by 
stating,  
I also consider I am being influenced by my professional network regarding 
Fintech decisions because I always listen to them.  
P2 reported that the experience of other CEOs from his networking influenced how he makes 
Fintech decisions. P2 stated,  
Not necessarily, except other CEOs from my networking share their 
experience, so sometimes it influences my Fintech decisions.  
P18 also states that contributions from other professionals are essential in making Fintech 
financial decisions because they present more financial options and ideals. P18 supported his 
argument by stating,  
Yes, because of professional networking, you'll have more options for 
financing. As well as you'll meet more people that could potentially help you 
and give you [CEO] ideas.  
 P3 argued that professional networks are crucial in making Fintech decisions and reported 
that he uses social media to learn from others. P3 gave a comprehensive response supporting 
his argument by stating: 
 
Sometimes it influences my decisions. I feel the professional network is so 
important to make decisions as if it gives you a thought process. I also use 
social media to learn from others which also contribute to my decisions 
making. 
 
160 
 
4.3.2. 
Consulting Board while Making Fintech Decisions 
In the current age, corporate governance has become critical for organisations’ success, 
especially after the financial crash of 2008 (Calder, 2008). Corporate governance is a system 
by which the board directs and controls the organisation (Financial Reporting Council, 2018). 
In this research, all participants confirm that they have a board to make financial decisions. 
One of the main questions was establishing the board's composition to have a ratio of 
executive and non-executive directors. The executive directors perform the daily function of 
the organisation while the non-executive directors are independent, recruited from the industry 
with expertise in their areas of employment to serve the specialised work. Non-executive 
directors’ role is to scrutinise the board’s performance and oversee the financial control. The 
participants’ SME CEOs have a board in their organisation composed of executive and non-
executive directors. Having non-executive directors on the board confirms independent and 
impartial advice and sound decisions for the organisation's interest, especially for the benefit 
of stakeholders (Corporate Governance Institute, 2023). The board's composition with 
independent elements at the senior level increases accountability (Agarwal, 2020) and 
confirms the highest standard and the integrity of the decisions (Scott, 2018). According to P9,  
The board members create a culture of accountability.  
Institutional factors are crucial in reinforcing innovations in information and communication 
technologies, including adopting and using Fintech options (Setiawan, et al., 2021). For 
example, boards can influence firms’ decisions by contributing to the decision-making 
process. Corporate boards and directors play a crucial role in an organisation’s strategy and 
performance. The contribution of board members could support CEOs' decision-making in 
Fintech. A notable finding that supported the research question was that CEOs consult board 
members while making decisions related to Fintech financing. For example, P10 reported that 
most of his decisions are endorsed by the board members. P10 stated, 
161 
 
Working the board is amazing. I learn and make decisions independently. 
The board has a committee. Most of my decision is endorsed by the board 
committee without my changes.  
Despite not being part of the board, P11 reported that board decisions influence how he makes 
Fintech decisions. P11 stated, 
 I am not a part of our board of directors. They hired me for a CEO role. 
Their decision and direction help me in my Fintech decisions.  
P8 perception of the board work is through policies as rules to guide his decisions.  
the board influences my decisions, mainly because of board policies and 
agenda for the work.  
P1 gave a specific example of how the board influenced the selection of Fintech financing 
options for raising the required funds in their company. P1 stated,  
Once our company needed £75000 and we decided to sell our assets, and 
when I spoke to my family, they told don’t do it, just sell your property. But 
when I spoke to the board, they said, we should raise money through 
Fintech pools.  
When asked whether board members influence how he makes decisions, P2 acknowledged 
that they make collective decisions and that board members influence how Fintech decisions 
are made. P2 supported his opinion by stating: 
 
We all work collectively when we have to make decisions such as Fintech 
finances. We talk to each other, we have a lot of information from 
management, we use scenarios based on decisions making tools, we 
evaluate risks, and then our chairman makes decisions. 
However, with the board, the Fintech decisions are not the final verdict of the CEO for some 
SMEs. This matter is escalated to the Board for financial decisions, which can be collectively 
162 
 
made by members or by the chair. For example, P5, a part of the board, confirms that “only 
the chairperson makes the final decision”. P13 stated that he is a part of the board, and they 
[the board] make the collective decisions. According to P13, when it comes to financial 
decisions,  
They [the board] contribute to my fintech financing decision.  
One of the findings confirmed the board-led decisions in Fintech matters such as P19 states 
that.  
The board of directors make collective decisions and as a CEO, you know, 
my decision doesn't matter.  
P3 who is a part of the Board but without any non-executive directors in the organisation 
confirmed that the decisions are made collectively. P3 stated,  
Senior-level collective decisions help the company.  
P4 and P7 also provided similar views on making collective decisions on financial matters. 
P16 also confirmed that the board contributed to the fintech financing decision. P16 supported 
the role of the board and its influence on the decision as   
More strategic level decision making in the board and when working in a 
board with other members, executive knowing exactly what maybe 
depends on the composition like you have, isn't this influence your decision 
making 
 
4.3.3. 
Effects of the Organisational structural to make Fintech 
Decision Making 
 
The current study's number of questions to CEOs was based on their organisational structure 
and the impact of it on their decision-making. That includes the company's size, the information 
flow mechanism from the employees to CEOs, the number of departments, and the 
163 
 
organisation’s structural composition for innovation and change to contribute to CEOs’ 
decision-making. The valuable research results show that 50% of SMEs have their 
organisational structure developed in a way that is based on the different departments. For 
example, P6 who works in a small SME mentioned, 
We work on different business operations and are divided into departments 
separately to consider sales and product development. 
P10 described the departmental structure of their small SME as  
We have a customer services and service distribution centre, Human 
resource, marketing and Sales, Finance Operations and Innovation Hub.  
P10 further explored the concept of decision-making within such organisational structures as  
All our departments have heads. They report to their line managers and the 
line managers to their CEO.  
That shows that the number of departments within the organisation causes a higher line of 
accountability for information travel from the employees to the department heads and then to 
the CEO. That is far different from the micro-SME functions where information does not have 
many barriers for CEOs to make timely FinTech decisions. For example, P2 stated  
We all work collectively in the same on the same floor. And our desks are 
very close to each other and they share information very easily. We are all 
so happy to work together in the same floor that help our communication 
and networking. So it's just like very easy to communicate and share our 
information.   
P8 supplement the thoughts of P2 as  
We work collectively, discuss and share what information is readily 
available to make decisions.   
164 
 
Some SMEs, which are micro with less than 10 people, have a smooth process to perform 
their daily tasks, and the CEO remains the main person for operational and oversight of the 
organisational activities. For example, to provide a detailed understanding of the flow of 
information for decision-making, the P12 stated,  
I actually have technicians and suppliers. I allocate work to that to my team. 
They are accountable to me for the work. When they complete the task, 
they inform me and I contact the distributor to deliver the order. 
 Likewise, P20 described it as  
I don't have any layers between me and my staff. So. But whenever I give 
them any task, they come to me directly and they, you know, respond really, 
really quickly.  
This confirms that SMEs are clear about their organisational structure, understand their staff 
accountability, and CEOs remain the main decision-makers. However, when it comes to the 
flow of information, few CEOs went into details of how they ensure timely information. For 
example, P14 confirmed the use of IT applications and the role of the staff member for the 
information to make decisions   
My subordinates provide me all information I need. We use CRM and all 
information is shared instantly and it's helpful to accept change.  
However, when it comes to the barriers in decision-making due to complex organisational 
structure or the flow of information within the organisation, all CEOs (100% of the sample) 
don’t consider their organisational structure to be complex regardless of whether they are 
sitting in the same floor to work together or have a number of departments. They have different 
opinions; for example, P6 explained the use of software and timely reports for decision his 
decision-making. According to P6  
So I don't see any problem on the information flow. We use centralized 
software where reports updated by managing stocks are listed. Finance 
165 
 
reports are available. Account information is published every week. Staff 
training and development is recorded as well. Money matters and the 
associate reports are produced by Finance Department.  
P18 explained,  
Having a complex structure doesn't necessarily mean it's going to be hard 
for decision making because only the directors will be doing the decision 
making. Everyone else will do their part for the decision or out of it. So, no, 
I don't think it'll affect the organisation to accept change.  
The statistics from the participants further confirmed that the composition of the organisation 
structure to facilitate the flow of information has an impact on the CEOs to make FinTech 
decisions as well as that also affects the SMEs to accept change. At least 60% of CEOs from 
micro, small or medium SMEs have opinions that the flow of information from their employees 
to them for timely and effective FinTech-related decision-making has causes and 
consequences of accepting the change. Meanwhile, 70% of CEOs confirmed that their 
decisions are influenced by their subordinates' or staff members’ reports to make FinTech 
financing decisions. 
 
4.4. How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate the complexities of 
macro-level factors, including government support and laws, 
when making Fintech financing decisions? 
 
Six major themes emerged in the fourth research question to explore the complexity of 
government support and regulations as macro factors to navigate CEOs' perceptions to make 
Fintech financing decisions at the UK SMEs. The themes include (a) aligning Fintech decisions 
to government policies, (b) insufficient information and policies about Fintech limits decision 
making, (c) using UK laws to guide decision making, (d) negative political and economic 
situations necessitate Fintech options, (e) some laws impede Fintech decision-making, and 
166 
 
(f) making Fintech decisions to align with the emerging technologies. Each of the themes is 
discussed below. 
4.4.1. 
Aligning Fintech decisions to government policies 
The financial sector and organisations plan Fintech adoption, change management, 
leadership commitment, and stakeholder management to effectively align with government 
policies to transition without resistance. Aligning Fintech decisions will improve adoption and 
change, competitiveness, and performance in the financial and banking sector (Dwivedi, et 
al., 2021). Therefore, CEOs seeking to adopt Fintech consider government regulations while 
making Fintech decisions. P19 gave a comprehensive response on aligning their financial 
decisions with government policies to avoid engaging in illegal activities such as money 
laundering. P19 stated,  
 
As I mentioned, we have legal department and always follow up on 
government policies as they severely affect our decision. For example, one 
person came to me and he wanted to transfer his house on his wife name 
or his son's name. So, we are the source of funding. We will not look at the 
field. We will not look at the funding forensic technology. We looked at the 
government policies like we always do. We must check in on the money 
laundering, will checked the source of funding, We also check the other 
rules. So definitely, we must always follow the government regulations, 
which affect our decisions 
The first theme that emerged to support the fourth research question was that CEOs reported 
that they align Fintech decisions with government policies. Almost all participants reported 
keenly considering government policies and regulations before making Fintech decisions. 
Also, most participants reported that they have a legal team that guides them on government 
policies and regulations related to Fintech financing. For example, P10 said they have a legal 
team that follows up with legal requirements before signing contracts. P10 stated,  
167 
 
I follow up with our legal team before I sign a contract with the distributors 
and investors.  
Besides the internal rules, P11 reported adhering to government policies while making 
decisions. P11 stated, 
Our board of directors makes rules for our company, and we consult with 
the government policies. 
 Also, P13 acknowledged the presence of several regulations and reported that they comply 
with them when making Fintech decisions. P13 supported her argument by stating,  
Our sector is complex; it has many regulators, and we have to comply with 
them. It supports my Fintech decisions.  
In addition, P5 believe working within the government policies is crucial for their business. P5 
stated,  
Yes, we follow government regulations, and they are very important for our 
organisation to work within consistent policies.  
Similar to P11, P13, and P5, P6 indicated that they regularly check on different government 
departments' reports about Fintech. In addition, P7 supported the theme of aligning Fintech 
decisions to government policies by acknowledging that such regulations impact how he 
makes Fintech decisions. P7 stated,  
Government support businesses, and they have regulations that have an 
impact on my Fintech financing decisions.  
Like P10, P8 reported that they have a legal team that ensures they follow government 
regulations. P8 stated,  
Our legal department ensures we follow government regulations. I make a 
decision by ensuring it complies with laws.  
168 
 
P9 further supported the first theme by reporting that they are in a highly regulated industry 
and must comply with different policies. P9 stated,  
Our industry is very much regulated. We have to follow government 
policies. I follow my company’s policies and the skills for the manager’s 
code of practice to maintain my membership. I follow up with policies during 
my decisions. 
Similarly, P1 acknowledged the presence of government policies and reported that they follow 
them while making Fintech decisions. P1 stated,  
Government policies are guidelines for us, but it depends on how 
supportive they are for our business, and my decisions are influenced by 
them. I find them much supportive of solving my situations relating to 
Fintech finance.  
P3 supported aligning Fintech decisions to government policies by arguing that they ensure 
they follow government policies while making Fintech decisions. P3 stated,  
 
Yes, I ensure my decisions follow government policies.  
 
4.4.2. 
Insufficient Information and Policies about Fintech Limits 
Decision Making 
According to Ahern (2021), an inadequate framework in Fintech markets results in regulatory 
fragmentation and friction. The European Union’s Fintech competitiveness in innovation is 
compromised if the regulatory landscape is cumbersome or incomplete for new business 
models (Ahern, 2021). Fintech has no single, comprehensive, and credible regulatory market. 
There is a demand for clear regulations to improve the development of Fintech. The lack of a 
comprehensive regulatory framework for Fintech firms depicts inconsistencies and a lack of 
standards, which could negatively impact Fintech adopters' financial decisions. A second 
theme that supported the fourth research question was that inadequate information and 
169 
 
policies about Fintech limit the CEOs' decision-making of Fintech financing decisions. For 
example, P12 detailed that the government does not have a digital platform for listing Fintech 
financing companies and suggested that they develop such systems to help them. P12 
responded to the question as,  
The government does not have any digital platform to list Fintech financing 
companies. They should work on this agenda. It will help many businesses 
like us. 
P14 believe in the following government regulations, however, with the opinion that there is 
no specific support for Fintech. P14 stated,  
We don’t have any particular link with the government department for 
Fintech funding. Their policies are regularly viewed by our board.  
P5 reported that there are limited policies about Fintech on government websites and there is 
insufficient support for Fintech. P5 supported his argument by stating,  
There are not many policies about Fintech on the government websites 
regarding Fintech. Small business support is always an issue and support 
is available through local authorities, but not much support is available on 
Fintech.  
Also, P5 stated that there is limited information about Fintech options such as cryptocurrency.  
What we can see that P2P lending and crowdfunding there is some 
information while cryptocurrency information is very limited and if that 
information exists that is only on tax return related compliance. 
P2 supported the theme that insufficient information and policies limit making decisions about 
Fintech and that the government does not have enough policies and forums. P2 indicated,  
The government doesn’t have many policies or even forums to help us if 
we go to alternative funding options other than banking.  
170 
 
 
4.4.3. 
Using UK Laws to Guide Decision Making 
Quick and reactive Fintech regulations may hinder innovations. However, failure to impose 
rules may lead to innovation failure and associated negative consequences for investors and 
consumers. Fintech regulations are needed to minimise risk and necessary to educate 
customers to be knowledgeable of Fintech so that they are comfortable and safe while making 
transactions (Yahya, 2021). Therefore, Fintech policies and regulations provide a framework 
for upholding ethics and integrity that protects the concerns of both investors and consumers. 
Despite lacking comprehensive and specific Fintech regulations, the FCA regulatory sandbox 
guides Fintech operations in the United Kingdom (Everhart, 2020; Giglio, 2021). Participants 
also stated UK laws related to Fintech impact how they make Fintech financial decisions. For 
example, P10 argued that they follow laws about production insurance, anti-fraud, money 
laundering, and identity checks of buyers while using Fintech financing. P10 stated,  
Laws relating to product insurance, anti-fraud, money laundering, identity 
checks of buyers, and customer due diligence are in place to facilitate 
Fintech financing.  
In addition, P7 acknowledge the presence of financial market laws that guide how they make 
Fintech transactions. P7 supported his argument by stating,  
Yes, we have financial market laws and fair trading that help us to make 
Fintech transactions.  
Similar to P7, P8 reported that there are laws that guide making Fintech decisions by stating,  
 Yes, laws support general terms of doing business even if you use Fintech 
or not.  
P3 supported that theme that the UK laws facilitate Fintech financing by stating,  
Yes, UK laws facilitate Fintech finances, but I think a lot of work has to be 
done in this area for its development.  
171 
 
 
4.4.4. 
Negative Political and Economic Situations Necessitate 
Fintech Financing Options 
The coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic created financial and psychological distress 
in different sectors and economies (Kou, et al., 2021). In response to financial distress caused 
by COVID-19, technological innovations were integrated into financial systems to address 
some of the associated challenges. Another notable theme explaining how macro factors 
influence CEOs' Fintech decisions was that negative political and economic situations 
necessitate Fintech financing options. A hostile political situation identified by one of the 
participants was the impact of war on the economy. Other financial situations reported by the 
participants were the negative impacts of COVID-19 and inflation on the economy. For 
example, P11 SMEs lacked enough money to pay rent during the COVID-19 pandemic 
because the premises remained closed. Also, P11 argued that the lack of adequate support 
from the banks made them opt for Fintech financing. P11 gave the comprehensive response: 
 
Our premises remained closed during COVID-19, and we did not get 
enough finance to pay rent. We don’t have great support through traditional 
financing from banks. They don’t give money if you don’t make sales. I 
support emerging artists and illustrators. They know it is a difficult time for 
them to survive. Our team goes through their artwork. If we need money to 
order equipment for mass printing, then we will go for crowdfunding. 
In addition, P5 mentioned that increased inflation resulted in corresponding increases in 
banks' interest rates, and they had to make financial decisions by selecting options with fewer 
interest rates. Specifically, P5 considered seeking loans with low-interest rates from Fintech 
options because the banks offer high rates because of inflation. P5 supported his argument 
by stating,  
172 
 
Thinking inflation is very high so as the interest rate is very high on loan so 
all priority is not to use more debt in over company seeks funds through 
Fintech. 
Like P5 and P11, P7 acknowledged the financial difficulties associated with COVID-19 and 
inflation and indicated that he considers options with a low interest rate when making a 
financial decision. P7 stated: 
 
We are currently in a difficult financial situation due to COVID-19 and war 
effects; banks have more regulations and difficult lending criteria. If they 
lend us money, then it has a high interest rate. As a CEO, I have to consider 
all of them to make a suitable decision.  
Also, P1 acknowledged that economic situations, such as the impacts of COVID-19, influence 
how he makes Fintech financial decisions.  
We are still in the COVID-19 Pandemic, and I think it will help Fintech 
Market. My decisions are always influenced by factors such as COVID-19.  
Similar to P11, P2 indicated that Fintech financing options considering the current economic 
situation due to COVID-19. P2 stated,  
It was a feeling that COVID-19 was going to finish early 2020, but this is 
not the case, so I would say Fintech decisions are based on businesses 
situations and their need for money. 
 Additionally, P3 supported the theme by arguing that economic recessions, such as 2008 and 
the negative impacts of COVID-19, necessitated Fintech financing options. P3 stated,  
We learned out of the 2008 financial crush which gave birth to 
cryptocurrency, and the current COVID-19 circumstances surely will bring 
lots of opportunities for the development of Fintech.  
 
173 
 
4.4.5. 
Some Laws Impede Fintech Decision-Making 
Regulating all innovations in the financial sector has a limited advantage (Giglio, 2021). For 
example, preventive Fintech regulations increase regulatory agencies' workloads, discourage 
innovations, and are associated with limited benefits (Giglio, 2021). The inadequate and 
evolving European Union’s Fintech regulatory framework has resulted in regulatory 
fragmentation and friction. For example, the progress of Fintech regulation in European Union 
member states is slow and uneven. It poses a risk to investors and consumers because the 
associated risks are not fully mapped (Ahern, 2021). Some of the participants reported that 
there are some laws and regulations that impede making Fintech decisions. For example, P10 
mentioned that the government should not develop more rules and regulations for Fintech. 
P10 stated,  
The government should facilitate Fintech. They should not need to make 
more laws and regulations for Fintech. They should allow banks to work on 
Fintech models.  
Also, P7 supported the theme that the government should ensure fairness in laws and 
regulations guiding Fintech and traditional financing options. P7 stated,  
The government should allow Fintech companies to follow the same 
regulations they make for the financial market. That will create equal 
opportunities and a competitive market for us.  
P1 further supported the theme that government policies negatively impact how they make 
Fintech decisions. P1 stated,  
Yes, we have a legal department. All departments follow government 
policies, which severely affects my decisions.  
 
174 
 
4.4.6. 
Making Fintech Decisions to Align with the Emerging 
Technologies 
Fintech is driven by digitalisation and digitisation and is increasingly integrated into daily 
economic transactions (Mention, 2019). The current global technology trend necessitates 
financial organisations to focus on utilising digital systems to improve their services (Bhat, et 
al., 2023). Fintech is among the viable options in the digital landscape that incorporate 
disruptive technologies such as artificial intelligence and blockchain to improve financial 
services' effectiveness and efficiency. The emergence of new technologies and their potential 
to improve customer services compel financial institutions to integrate and align their financial 
decisions to emerging technologies. Another notable theme that supported the fourth research 
question was that CEOs make Fintech decisions to align with emerging technologies. For 
example, P12 acknowledged that digital technology and cryptocurrency would be crucial in 
the economic situation of a country, and they seized such opportunities. P12 stated,  
Digital technology and cryptocurrency are the future of the country. Our 
company, like others, will take advantage of cryptocurrency. Its valuation 
price is higher, it is secure to trade, and the exchange rate is high. 
P5 believe that positive innovations necessitate Fintech decisions in their company. P5 stated,  
Yes, the Innovation department continues to work to recommend 
innovation in the company. It depends on whether the innovation is related 
to the products, services, or a specific area. We accept innovation, but it is 
not my sole decision. I only recommend it to the board.  
P1 believes that they have to make financial decisions to align with emerging technologies to 
remain relevant and progressive in the market. P1 stated,  
I think we are quite fit with this digital age. As I mentioned, we have to 
remain current and progressive and know what the economy demands, 
which is life with the time.  
175 
 
P1 also supported the theme that the emerging changes brought about by technological 
advancement make it inevitable for businesses to accept the changes and reduce reliance on 
traditional financing options. P1 gave a comprehensive response: 
 
I feel technology is changing, new features are coming every day, and we 
are less reliant on traditional financing. We have to accept if something is 
new coming to us, who knows, that may change our life. Our company is 
very welcoming to new technology to support our financing decisions. We 
embedded new features in our payment methods like Stripe, Google 
Wallet, and Bitcoin. 
Also, P3 supported the theme that they make financial decisions to align with the emerging 
changes and advancement of technology.   
I think as a small SME we CEOs prefer Fintech financing. This also fits our 
vision of being a technological company to live with the present and future 
demands of changing technology. 
 
 
176 
 
5. Discussion and recommendation 
 
5.1. Discussion of Findings and Recommendation  
5.1.1. 
Research Question 1 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate 
their financing choices in the context of a rapidly evolving 
economic and financial technology environment? 
The themes and sub-themes developed in the first research question included (a) CEOs in 
the UK prefer Fintech to traditional financing, (b) Fintech provides more financing options, (c) 
Fintech is associated with lower costs, (d) Fintech is associated with lower regulations (e) 
Increasing demand and banking challenges necessitate Fintech financing options (f) limited 
awareness about Fintech options. 
 
5.1.1.1. 
Financing Preferences 
The preference for Fintech over traditional finance among the CEOs in the United Kingdom is 
based on the provision that it provides more financing options, fewer regulations and low cost. 
CEOs' financing preferences in their organisations depend on many reasons, such as their 
strategic choice (Ghemawat, 2006; Johnson, et al., 2020) or the associated benefits with 
traditional or Fintech financing for them to remain competitive in the business environment. It 
also depends on the organisations to accept innovation by responding to the change in their 
external financing environment, where they now have more funding options than a decade 
ago. These funding strategies (Shepherd et al., 2015) can be a switching (Trabucchi & 
Buganza, 2019) from traditional to Fintech financing or Fintech to traditional financing or 
finding the preferred substitution (Kivimaa et al., 2021) by evaluating the cost and benefits of 
financing options. In the current research, financing preferences for CEOs to accept Fintech 
are more related to their organisations’ strategic choice to accept innovation to take the 
benefits of Fintech financing due to technology. The advantage of blockchain technology is 
that it provides users with trust and influences their intentions due to the transparency and 
efficiency features of financial transactions. The benefits of simple and user-friendly 
177 
 
technology-enabled Fintech platforms make financial transactions between SMEs and 
investors more simple and easier through phones or laptops, which is considered cheaper 
than traditional financing methods, enabling flexibility and accessibility, that is, consuming less 
time and effort compared to traditional financing. Therefore, the findings are congruent with 
Pizzi et al. (2021), Alshareef & Tunio (2022), and Bag et al. (2023) that efficiency is one of the 
factors due to blockchain for Fintech financing. The use of blockchain technology in Fintech 
not only creates efficiency in funding transactions but also increases CEOs’ trust to prefer 
Fintech financing which is consistent with the findings of Lemieux & Lemieux (2016). In 
addition, not involving banks reduces many regulations and costs for SMEs’ access to the 
various Fintech options to access funds. Thus, the benefits associated with Fintech are the 
main factors that influence its preference compared to traditional financing.  
 
5.1.1.2. 
More funding options 
It is important to note that besides the potential benefits of accepting Fintech, UK SMEs have 
to safeguard the industry code of practices, laws, regulations, and related standards to protect 
their funding through Fintech. The findings revealed that most CEOs are aware of the 
challenges of FinTech and would still like to accept it. Technology-based funds through 
Fintech facilitate investors and a fund acquiring party to link P2P lending or cryptocurrency or 
through crowdfunding platforms. In these platforms, investors, either individuals or 
organisations, choose the project they wish to invest in. According to Kumari and Devi (2022), 
Fintech solutions are more equipped to provide quick access to reserve cash and loans to 
those with low or no income than banks and other lending institutions. Kickstarter, Indiegogo, 
Crowcube, Seedrs, GoFundme, Funding Circle, Syndicateroom, JustGiving, Lendinvest,
