Zoopa, FundRazr and Crowd for Angels are widely used crowdfunding platforms in the UK. 
SMEs have more options to choose the best to suit their funding needs. These Crowdfunding 
platforms also support funding to start-up businesses with no credit history but a sound project 
to provide a good return on investment. In contrast, banks do not support such lending criteria. 
Banks or financial institutions have strict lending criteria. In the case of P2P lending, noticeable 
178 
 
platforms are CapitalStackers, Loanpad, Assetz Exchange, AxiaFunder, Invest & Fund, 
Proplend, Kuflink, and Easy Money. These P2P funding platforms provide SME CEOs with 
options to take funds for specific purposes such as legal cases, property, short-term projects, 
etc. Therefore, CEOs have more options within the P2P platforms to choose the best funding 
options to meet their business needs. The technologically driven P2P platforms match SMEs 
and investors directly without the needs of banksLikewise, cryptocurrency platforms are 
limited but are also increasing in the UK; for example, crypto funds offer digital currency, coin 
shares, insulina, and nickel digital. Some of them, such as Nickel Digital, are regulated by the 
Financial Conduct Authority and offer secure and transparent access to crypto assets. The 
findings of the sub-theme of the first research question are consistent with the findings of 
Bollaert et al. (2021) and Haddad & Hornuf (2019) that FinTech provides more funding options.   
 
5.1.1.3. 
Lower financing cost 
The researcher found that the cost is a significant factor influencing consumers’ decision to 
adopt Fintech, and CEOs would prefer financing options at a lower cost. Price value is a 
consumer’s cognitive characteristic that influences their decisions based on the cost (Najib, et 
al., 2021). Fintech significantly contributes to the financial system by increasing customer 
satisfaction, ensuring quality services, and reducing costs (Kou et al., 2021). The low cost of 
financing through Fintech is because of many reasons. The main reason is that Fintech uses 
blockchain technology, which makes its transactions more convenient, affordable, and secure. 
Because it does not involve intermediaries such as banks or other payment processors, 
processing financial transactions is faster at reduced overhead costs. The other reason is that 
SMEs can access various funding options through Fintech without geographical restrictions. 
If conventional financing through banks is involved in these transactions, it also includes the 
cost of maintaining anti-money laundering and anti-terrorism as a regulatory burden. 
Therefore, financing through Fintech supports businesses and individuals through blockchain 
technology without cross-border restrictions, which is efficient, secure, and cheaper than 
traditional financial options. Furthermore, through Fintech, CEOs can access various financing 
179 
 
options such as P2P, Neobanks, Crowdfunding, and Cryptocurrency, where they can 
negotiate with investors to offer them funds at reduced rates. While at banks, due to the high 
cost of living and inflation, the interest rate is very high, which increases the cost of funding.   
The research findings are consistent with the outcome of Carlin et al. (2017), Najib et al. (2021) 
and Kumari & Devi (2022) that Fintech provides lower cost, which is one of the factors for 
SMEs' financing preference to accept Fintech financing. As per CEOs' views, the interest rate 
on conventional financing through banks is higher on loans. However, with more funding 
options via Fintech, they can get cheaper and more negotiable rates. The study results also 
support Ornelas & Pecora's (2022) findings that P2P lending offers lower interest rates than 
traditional banks. The feature of the lower interest rate by Fintech is evident from Elliehausen 
& Hannon (2023), who state that Fintech offers lower interest rates that reduce funding costs. 
The CEOs’ preference to accept Fintech to traditional financing has similar findings to the 
research report published by the European Central Bank, which confirmed that SMEs are 
actively switching to Fintech lending, which reduces their dependence on the banks. It reduces 
the cost of bank debt on SMEs (Eça, et al., 2022). The study findings are consistent with the 
IMF staff discussion notes that blockchain technology in Fintech does not involve banks, which 
results in cost reduction for that financial transaction (He, et al., 2017).  
 
 
5.1.1.4. 
Lower regulations 
The other reason that influences CEOs to accept FinTech is the discouraging approach by 
banks due to their regulation and strict lending criteria. Globally, regulators respond to rapidly 
evolving Fintech by implementing regulatory sandboxed regimes (Everhart, 2020). Regulatory 
sandboxes allow Fintech firms to test their services, products, or solutions in the market under 
more relaxed regulations within well-defined boundaries (Everhart, 2020). The regulatory 
sandbox approach enables Fintech companies to meet regulatory requirements while 
providing regulators opportunities to gain more understanding about financial services, 
products, and delivery channels that would allow them to enact Fintech regulations (Everhart, 
180 
 
2020). Regulatory sandboxes allow firms to safely test their business models, innovative 
products, delivery, or services without immediately accounting for all the regulatory 
consequences (Goo & Heo, 2020). The approach effectively deploys Fintech solutions that 
promote consumer confidence and protection. In the United Kingdom, the Financial Conduct 
Authority (FCA) sandbox is open to authorised firms, unauthorised firms that require 
authorisations, and technology businesses (Everhart, 2020). The FCA sandbox was 
established to determine whether delivering consumer benefits will be feasible while 
effectively managing the risks of Fintech solutions (Everhart, 2020). The United Kingdom was 
the first country to introduce a regulatory sandbox and has several competitive Fintech 
technologies and companies (Goo & Heo, 2020). Currently, the United Kingdom is considered 
one of the active Fintech markets (Goo & Heo, 2020). Traditional financing institutions such 
as banks and similar lenders are constrained by prudential regulatory requirements (Gao, 
2022). In the initial stages, there were minimal regulatory requirements for peer-to-peer 
lending networks in China and the United States. However, China's financial regulatory 
agencies have increased the oversight of Internet financing regulations due to the 
concentrated breakout of risk in the industry (Gao, 2022). In the United States, there is no 
specific regulatory framework for Fintech in State or federal regulations (Giglio, 2021). The 
company may be subject to laws and regulations in the United States depending on the 
Fintech assessment provided (Giglio, 2021). According to Jhariya et al. (2023), most of the 
Fintech companies in India are not entirely regulated. The European regulatory framework 
does not follow a specific Fintech approach. European Fintech regulatory approach is 
fragmented in encompassing different pieces of legislation about various topics related to 
digital banking (Hanten, et al., 2022). Although banking laws have been harmonised in the 
European Union to some extent, there are no harmonised regulations for digital banking 
(Hanten, et al., 2022). The European Banking Authority (EBA) developed a roadmap, Fintech 
knowledge hub, and Fintech priorities in 2018 (European Banking Authority, 2017). The goals 
of EBA regulations were to enhance consumer protection, promote sound, effective, and 
consistent regulations, and ensure transparency, integrity, and orderly functioning of financial 
181 
 
markets (European Banking Authority, 2017). Existing research by leading researchers such 
as Magnuson (2018), Alam, et al. (2019) and Chorzempa & Huang (2022) findings are 
consistent with the current study outcome that the Fintech has lower regulations and that also 
attracts borrowers to use wider Fintech funding options. Also, Banks’ discouraging approach 
is evident in the statistics that banks declined almost half a million UK SMEs’ credit 
applications, which is 9.3% of total SMEs (Brown, et al., 2018). Further, 70% of micro SMEs 
failed to secure traditional funds in their first attempt ( The Credit Protection Association, 
2019). SMEs' traditional way of obtaining loan rejection remained at 17% or higher each year 
from 2012 to 2018 (Statista Research Department, 2022).  
 
5.1.1.5. 
Increasing demand and banking challenges 
The increasing demands in financial services include infrastructural, digital connectivity, the 
introduction of new products, regulatory burdens, and evolving finance strategies (Feyen, et 
al., 2021). The theme of increasing demand and banking challenges necessitating Fintech 
financing options. The negative consequences of the last financial crisis and the emergence 
of new technologies created disruptive forces in the financial markets (Kliber, et al., 2021). 
The introduction of Fintech financing is beneficial in meeting the demand of novice enterprises 
due to the cost of licensing, cards, payments, and costly activities such as security 
underwriting and on-balance sheet lending (Feyen, et al., 2021). Fintech has reduced the 
regulatory burden associated with cross-border transactions by enabling digital banking using 
applications or methods such as PayPal or TransferWise (Feyen, et al., 2021). Cost and 
efficiency concerns have accelerated the adoption of Fintech as viable options to improve 
efficiency and optimise cost in the financial market (Omarini, 2020). 
Fintech addressed challenges associated with the traditional approaches by improving 
customer experience (Martinčević, et al., 2020). It creates and sustains a competitive 
advantage at reduced costs, manages risks, and enhances efficiency (Restoy, 2020). Fintech 
improves the efficiency of the traditional banking process by integrating technology. Fintech 
182 
 
financing enable infrastructural connectivity without a physical bank branch, thus reducing the 
burden of travelling from one area to another (Feyen, et al., 2021). Additionally, Fintech 
promotes connectivity through mobile technology that has increased efficiency and access at 
a lower cost. According to Ahern (2021), the COVID-19 pandemic increased the demand for 
digital financing services. Consumers suppressed their fears and concerns about Fintech to 
avoid the risk of COVID-19 infection, thereby improving the acceptance and adoption of 
Fintech financial options during the pandemic (Abdul-Rahim, et al., 2022). The onset of 
COVID-19 proved that people could gain technological literacy within a short time and forced 
them to use digital options to manage their finances (Setiawan, et al., 2021). Fintech solutions 
are adopted based on its ability to resolve the challenges associated with traditional methods. 
Fintech enhances the customer experience and helps to build and maintain a competitive 
advantage due to lower costs, risks, and high efficiency (Restoy, 2019; Martinčević et al., 
2020). FinTech aims to overcome issues with traditional approaches. The findings revealed 
that the perception of adopting a FinTech solution is based on its benefits compared to the 
conventional options. Razzaque and Hamdan’s (2020), Ahern's (2021), and Abdul-Rahim et 
al. (2022) findings were congruent with this study’s results, revealing that the perception of 
incorporating Fintech solutions is based on its benefits and advantages. Therefore, these 
results are considered when advocating the adoption of Fintech solutions. Fintech resolves 
some of the issues associated with traditional financing and meets the surging demand in the 
market. Thus, the benefits associated with Fintech solutions over conventional methods are 
used as compelling factors to adopt digital finance. Therefore, CEOs are more focused on 
emphasising the advantages of Fintech financing when compared with traditional funding 
methods. 
5.1.1.6. 
Limited Awareness 
The limited awareness about the Fintech financing options hindered its adoption by the UK 
CEOs; hence, they preferred using traditional financing choices. Low digital financial literacy 
and awareness make it difficult for entrepreneurs to transit from traditional to digital financial 
183 
 
options (Basar, et al., 2022). Basar et al. (2022) conducted a study to identify the extent of 
Fintech adoption and the level of digital financial literacy. Basar et al. (2022) found that 
awareness of digital financial risk did not significantly influence the adoption of Fintech among 
SME entrepreneurs. Also, Basar et al. (2022) found a negative association between the 
awareness of Fintech risk and adoption among SME entrepreneurs. Entrepreneurs who are 
aware of Fintech risks such as cyber security and online fraud risk might opt for i-Fintech 
(Basar, et al., 2022). Knowledge about Fintech, performance expectations, safety 
performance, facilitating conditions, and social influence were among the factors that influence 
the adoption of Fintech among small food business owners (Najib, et al., 2021).  
According to the Fintech Adoption Index, the adoption of Fintech doubles every two years 
globally (Lomachynska, 2020). In 2015, 16% of global consumers used one or more Fintech 
options, which increased to 33% in 2017 and 64% in 2019 (Lomachynska, 2020). In 2019, 
99% of consumers were aware of Fintech's financial options. Globally, there is a high level of 
consumer awareness about Fintech, with money transfer and payments leading (96%), 
followed by insurance (86%), saving and investment (78%), borrowing (76%), and financial 
budgeting and planning (71%) (Lomachynska, 2020). According to Lomachynska (2020), 
SMEs demonstrated a high Fintech adoption rate of 25%. 
The finding was similar to Basar et al. (2022), who discovered that limited literacy and 
awareness of digital financial services and options hampered entrepreneurs from adopting 
digital financing platforms. The literacy and awareness level of Fintech services influence the 
decision to adopt digital services. The study's finding is also similar to or perhaps consistent 
with the research by Das and Das (2020), who used surveys of 215 respondents to understand 
the adoption of Fintech by bank customers. They found the considerable benefits of FinTech, 
such as being user-friendly, low cost, quick, and less risky; however, they found that adoption 
and the use of Fintech among bank customers is very low due to limited awareness (Das & 
Das, 2020). Therefore, these findings suggest that increasing CEOs' awareness of Fintech 
services can result in a surge in the adoption of digital finance services. According to Niu et 
184 
 
al. (2020), an increase in the education level results in a rise in the adoption of Fintech services 
such as mobile banking and digital banking, credit, and wealth management. Literacy 
workshops can increase awareness of the benefits of using technology-enabled FinTech 
funding (Das & Das, 2020). Companies offering financing through Fintech can conduct training 
and awareness campaigns on the benefits of digital financing and how to apply it to increase 
the chances of adoption. These FinTech companies can improve their sustainable practices 
for SMEs to ensure their clients are aware of data protection measures, compliance with laws, 
privacy issues, and secure lending that will raise the customers’ awareness of using Fintech 
(Global Lancers, 2023). The interesting result of this research is that most CEOs with 
considerable experience using Fintech. However, more awareness-related initiatives are 
required to spread Fintech funding.  
5.1.2. 
Research Question 2 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs perceive 
the influence of micro-level factors, such as personal 
leadership traits, vision and risk-taking attitude on their 
Fintech financing decisions? 
The themes obtained in the second research questions include (a) leadership traits (b) using 
vision to guide Fintech decisions (c) using values to guide decision-making, (d) incorporating 
family members’ perspectives in making Fintech decisions, (e) using personal beliefs to 
reinforce Fintech decision-making, (f) use educational knowledge to improve Fintech decision 
making, and (g) use prior experience to improve Fintech decision making (h) risk approach to 
making FinTech decision.  
5.1.2.1. 
Leadership traits 
The findings suggest that at the micro level, CEOs are very conscious about their personal 
leadership. They consider that personal leadership help to promote their organisation 
partnership for the business expansion that in return increases their organisational turnover to 
remain financially healthy. CEOs consider that personal leadership is significant to make 
decisions and it is linked to ethics, morality, dedication, goal-oriented vision, power, 
accountability, and integrity. Results are consistent with the findings of Waldman, et al. (2001), 
185 
 
Simsek, et al., (2010), Kaplan, et al. (2012), Chen, et al. (2013), Haas & Speckbacher (2017), 
Huang, et al. (2019), and Ibarra et al. (2020). Such leadership traits are associated with the 
transformational leadership styles because majority of CEOs are focus on the long-term vision 
to achieve the organisational goals, built trust in their team, engage with them, focus on the 
accountability as well listen others for the promotion of creativity and innovation in their 
organisation. Innovation in this case is the adoption of FinTech.  
These CEOs also consider that personal leadership traits enable them to make innovation 
decisions – a characteristic of the transformational leaders. These decisions lead their 
organisation to achieve sustainability with innovation in the competitive market. According to 
CEOs, the personal leadership skills are linked to their communication skills to inspire others 
and foster a culture of creativity among team to achieve their organisational visions. CEOs 
personal leadership skills help them to lead for the better sound financial decisions. For 
example, these leadership attributes as discussed before enable them to make better 
financing choices as to where they should fund their business operations. CEOs work for the 
development of leadership skills, they emphasise on technology, gaining knowledge through 
training and formal education to make good decisions and also lead their team. They also 
work for the improvement of their communication skills which is believed to motivate their team 
and sub-ordinates to achieve their daily tasks. CEOs also think that task-oriented goals, 
respecting others, and caring for their clients are possible ingredients of their personal 
leadership. When it comes to Fintech financing, attributes such as trust, listening to their 
people in their personal and professional networks and a good vision contribute to their 
financing decisions. Due to these reasons, they work for the development of their leadership 
skills to make good financial judgements.  
As CEOs’ opinions suggest, personal leadership is significant to making FinTech decisions. 
Therefore, it is advised and recommended that they continue to develop their leadership skills 
through professional development training. Professional development courses are not formal 
qualifications, they are designed for managers and CEOs to update their professional 
development and by taking a training, they can enhance their decisions making in FinTech 
186 
 
financing. Professional development training boosts the confidence of individuals, prepares 
CEOs to perform their responsibilities with confidence, and further helps them to understand 
challenges in Fintech financing. The factor that can’t be ignored is that the majority of CEOs, 
at least 70% of the sample SMEs, prefer FinTech financing that is because of the benefits of 
FinTech to traditional. However, the personal leadership to make FinTech decisions require 
considerable skills to make the right choice. Therefore, leadership training in the perspective 
of financial decisions, especially in FinTech is beneficial for CEOs to make the right choice.    
  
5.1.2.2. 
Vision  
At the micro level, the vision is a factor for CEOs which they believe to guides them in their 
FinTech decision making. Evidence from the sample statistics suggest that 95% of CEOs 
consider their vision have a significant impact on their decision making. Vision influence CEOs 
directly or indirectly to accept Fintech or traditional financing. When it comes to the direct 
influence, CEOs take several steps to visualise the future demand and benefits of the Fintech 
financing and make decisions. Indirect influence happens when they work in the Board of their 
organisations and other decisions makers influence them. In this perspective, they consider 
vision is related to their organisational directions and make financial decisions to achieve the 
strategic vision of organisations. That is also related to their transformational leadership style 
(Bass & Riggio, 2006).  5% CEOs consider that they have personal vision, however, their 
vision has no link to their companies’ vision and therefore it does not influence them. Vision 
or visionary CEOs or the organisational vision are terms that are often used in the business 
literature but rarely understood or applied consistently. These terms are used in the context of 
the current research which is relating to the factors that influence CEOs to make financing 
decisions. An organisational vision is generally a written document or a statement to clarify its 
stakeholders about what the organisation wants to be in the future (Hoy, 2023). It also 
describes the long term plans or objectives or directions (CFI, 2023). The personal vision is 
direction to achieve the short or long goals (Davenport, 2023). According to Berg (2015), the 
187 
 
personal goals and purpose of the senior managers to the organisational vision confirms the 
success of the organisation if senior managers articulate their goals to the organisational 
vision. The findings confirm that CEOs have powers to make bold decisions, work as visionary 
leaders, empower team, and make decisions with wisdom, dedication and integrity to remain 
competitive with innovative strategies. However, their decisions are based on the 
organisational priority to seek funds. The results are consistent with the findings of Nwokedi 
(2015), Collins (2020), Slåtten et al. (2021), Maran et al., (2022), Yuanyuan et al. (2022) and 
Ji et al. (2023).  
CEOs visionary characteristics are more associated with the factors in the transformational 
leaders where they look at the bigger picture to making financing decisions. Having a long-
term personal vision is a notable factor for the CEOs especially when it comes to their decision 
making. CEOs with visions of integrating technology to improve a firm’s efficiency and 
competitive advantages prefer using Fintech financing options. CEOs present a clear vision 
for digital transformation, showcase company leadership, and develop their business 
strategies (Kim & Kim, 2022). CEOs with strategic mind-sets are highly demanded at top levels 
of business organisations which contribute to the long-term success of the firms (Korherr, et 
al., 2022). CEOs should keep their personal vision forefront of what they do and that should 
be consistent and integrated with the organisational vision. CEOs are recommended to align 
their vision to shape, lead, motivate and engage with other stakeholders to make the better 
financial decisions. That will also give CEOs confidence that the success of the organisation 
has direct impact on their career related vision. Thus, vision is a vital aspect that CEOs should 
possess and can be used to guide Fintech decisions. 
 
5.1.2.3. 
Values 
The current study statistics confirm that 80% CEOs consider their values as guidance to make 
Fintech financing decisions. They consider that having values has positive impact on their 
organisational performance for Fintech related decisions. CEOs provided various definitions 
188 
 
of values, some of them were considering values at the personal level. CEOs think that their 
values are very personal and consider values to be inbuilt features inherited from their 
background, family, or perhaps religion, and they do not relate to their financing decisions. It 
was noted that many CEOs think out of box to distinguish their personal values to the 
organisational values. These CEOs were more focused on their organisational values, which 
provide a code of conduct and general principles for performing their jobs. In other words, 
CEOs think their organisational values guide their personal and business-related matters. 
This result is consistent with the research outcomes of Vitt (2004), Berson et al. (2008), 
Gorgievski et al. (2011), and Lofving et al. (2023), which concluded that CEOs' leadership 
styles are guided by their values and positively contribute to their organisations’ success. The 
findings are also consistent with the results of Ryu (2018), Yang & Lee (2019) and Gannon 
and Hieker (2022) that values are a significant factor for innovation, attracting investors to fund 
the business and individual decision making. CEOs' decisions are not limited to Fintech 
financing decisions but also to other matters that contribute to the long-term success of their 
organisations. In these perspectives, when it comes to values, CEOs are recommended to 
consider aligning their personal values with organisational values to guide their professional 
decision-making in their organisations. Most CEOs work in the board level of their organisation 
to make collective decision making. Aligning personal values to the organisational values will 
enable CEOs to work for the common organisational goals that will ultimately promote the 
healthy working environment to promote stakeholders’ interest in the organisation.  
 
5.1.2.4. 
Family members 
The research's findings revealed that the majority of CEOs’ decisions regarding Fintech 
financing are influenced by their family members. When it comes to the major decisions 
making in the organisation, CEOs require confidence and emotional support and due to those 
reasons, they consult such important matters with the family members. Discussion with the 
family not only provide them emotional support, but also help CEOs to feel confident that their 
189 
 
family stands by their major decisions in the workplace. Discussions with the families add self-
confidence to the CEOs' Fintech decision-making, and such consultations give them direction 
and guidelines for making confident decisions. Even though the empirical evidence (Anderson 
& Reeb, 2003; Chrisman et al., 2004; Jonathan, 2021; Buchanan et al., 2022; Irdiana et al., 
2022) confirm that families involvement has a significant impact and the role in business and 
decision making. It was not known whether they could contribute to CEOs' decision-making 
regarding their SMEs’ financing options. 
When the CEOs were asked to confirm whether the family's discussion or contribution 
influenced their Fintech decisions, 65% of participants responded positively, while 35% denied 
the family's influence on their decisions. The finding confirms that inputs from the family are 
useful advice and mostly related to emotional support, which contributes to CEOs’ decisions 
regarding Fintech financing. The study also reveals that CEOs who don’t discuss professional 
matters with their family have issues of work privacy, where they are bound not to share 
financing matters. The other issue is the conflict of interest of not including those people who 
are not a part of the organisation in the major decisions.     
 
5.1.2.5. 
Personal beliefs 
The finding shows that CEOs refer to their personal beliefs as opinions, perceptions, or even 
ideas about which they have faith to make decisions. CEOs were asked to confirm if their 
personal beliefs affect their decisions, the statistics confirm that 95% (n=19) believe that their 
personal beliefs contribute to their Fintech financing decisions while only 5% participants 
denied. The follow up questions confirm that CEOs have faiths that their decisions are dynamic 
– it means they change them if they have the perception that the outcome of their decision is 
not in their favour or make decision if it gives them a good value of money or a hope that their 
decisions would favour their organisation to involve in the ethical or moral transaction of 
funding. It further implied that they look for the risk of their decisions which influence their 
financing decisions. These risks could be the financial or operational risks. For example, the 
190 
 
risks are particularly concerned with regulations and security issues, monetary losses and 
fraudulent activity, and the manner in which funding by FinTech companies respond when a 
financial loss or financial information is disclosed. The findings at the micro level are congruent 
with Baxamusa & Jalal (2016) that the religious beliefs have effects on the human decision 
making. However, these are consistent with the findings of Setiawan et al. (2021), 
Mascarenhas et al. (2021), Abdul-Rahim et al. (2022), Gupta et al. (2023) that the personal 
beliefs of the consumers based on trust including perceived risks and benefits to affect the 
perception and intention to accept FinTech solutions.  
Therefore, based on the trust to enhance CEOs’ belief on Fintech, it can be inferred that 
appealing to the trust is important to enhance their personal belief in the adoption of FinTech. 
Elements of safety, privacy, regulation, ethics and morals, and authenticity in digital solutions 
enhance their confidence to use Fintech funding options. Therefore, the findings suggest that 
the individuals involved in FinTech actively evaluate and resolve the perceived risks while 
enhancing the perceived benefits of SMEs. However, to increase CEOs' belief and their trust 
in Fintech financing, Fintech funding firms should use different strategies to attract SMEs 
towards FinTech financing. These strategies should link to literature for CEOs’ information that 
funding through Fintech is less risky, safe, secure and more beneficial than traditional sources 
of funds. These companies can arrange workshops to train and sensitise their existing and 
potential clients on their services and funding options.  
 
5.1.2.6. 
Education 
Education is a crucial factor for CEOs to make professional decisions. Through education, 
CEOs acquire domain-specific knowledge and skills to make decisions in their respective 
professions. The first question was if education contributes to CEOs' Fintech financing 
decisions. The sample statistics confirm that the 95% (n=19) believe that the education is a 
significant factor to positively influence their Fintech financing decisions. 5% (n=1) contradict 
with the education factor to contribution in their Fintech decisions. The result of this research 
191 
 
produces valuable knowledge that higher knowledge through formal or informal vocational or 
professional development education contributes positively to CEOs' making Fintech decisions. 
Expertise improves the capacity to identify patterns, assess risks, and generate innovative 
solutions. Education impacts CEOs' decision-making as well. Formal education equips 
individuals with foundational knowledge, critical thinking skills, and broader comprehension of 
their field, allowing them to make skilled decisions. In professional contexts, higher levels of 
education develop their critical skills in their complex decision-making. The sample of this 
research includes CEOs of SMEs who are highly qualified people and 90% holds higher level 
degrees while others substantial experience.  
The results of this micro level are consistent with the findings of Norburn & Birley (1988), 
Garnier (1982), Hambrick (2007), Xiaowei & Zhang (2010), Soriano & Castrogiovanni (2012) 
and Herdinata, et al. (2019). Education level of CEOs helps them to evaluate all alternative 
funding options, bring innovation in the organisation, and enable CEOs to understand 
regulations, cost and benefits, advantages and disadvantages as well as improve their 
decision making. The higher level of expertise through education also increases CEOs 
productivity in the organisation.  
 
5.1.2.7. 
Experience 
Experience is an additional crucial factor in professional decision-making. CEOs acquire tacit 
knowledge and develop intuition through experience, enabling them to make quick and 
accurate decisions. In this study the 95% CEOs confirm that experience is vital and a 
significant factor to contribute in their Fintech financing decisions while 5% CEOs have 
opposite views. Their experience includes the knowledge of bank loans and the knowledge of 
block chain and the Fintech funding platforms. In the findings of the current study regarding 
experience at the micro level of CEOs to influence their Fintech financing decisions are 
consistent with Xiaowei & Zhang (2010), Moritz, et al. (2015), Zheng & Piao (2019), Mohd 
Thas Thaker (2019) and Duréndez, et al. (2023) that experience is vital to make good 
192 
 
decisions. Experience enables CEOs to recognise the best possible funding solutions and 
anticipate potential outcomes of FinTech, thereby enhancing their decision-making abilities. 
As Fintech is an emerging field in the financial sector, CEOs’ experiences increase with time, 
enabling them to make good funding choices. This is because 35% of the current sample's 
CEOs have limited knowledge of using Fintech, but all of them are experienced CEOs with 
years in their current roles. In addition, there is significant gaps of regarding Fintech 
knowledge. 50% of CEOs have little knowledge of Fintech, and they are seeking ways to 
improve them. To bridge this gap, professional bodies, education institutes and online learning 
providers can develop training or even degrees to complement knowledge. These training can 
also include practical elements to enhance CEOs’ experience. As a researcher, I would also 
suggest that there should be an independent body to validate each crowdfunding, P2P, and 
Crypto platform in the UK so CEOs will have high confidence in using them. Such an 
independent body should also work in collaboration with education providers to develop 
knowledge and skills-based training for investors, borrowers, and CEOs.  
 
5.1.2.8. 
Risk attitude 
The risk attitude is a micro factor to influence CEOs’ decision making. As per the findings of 
this research, the risk taking attitude is linked to CEOs’ beliefs and personalities and they think 
that the riskier decisions have greater returns and more benefits. According to CEOs, FinTech 
market is volatile, uncertain, and unpredicted. The sudden and unexpected change in the 
cryptocurrency is due to factors of the demand and supply, government support, investors’ 
attitude, and risk factors associated with it (Koutmos, 2020; Delfabbro, et al., 2021; Smutny, 
et al., 2021; Khan & Hakami, 2022). Because the Fintech financing is comparatively new as 
compared to the traditional financial market (Ryabova, 2015; Gulamhuseinwala, et al., 2015; 
Moro-Visconti & Moro-Visconti, 2021). These are the main reasons for CEOs to have a risk 
avoider approach towards FinTech financing.  
193 
 
While taking into the account the findings of existing research that younger people are more 
risk taker (Jianakoplos & Bernasek, 2006; Steinberg, 2007; Mitsopoulou, 2019; Breivik, et al., 
2020; Gunier, et al., 2022), the outcome of the current study produce opposite results that 
middle-aged CEOs are more risk taker than young CEOs because they have more experience 
of using Fintech. The level of experience is relating to the confidence which enable them to 
make innovative (Galasso & Simcoe, 2011; Berson, et al., 2008) and rational decisions (Gray 
& Carlisle, 2013). The findings are also similar to the results of Melović, et al. (2022) that 
experienced entrepreneurs are more risk takers to make rational decisions. A risk-taking 
attitude is a personality trait of CEOs; the literature shows that a risk-taking attitude positively 
impacts the company with higher returns (Galasso & Simcoe, 2011; Cain & McKeon, 2016; 
Bean, 2018). In addition, the risk-taking attitude of individuals is also related to their 
transformational leadership styles (Wansink, et al., 2008; Konorti & Eng, 2008; Gaan & Gaan, 
2014; Alqatawenh, 2018; Buchholtz, 2021).  
These findings suggest that CEOs’ micro factors include their leadership traits, values, 
personal beliefs, education, experience, family and the risk attitude. These micro factors 
influence CEOs’ decisions making relating to Fintech financing. FinTech companies should 
consider the main challenges associated with the above factors when encouraging and 
persuading CEOs to use FinTech funding options. CEOs in SMEs make decisions and 
evaluate all possible sources of evidence before they reach Fintech-related decisions that 
include their long-term visions, personal beliefs, expertise and support from the family. Their 
leadership style is more transformational due to their innovative approach and risk-taking 
attitude. Additionally, the findings revealed that earning the trust in technology e.g. Fintech 
increase beliefs and confidence to enhance the positive perceptions about FinTech. Thus, 
factors that affect trust, such as privacy, ethics, morals, security, fraudulent activities, and 
monetary losses during financing transactions, should be addressed and considered to 
enhance CEOs' confidence in adopting Fintech. Addressing these issues reduces risks 
associated with FinTech and helps to improve CEOs' perceptions of being able to use FinTech 
financing with confidence.   
194 
 
 
5.1.3. 
Research Question 3 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs make their 
decisions on Fintech financing through professional 
networking, board of directors and organisational structure 
contexts at the meso level? 
 
The third research question was addressed to explore the influence of meso factors such as 
professional networking, board of directors, and organisational structure in CEOs’ Fintech 
financing decisions at the UK SMEs. The themes that emerged were incorporating advice and 
contributions from other professionals, consulting with the board while making Fintech 
decisions and the effects of the organisational structure to make Fintech decisions.  
 
5.1.3.1. 
Professional networking 
Professional Networking in the finance industry is important in improving the adoption of digital 
and Fintech solutions due to their feedback and contributions. The results of the current study 
confirm professional networks influence 85% of CEOs in their Fintech financing decisions, 
while 15% of CEOs have the perceptions that there is no contribution of their professional 
networks in their financing preferences. Professional Networking helps to increase awareness 
of Fintech solutions as well as provide contribution among peers and professionals in a 
dynamic business environment that can be incorporated to enhance its outcome. According 
to Tajeddini et al. (2020), the networking fosters strong communication that is associated with 
developing and sharing novice ideas and insights, as well as effective communication and 
collaboration. Strong relations are also associated with improving innovation and balancing 
business operations, resulting in strategic and effective developments based on a web of 
actors and activities (Tajeddini, et al., 2020). These results suggest that networking platforms, 
including investors from the finance and technology sectors, should be conducted regularly to 
provide feedback and make contributions to Fintech. The finding of the research confirm that 
the most CEOs believe the positively contribution of their professional networking in their 
195 
 
Fintech financing decisions. This is consistent with the research by Xiaowei & Zhang (2010), 
Wang & Tu (2016), and Eze, et al. (2018) that professional networking helps CEOs to seek 
knowledge through opinions and that also help them to make decisions. The findings suggest 
the importance of networking in promoting a FinTech through providing feedback, interactions, 
effective communication, and peer referrals.  
Additionally, CEOs also have professional networking with their organisational staff to build a 
strong organisational culture. Organisation culture is made up with the group of people who 
share their norms, beliefs, understandings and values in line with the organisation policies and 
rules, interact with each other’s and with their external environment (Aquinas, 2010). 
Organisational culture exists within the internal business environment (Janićijević, 2013) 
where people share their thoughts, attitudes and learn from each other’s behaviour to solve 
complex business problems (Schein, 2017). People’ own ideas, feelings and attitudes are 
guided by shared cultural values and norms (Alvesson, 2013) and they work collectively to 
deal with the organisational external environment (Schein, 2017). CEOs know the credentials 
of all individuals working for their organisations such as background, social networking, 
education and other situational factors on behaviour (Kondalkar, 2007) that help them to 
develop their professional network with similar interests. This helps them to act as a leader to 
direct their subordinates’ ideas and understanding while promoting ethical culture in the 
organisation. Therefore, networking within the organisation is highly significant of how SMEs 
operate in the competitive business environment and how their CEOs interact with their 
employees (Alvesson, 2013) and external competitors, suppliers, and customers (Daft, 2008) 
which ultimately influence their Fintech financing decisions. Therefore, creating strong 
relations promote innovation in the business which is facilitated by networking that can propel 
the adoption of Fintech. Hence, organisations which provides funding through FinTech should 
prepare and conduct workshops and seminars for stakeholders, investors, business owners, 
partners, clients, and customers in the finance and technology industry to interact and provide 
feedback on digital options and their benefits and advantages. In addition, networking forums 
196 
 
should be conducted often to increase new customers and ensure the continued use of 
existing clients. 
 
5.1.3.2. 
Board  
The second theme was consulting with the board members while making the Fintech 
decisions. All CEOs in this project confirmed that they have a board where non-executive and 
executive members play a major role, and that affects their decisions. That results in their 
collective decisions to make Fintech financing decisions. Having a board in the SMEs leads 
to corporate governance. Corporate Governance enables and protect all shareholders interest 
and safeguard proper conduct within the organisation. Effective governance for SMEs is 
crucial for their success, competence, sustainability, and growth in the competitive business 
environment (International Finance Corporation, 2019). The need for Corporate Governance 
has increased tremendously in the past decade because of the collapses of corporate 
organisations worldwide. This collapse generated much focus to other issues of Corporate 
Governance. The role of the Board is a combination of skilled people selected via voting or 
nomination based on their credentials, cultural knowledge, age, gender and others. The Board 
leads the organisation to success and ensures that there is risk management in decision 
making. The major strategic decisions for their organisation are led by the chairman on the 
board. The results of this study are somehow consistent with the findings of Gurdgiev and Ni 
(2023) that the diverse board plays an important role to moderate CEOs’ financing decisions. 
Further, results are also consistent with research by Croci, et al. (2011) and Xiang, et al. (2018) 
that CEOs of micro SMEs avoid Fintech adoption due to their size and high risk, while CEOs 
of large, financially healthy SMEs with effective governance accept Fintech financing 
decisions. Additionally, the results are consistent with the findings of Abor and Biekpe (2006) 
that the corporate governance structures influence the financing decisions in the SMEs. 
Having the board in the SMEs overall reduces the risk to make the wrong decisions. The 
diverse board with independent experts as non-executive directors and skills executive 
197 
 
directors manage the best decisions to meet the organisational long-term strategy. The 
diverse board with expertise also improves the organisational performance. It is advised that 
the SMEs board should have a clear composition of the board members with job 
responsibilities. They should have policies, procedures and systems in place for the flow of 
information from the CEO to the board, decision making tools and also a conflict of interest 
system in place to ensure the integrity, validity and unbiasedness in the financial decision 
making to meet their stakeholders’ interest. The Board role is to negotiate, protect and 
represent the shareholders interest in the organisation through accountability, control, 
strategic direction, and transparency while ensuring that they can contribute to the major 
decisions including FinTech. The findings confirm that CEOs consult with their respective 
boards while making FinTech-related decisions and protecting the interests of their 
shareholders. The findings also conclude that shareholders appoint the board for good 
governance and that the board oversees and ensures that CEOs' FinTech decisions benefit 
shareholders. However, having the board in the SME also increases the challenges of 
mitigating the conflict of interest and incurs agency costs.   
 
5.1.3.3. 
Organisational structure 
The third theme at the meso level was the effects of the organisational structure on the flow 
of information within the organisation, which is crucial for CEOs to make Fintech decisions. 
The study reveals that the different organisational structure exists in the SMEs regardless of 
their size. Most of the work in these SMEs are collective and that is led by CEOs. The study 
further reveals that the flow of information from different organisational structures affects 
CEOs’ Fintech decisions and organisations’ ability to accept change. The results are 
consistent with the study by Chehade and Vigneron (2007) findings that the complex 
organisational structures SMEs face more credit restaints. That is because of the 
decentralised organiational structure. The result of this study confirm that the organisational 
structure with many levels or departments have barriers for CEOs to make Fintech decisions 
198 
 
and further, the level for departments within the organiation reflect that the organisational 
structure is complex. Also, if the structure is considered as complex, it create any barriers for 
affects CEOs to make timely fintech decisions. However, it influences CEOs’ FinTech-related 
decision-making. These results are consistent to the study by Worthy’s in 1950 as cited in 
(Carzo & Yanouzas, 1969) that decisions of CEOs are less productive if the organisational 
structure is complex and based on number of departments.  
The outcome of the study at the meso level of the organisational structure align with the theory 
of the organic structure. Because Fintech is the financial innovation and that the organic 
organisational structure SMEs promote innovation (Saleh & Wang, 1993; Hage, 1999; 
Jenssen & Randøy, 2002; Camison & Villar‐López, 2012; Gaspary, et al., 2020). The less 
barriers in the organisational structure enable CEOs to make innovative and timely Fintech 
decisions which is consistent with the findings of Chaston (1997), Bai et al. (2017) and Su et 
al. (2019). The study further reveals that the use of software such as CRM, daily meeting with 
staff, and effective networking are main factors in the organisational structure to assist CEOs 
to have timely information for their Fintech related decisions.  
 
5.1.4. 
Research Question 4 - How do CEOs of UK SMEs navigate 
the complexities of macro-level factors, including 
government support and laws, when making Fintech 
financing decisions? 
The fourth research question was used to explore the complexity of macro factors that 
navigate CEOs making Fintech financing decisions at SMEs. The themes that emerged 
included (a) aligning Fintech decisions to government policies, (b) insufficient information and 
policies about Fintech limits decision making, (c) using UK laws to guide decision making, (d) 
negative political and economic situations necessitate Fintech options, (e) some laws impede 
Fintech decision-making, and (f) making Fintech decisions to align with the emerging 
technologies.  
199 
 
The findings align with Dwivedi et al. (2021) and Ng and Pan (2022), who indicated that 
aligning the Fintech solutions with governmental regulations and policies was a determining 
issue in its adoption. Therefore, Fintech solutions should align with governmental regulations 
and policies, which will help increase their popularity among CEOs. Excessive regulations 
negatively impact on innovation and the growth of Fintech (Goo and Heo, 2020; Zarrouk et al., 
2021; Ahern, 2021). The government support is necessary (Yee‐Loong Chong et al., 2010; 
Kiwanuka, 2015; Hu et al., 2019; Owen et al., 2019) in building trust (Marakarkandy et al., 
2017; Rosavina et al., 2019; Rosavina & Rahadi, 2018) and awareness (Ghazali & Yasuoka, 
2018) for the fintech adoption. Comprehensive, standardised, and consistent Fintech policies 
and regulations could improve CEOs' Fintech financing decisions. The findings confirm that 
UK laws, regulations, and government policies support Fintech. 
However, there is no particular Fintech legislation and a particular regulatory framework to 
govern FinTech in the UK. That means that the Fintech sector is liable to follow the established 
legislations (not an exhaustive list) such as anti-money laundering, anti-fraud, know your 
customer, counter- terrorism financing, data protection and UK GDRP, and Computer misuse 
act. Takeda and Ito (2021) found that aligning Fintech with regulations and policies is important 
in promoting its effectiveness, increasing its acceptability in society, and value addition. 
Hence, FinTech solutions should collaborate with governmental institutions to align their 
policies with governmental regulations and ensure that they address all issues with regard to 
privacy, possible threats, and customer protection (Takeda & Ito, 2021). The results indicate 
that the majority of CEOs expect a substantial government support on Fintech, particularly 
relating the information to support their Fintech financing decisions. The government are 
recommended to establish a financial innovation hub to promote UK Fintech.  
The research's findings suggest CEOs are concerned about the tight financial credit terms 
due to the high interest rate and the current negative political and economic crises. The 
economic crises in the UK are getting worst due to the high inflation which is currently 6.9%, 
the highest since 1992 (Office for National Statistics , 2023) and that also increased the cost 
200 
 
of living due to the fall of 4.3% fall of the household income, the highest since 1956 (Parliment, 
2023). The political and economic crises severally affect SMEs cash flow due to Covid-19 and 
fallout from Russian war with Ukraine (OECD , 2023). To cater with these negative effects and 
for CEOs to make the better financial decisions, the banking sectors are advised to establish 
a partnership with Fintech companies to design innovation applications. These applications 
should have transparent and accessible features to categories income and expenses for better 
budgeting and cheaper access to the financial credits.  
The findings obtained revealed that the benefits and abilities of the FinTech options to address 
the demerits of traditional methods can be regarded as determining factors in the perception 
and adoption of digital solutions. Therefore, it is important to ensure that the FinTech solutions 
are superior to the current traditional methods and can resolve existing and emerging issues 
in the finance sector. Companies that provide FinTech funding solutions to SMEs should 
adhere to and meet the legislative regulations and policies within the country to increase the 
possibility of adoption and application among SMEs. CEOs consider FinTech-related funding 
options if the country's regulations support them. Therefore, FinTech service providers should 
ensure that their respective countries' legislative requirements are met and observed in every 
stage of the digital options, from development to dissemination to the market and their clients. 
Based on the findings obtained in the study on the factors that affect the perception of the 
CEOs in adopting and using FinTech financing, a detailed conceptual framework or model is 
developed to guide CEOs’ decision-making on Fintech financing. This study emphasised how 
factors at the micro, meso and macro interact to affect intent to utilise FinTech as financial 
solutions for their organisations. CEOs make decisions for their financing preferences whether 
to go for the traditional financing options or Fintech. Their decisions are based on their 
perceptions and factors to influence their decisions to what portion of their organisation should 
be financed by the debt or equity or the combination of both. CEOs’ decisions making is based 
on  different decision making theories such as rational models, bounded rationality models, 
naturalistic decisions, the casual decision, game, evidential decision, Bayesian theories and 
the cogitative decision making models to make operations or strategic decisions. The decision-
201 
 
making theories and models guide their decisions and factors such as micro (personal 
leadership, vision, values, education, personal beliefs, and risk taking attitude), meso 
(networking, governance within the organisation and the organisational structure) and macro 
(government and laws) contribute in their financing decisions. Therefore, the model used in 
the study is multi-layered, and it examines their factors to determine risk, benefit, and trust to 
make their financing preferences. The study reveals that CEOs have transformational 
leadership styles. They work for the best interests of their shareholders, work collectively on 
the board and in the organic structure of their organisation, promote innovation through their 
decisions, and obtain funding through FinTech as preferred financing for their SMEs. However, 
issues relating to trust, confidentiality, privacy, cost, awareness and regulations should be 
addressed by the government to enhance CEOs' confidence to accept FinTech. 
 
 
5.2. Conceptual Framework 
Besides the main findings, the data adds three more factors to the research framework 
developed in the literature review that significantly navigates CEOs in their decision-making 
regarding Fintech financing. These factors are age and gender at the micro level and the role 
of the chairman in the board of directors at the meso level. 
 
5.2.1. 
Chairman 
The chairman is the position in the organisation which is specifically related to the Board 
(Lechem, 2003; Kakabadse & Kakabadse, 2007; O’Higgins, 2009; Liao, et al., 2015; Naciti, 
2019) to provide leadership (Institute of Directors, 2017; Harper, 2007; Pettigrew & McNulty, 
2019; Corporate Governance Institute, 2023). The chairman is usually elected by the board, 
while the shareholders hire the CEO to oversee the management (Croci, 2018; Graham et al., 
2020; Chatjuthamard et al., 2022). Having a board in the organisation is specifically related to 
achieving good governance (Tricker, 2015; Mallin, 2016; Baysinger & Butler, 2019). The 
202 
 
theme that emerged during the analysis was the factor of the chairman on the board at the 
meso level contributing to the CEO’s fintech financing decisions. For example, P2 highlighted 
the position of chairman on the board 
We all work collectively when we have to make decisions such as Fintech 
finances. We talk to each other, we have a lot of information from 
management, we use scenarios based on decisions making tools, we 
evaluate risks, and then our chairman makes decisions. 
According to P2, their SME has an independent board that depends on management to seek 
information relating to Fintech financing. Further, they work collectively on the board. That 
means the CEO is also part of the board. The board members work collectively and use 
decision-making tools, which could be based on the information they receive from their 
management regarding traditional or fintech financing. The more important part is that their 
chairman makes the final decisions. Similar to P2, P5 also highlighted the role of the chairman 
by stating  
We just recommend the project but the final decision is the chairperson. 
The chairperson issue agenda on the potential project and we make our 
paperwork and contribute. 
While discussing the board's role, the P5 provides valuable information that their SME board 
has a separate chairman role, who issues the agenda to all members, including the CEO, for 
projects. The CEO and other board members prepare for those projects and report to the 
chairman, who makes the final decisions, e.g. whether the project should be financed through 
fintech or conventional financing.   
It is important to note that in the UK there are no regulatory requirements for SMEs to have a 
separate board, even not a requirement to have a separate role of the chairman in the board. 
These SMEs strive to achieve good governance by having a board in their organisational 
structure. Further, the corporate governance code, which is a regulation for only listed 
203 
 
companies in the London Stock Exchange to either comply or explain good governance 
(Financial Reporting Council, 2024), does not apply to SMEs (Institute of Directors, 2024). The 
powers and responsibilities of the board, including decision-making and chairing the meeting, 
are determined through the legislative requirements of the Companies House Act 2006 and 
the Articles of Association (Tricker, 2015; Hannigan, 2018; Davies, 2020). One of the 
principles of the UK corporate governance code is to distinguish the role of the chair and the 
CEO on the board (Financial Reporting Council, 2024). Having a separate role of the chairman 
and CEO on the Board reflects their voluntary practice; however, it is good enough to bring 
accountability and transparency in their systems to achieve good governance.  
When asked about the decision-making on the board, P19's opinion about the chairman 
differed from P2 and P5 because of the duality of the role of a CEO and Chairman. According 
to P19 
I believe in working as a board …. I'm the chairman of that board , you know 
, I will listen to everyone with which with the give me the idea, you know 
what decision I should make , you know , because everyone got their own 
opinions and when I will be , I will be in unless they analyze their opinions. 
You know, I always learn something new and which will help me to make 
decision. 
P19 works with the board members in his dual roles. The duality of roles exists for 
organisations where CEOs work as a chairman on the board and simultaneously perform their 
CEO role (Palanissamy, 2015; Tang, 2017; Alves, 2023). For example, Nguyen et al. (2020) 
found that the duality of CEOs provides effective governance with the benefit of reducing 
agency costs. Agency cost is the expense which is incurred to resolve the conflict between 
management and the shareholders (Florackis, 2008). The agency cost associate in the 
organisation when directors or appointed CEOs controlling the company work for their own 
interest rather than the interests of their shareholders – agency theory in governance (Bosse 
& Phillips, 2016; Zogning, 2017; Schillemans & Bjurstrøm, 2020). Therefore, the chairman's 
204 
 
role on the board significantly impacts the organisations in achieving governance by reducing 
agency costs. The chairman factor is an independent element in the board to ensure the 
shareholder's interest is protected in the organisation. The findings of Tibiletti et al. (2021) 
indicated that the highest number of independent directors on the board provides more 
protection for shareholders’’ interests. Naciti (2019) found that the separation of the CEO and 
chairman role in the organisation creates the highest sustainability for the organisation. 
Further, the duality of the CEO in the organisation leads to inefficient investment decisions 
while lowering the firm’s value (Aktas, et al., 2019). Renders & Gaeremynck (2012) also found 
that good governance practices minimise agency conflict and lead to higher firm value. 
Further, a small board with separate roles of CEO and chairman results in higher performance 
for the firm (Azeez, 2015). The above evidence from the literature suggests that there are 
significant benefits of having the separate role of the chairman and CEO in the board for 
effective governance and that also results in minimising the conflict between the shareholders 
and the management. Further, having a chairman on the board reduces the agency's cost.  
 
5.2.2. 
Age 
The demographic data of the participants in this research is categorised into young CEOs 
(ages 18-25), middle-aged CEOs (ages 36-55), and older CEOs (ages 56+). Most 
respondents are in the age group of 36-55 years, representing 60% of the sample, while 25% 
represent 18-35 years and 15% represent 56+.

The current statistics confirm that 45% of young CEOs, 15% of middle-aged CEOs and 5% of 
older CEOs prefer Fintech financing. The CEOs’ preference for traditional financing includes 
205 
 
15% young CEOs, 5% middle-aged CEOs and 5% old CEOs. In addition, some CEOs prefer 
both traditional and Fintech financing, regardless of the reasons, including 5% of middle-aged 
and 5% of old CEOs. This demographic representation of the data does not imply that the 
CEOs' financing preferences are solely due to their age. However, it only represents the 
descriptive demographic statistics that age could be a significant factor in influencing CEOs in 
their financing preferences. There is a general lack or absence of empirical evidence to 
confirm CEOs’ decision-making regarding accepting Fintech due to their age group. The 
closely related evidence confirms that age contributes to decision-making. For example, de 
Acedo Lizárraga, et al. (2007) found that age is a human factor contributing to their decision. 
They found that human makes decisions depending on their age group, which is relevant to 
the tasks and depends on the different social and work-related environmental factors. The age 
can be linked to the flexibility of the CEO in their SMEs in adopting new and emerging financing 
models, with younger CEOs having a greater affinity and flexibility to adopt new funding and 
financing models, including Fintech, compared to older CEOs. This contradicts the findings of 
Bruine de Bruin et al. (2012), who indicated that decision-making skills increase with age.  
Similarly, an empirical study by Dror et al. (1998) explored the cognitive processes based on 
age group and found that old and younger people use systematic approaches to make 
decisions, and there are no differences in decision-making based on their age group. Some 
studies confirm that CEOs' age significantly impacts firm performance; for example, Serfling 
(2014) found that older CEOs invest less in research and development, which affects their 
financing decisions to lower risk for the firm. Evidence from Osei Bonsu et al. (2023) also 
indicated that age is an attribute of CEOs that influences their corporate financing decisions 
and determines the firm’s performance. Further, with the increase in age, CEOs’ experience 
enables them to make confident and aggressive decisions for more proper financing options 
in their organisations (De Silva & Banda, 2022). Also, older CEOs rely more on debt financing 
(Loukil & Yousfi, 2023). This evidence confirms that CEOs' age is one of their attributes to 
contribute in different situations, e.g. in decision-making relating to financing, either to take 
debt or equity or use a mix of both. However, in this research study, age is linked to factors 
206 
 
such as experience and Fintech knowledge that influence CEOs' decisions. For example, 
older CEOs above 55 have longer tenures and are well-equipped with the knowledge and 
experience to make the right financing choices. Therefore, they have mixed opinions about 
not supporting Fintech or traditional but using any financing option suitable to their 
organisational funding needs. Similarly, younger and middle-aged CEOs prefer fintech 
because of efficiency, more funding options and lower regulations, which is already evident 
from the research findings. It can be implied that age enhances CEOs' experience as decision-
makers and knowledge of Fintech in emerging and developed markets. Younger CEOs are 
more likely to explore riskier strategies, including capital structure financing strategies, 
compared to older CEOs (Yeoh & Hooy, 2022) to accept Fintech financing. The discussion 
revolves around the question of identifying the significant relationship between the CEO's age 
and the adoption and pursuit of innovative strategies in SMEs, such as the adoption of Fintech 
financing. Therefore, the younger CEOs' financing preferences towards Fintech bring 
innovation to their organisation with new financing options. That is also evident from the 
findings of Chen et al. (2015), Garcés-Galdeano & García-Olaverri (2019), Yim & Kang (2020) 
and Oliver (2023) that the younger CEOs are more likely to participate in the firms’ innovation, 
including research and development with their decisions than older CEOs.  
 
5.2.3. 
Gender  
Gender is a term to identify the differences of feelings, mind-sets, interests and behaviour 
between male and female (Powell & Graves, 2003). It is related to the biological characteristics 
of individuals as a sex difference in leadership styles within the organisation. The biological 
differences between person are sources of their recognition such as face, finger prints, hand-
geometry, voice, ear, palm print, blood and deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and that ultimate 
influence their approaches and behaviour (Mordini & Green, 2009) in the social context 
(Voskoboynikov, 2017). Human beings either males or females have biological and social 
components in their personality. Biological components relate to their inner physical, mental, 
207 
 
cognitive and spiritual characteristics while social components are acquired from the 
environment (Voskoboynikov, 2017) such as education, experience and professional skills. 
Gender differences, therefore, exemplify of how a group of people within the society think, 
learn, remember, behave, make decisions and show attitude towards other group of people 
(Powell & Graves, 2003). People with different sexual orientation also distinguish themselves 
in their grooming style, hobbies, clothing, music, activities, friendship, professional association 
and opinions in the workplace (Powell & Graves, 2003). There is lots of literature about the 
male or female dominancy in the workplace, especially for the position CEOs or directorship. 
For example, women in Fintech are 29% of total employees while men 83% at the leadership 
role with 71% at the directorship of FTSE companies (Souliman, 2019). Even though there is 
variation in numbers at the top of firm leadership, the gender factor is still essential for this 
research to identify whether it contributes to CEOs' decision-making regarding their financing 
preferences. In this research sample includes the demographic gender statistics relating to 
their decision to accept Fintech, traditional financing or both. The significant of current 
research is that mixed gender views and perceptions are associated to the financing options, 
leadership styles within the organisation and the decision making at the organisational and 
board level.

As indicated in the above table, demographic information shows that 64% of males and 67%

Empirical evidence from Obamuyi (2013) to identify factors which influence the investment 
decisions of individuals indicated that the gender factor influences managerial decision-
making in organisations. Results of the study showed that gender is one of the influential 
factors in investment decision-making. Marta et al. (2008) also concluded that female 
managerial decision-making processes in small businesses differ from male managers. 
Missioura (2014), in their study of decision-making and gender differences, indicated that male 
and female directors' norms, attitudes, and behavioural beliefs are significantly different in 
their decision-making regarding strategy, future planning, and financial targets. Females are 
more interested in the future of SME targets and rely on outside organisations' suggestions in 
their decision-making than men (Missioura, 2014). The results of MacGregor (2004) are 
evidence that gender differences influence managerial decision-making in accepting 
technology in an organisation, such as e-commerce. E-commerce is a term used to describe 
financial transactions over the Internet (Laudon & Traver, 2020). Financial transactions 
through electronic platforms include taking and making payments for the business over the 
internet and accepting different forms of currencies, including cryptocurrency, bitcoins and 
other forms of digital currencies from customers. The study results are consistent with the 
results of MacGregor (2004), Missioura (2014), and Obamuyi (2013), which confirm that 
gender, among other micro-level factors, influences CEOs in their financing preference.  
Therefore, the conceptual framework is established in the following figure 15. The conceptual 
framework confirms that CEOs lead their major financing decisions due to their unique 
leadership style and decision-making skills. Several factors at the micro level, such as their   
leadership traits, vision, values, support from the family, education, experience, risk-taking 
attitude, gender, and age, contribute to their financing decisions. At the meso level, CEOs 
work in organisations with different organisational structures. These structures, if adaptive to 
209 
 
their business environment, such as organic structure, facilitate the smooth flow of information 
without any barriers from the management team for CEOs' timely decisions, leading to 
innovation such as the acceptance of Fintech financing. Their networking with professional 
experts, including members of the board or the management, significantly contributes to their 
financing decisions. CEOs consult with the government policies and laws at the macro level 
and ensure their SMEs comply with relevant legislation. They also seek support from the 
government to create enough awareness for them for Fintech financing.
