210 
 
6. Conclusion 
 
6.1. Summary of findings  
This study aimed to understand the CEOs' decision making relating to Fintech financing in UK 
SMEs. In this study, the researcher developed four key research questions based on the 
research objectives to explore the research phenomenon. The conceptual framework was 
developed to explore how CEOs perceive the influence of factors (micro, meso and macro) to 
navigate their Fintech financing decision. The first research question established that CEOs 
prefer Fintech because it provides more financing options, the cost of financing is lower than 
traditional financing through banks, and there are fewer regulations. CEOs mentioned many 
financing options through technology-enabled platforms such as Seedrs, GoFundMe, 
Indiegogo (P11), Kickstarter (P9), and Crowdcube (P6). Crowdfunding, P2P lending and 
cryptocurrency are prominent financing options. CEOs obtain funding of any amount from 
these financing options; either they need funds for equipment (P11), project (P5), open a 
franchise (P9) or business support (P10). More financing options enable CEOs to get funds 
from investors (P5, P6, and P10) by negotiating repayment terms (P6). Fintech's financing 
supports SMEs through equity or debt from investors. To avoid high costs of financing, CEOs 
avoid taking debt from banks due to higher interest rates (P5, P18, P6, P14) because of 
inflation, the disruption from COVID-19 (P5) and the economic crises due to war (P3, P7). 
They prefer to invite investors (P6) to negotiate cheaper lending rates (P9, P6, P5, P15, and 
P11). Further, CEOs' preference for Fintech is fewer regulations (P19, P20, P18, P7, P3, P6, 
P1) because the banks have strict (P6, P7), complex and lengthy lending criteria (P3). Due to 
the fewer regulations, CEOs find that Fintech offers quick finance with flexible repayment 
terms. In the presence of Fintech financing options, CEOs' decisions for lending through banks 
is their low awareness of Fintech (P13, P2, P9, P8, P5, P1, and P2).  
The second question was about how micro factors contribute to CEOs’ Fintech decision-
making. CEOs' leadership traits help them to find the best financing source (P1, P15, P19, 
211 
 
P12), and that includes leading the organisation with effective communication (P9, P20), 
dedication (P14, P11), and ethics (P11) to promote the culture of accountability (P12) with 
innovative decisions. CEO vision is more strategic, and they work within the values of integrity 
and transparency (P10, P14) to make FinTech decisions. CEOs' perceptions about the family 
contribution to their financing decision are that it provides them with ideas and emotional 
support. Their belief also contributes to their decisions, especially their trust in Fintech, which 
increases if it gives them more benefits and less risk. Education and experience provide them 
with knowledge and skills to improve their decision-making. Experienced CEOs consider 
themselves risk-takers because they anticipate a good return on their decisions. The result of 
this study further reveals that experienced CEOs are more risk taker, and that is because of 
their experience and knowledge of Fintech. However, that does not conclude that CEOs prefer 
Fintech financing. Younger CEOs are risk avoiders because they feel financing through 
Fintech is risky, and no regulatory body oversees their transactions. Younger CEOs need 
assurance despite their awareness that Fintech financing has more benefits than traditional 
financing through banks and is secure due to blockchain technology.  
At the meso level, CEOs' professional network, board, and organisational structure 
significantly influence their Fintech financing decisions. CEOs build their professional network 
with staff (P10, P7, P6, and P13) and experts (P2, P1, P18, and P3). The information they 
need to make finance decisions depends on their organisational structure to collect from the 
relevant departments (P6, P10) or their subordinates (P2, P12, P14, P20). However, their 
decisions are also influenced by the board's collective decisions led by the chairman (P1, P3, 
P8, P9, P2, P13, P4, and P7).  
At the macro level, CEOs consult with the government regulations (P1, P5, P7, P8, and P19), 
legal staff (P1, P10, and P14) and regulators (P9, P13) that influence their Fintech financing 
decisions. Further, there are no specific Fintech laws and even less support from the 
government to create awareness.   
 
212 
 
6.2. Contributions of the Study 
Money-related services are accessed in today's dynamic business environment via FinTech 
financing and traditional banking financing. Fintech can be leveraged to improve SMEs' 
financial services, profitability, and organisational capacity to be competitive. For example, 
organisations adopting Fintech can distinguish themselves from their competitors and boost 
their capacity to retain and attract new customers (Alkhawaldeh, et al., 2023). As a result of 
Fintech's proliferation, SMEs now have access to several new avenues for securing funding 
(Ferrarini, 2017; Temelkov & Gogova Samonikov, 2018; Giglio, 2021; Kumari & Devi, 2022). 
These include various digital platforms, online lenders, crowdfunding, peer-to-peer lending 
networks and crypto assets. Fintech has revolutionised the financial sector by offering efficient, 
cost-effective, scalable, stable, reliable, easy, and convenient innovative financial alternatives 
to traditional systems (Suryono et al., 2020; Kou et al., 2021; Kumari & Devi, 2022). Fintech 
has improved the delivery process, quality of financial services, and customer satisfaction and 
created a more diverse financial landscape (Mention, 2019; Kou et al., 2021). The study 
revealed different insights that would have notable contributions to knowledge and literature, 
practice, and policy changes.  
6.2.1. 
Contribution to Knowledge and Literature  
 
6.2.1.1. 
Transformational Leadership 
. There is no prior research in this field; hence, this project contributes to the extant literature 
on CEOs’ decision-making in Fintech financing. The study results show that CEOs have 
various functions to perform and consider their leadership traits crucial for their Fintech 
financing decision-making. CEOs work in a competitive organisational environment and 
respond to change with innovative decisions. They work with their team at the management 
level and board members at the Board level to promote collective decisions for shared 
organisational goals, such as financing decisions for their organisation's specific funding 
needs. Their vision enables them to compete with their competitors, based on their 
213 
 
organisational vision as a set of directions set by the Board and per shareholders' 
requirements, ensuring their decisions benefit their organisation. The study contribution to the 
existing literature includes CEOs' leadership traits, beliefs and values, enabling them to make 
independent financing decisions by inspiring and motivating their team for the long-term vision 
of their organisation (i.e., contribution to Bass & Riggio, 2006 and Baškarada, et al., 2017) by 
seeking emotional support from family and knowledge through their professional networking. 
They engage with their team and work towards their development by listening, inspiring and 
encouraging them towards their organisational long-term vision. Further, they also contribute 
positively to the board meetings for collective decisions. That promotes integrity (Liborius, 
2021) in financing decisions for long-term organisational financial stability (Gannon and 
Hieker, 2022).  
In addition, the researcher contributes to the extant literature by providing empirical evidence 
that family members improve CEOs' confidence and provide emotional support to proceed 
with crowdfunding, P2P lending, or financing through crypto assets. The discussion with the 
family enhances CEOs’ emotional intelligence. The positive benefits and lower risk impact 
their CEO's beliefs to gain trust in the Fintech financing, enabling them to make decisions 
confidently. CEOs actively seek to develop their knowledge and experience for personal and 
professional development through formal and vocational education, which the researcher 
believes enhances their decision-making skills. They maintain that higher knowledge of 
Fintech financing options contributes to their funding decisions. The practical knowledge is 
significant in this study because CEOs believe that professional networking contributes to 
developing their knowledge. They practically develop networking within their organisation and 
outside by joining experts. CEOs also learn from their experience and promote high-
performance teams by ensuring that their organisation, due to its complex hierarchical 
structure, doesn’t create barriers to information from their subordinates, which helps them 
make timely decisions. Especially during the pandemic, Brexit and the current era of higher 
inflation, where the cost of living has tremendously increased in the UK, CEOs ensure that 
214 
 
their organisation doesn’t pay much debt, so they collectively work with the board to get 
technical support from other directors and management team for better decisions especially 
in the interest for their organisation stakeholders without promoting their self-interest. 
Experience enables them to evaluate all possible sources of funds from traditional and Fintech 
financing and make rational decisions for their SME's benefits to avoid higher funding costs. 
Rational decisions include the evaluation of all evidence relating to the funding, including their 
feasibility and acceptability and the long-term impact on their SMEs to make the best choice. 
In making rational decisions, CEOs' decisions are based on the cognitive theory of decision-
making. They consider their vision, beliefs, family, and expertise as contributing factors in 
addition to the organisational factors where they work to shape their financing preferences.  
The researcher contributes that CEOs prefer Fintech because the benefits of technology-
enabled funding are higher at lower costs and fewer regulations. Even though Fintech is new 
in the UK, it has not fully evolved as the traditional financing method for funding. However, 
preferences towards Fintech suggest that they take risks to make innovative decisions, which 
is also a characteristic of transformational leadership. Therefore, the influence of micro-level 
factors such as having a long-term vision, working collectively with the board without relying 
on their self-interest for their organisational benefits, inspiring and motivating their teams 
towards shared goals, working within their values of integrity and beliefs, consulting with the 
family for emotional support and professional support from networking, using their expertise, 
and a risk-taking attitude towards innovative decisions are more aligned with the 
transformational leaders.  
 
6.2.1.2. 
Organisational Structure 
The study contributes to prior studies, i.e., Chaston (1997), Bai, et al. (2017) and Su et al. 
(2019) with the significance of the organisational structure, which influences CEOs in their 
financing preferences. Chaston (1997) identified the entrepreneurial marketing styles of small 
and medium-sized companies and the influence of the organisational structure for the 
215 
 
entrepreneurial orientation on the firms’ performance. They found that the overall performance 
of UK SMEs can be improved by becoming entrepreneurial with an organic structure. This 
research contributes that organic organisational structure in SMEs leads them towards 
innovative financing decisions due to fewer barriers to the flow of information to CEOs when 
making financing decisions. Su et al. (2019) research confirms that there is a positive 
relationship between the organic structure of the organisation and managerial innovation. The 
contribution to the extent of this research is that CEOs' innovation increases with their Fintech 
financing due to the organic structure, which is suitable to respond quickly and timely to their 
external environmental demand to accept innovation i.e. Fintech. Similarly, the research of 
Bai, et al. (2017) was focused on the impact of the organisational structure on the performance 
of a new product development team and found that organic structure leads towards innovation 
and is positively linked to the performance of a new product development team by reducing 
the barriers of cooperation and knowledge sharing. In the research, the researcher found that 
the organic structure of SMEs leads their CEOs to have information from their management 
team without barriers that encourage their innovative decisions by meeting their SME financing 
needs with Fintech effective improved teamwork. It is because CEOs are responsible for 
setting the company's overall vision and strategy. They decide what they want, take steps to 
identify the areas of improvement, and then plot a course of action to achieve the 
organisational goals. Being transformational leaders for their organisation, their ability to unite 
and lead their team around their vision, shared goals and inspire their subordinates is crucial 
to the company's success (Stoker et al., 2012; Latham, 2013; Saeed & Ziaulhaq, 2018; 
Alblooshi et al., 2021). The study concluded that micro- and macro-sized SMEs represent their 
organisational structure as organic because they work collectively in their organisation, the 
flow of information for CEOs to make decisions is efficient without any structural barriers, and 
due to this, they respond to innovation and accept Fintech financing by prompt responding to 
their environmental factors. In contrast, large-sized SMEs with complex organisational 
structures tend to favour conventional funding.  
216 
 
The choice of the preferred financing for their SMEs is a critical role of CEOs. Because the 
CEOs of SMEs ensure that the best financing options for their organisations bring innovation, 
create strategic alliances, and confirm their organisations’ market dominance. SMEs are 
challenged by their internal and external environmental forces, which pressure CEOs to make 
reliable financing choices. Their internal organisation environment includes tangible or 
intangible factors in the company's control, such as plans, policies, values, human resources, 
financial resources, band, identity, fixed assets, current assets, relationships between 
employees, and in-house technology. Internal factors influence CEOs’ decisions to identify 
business opportunities by using their resources and help them deal with external threats. The 
external environmental factors include intangible and tangible factors that are not in the 
company's control. Still, they impact the organisation, such as requirements from the 
customers, suppliers, public, competitors, media, external talent, external technology and 
government policies (Nieuwenhuizen et al., 2012). The CEOs’ Fintech decision-making in 
SMEs is significant in having the suitable organisational structure to accept innovation and 
change while dealing with challenges of internal and external environmental factors. The study 
found that CEOs often have the last say in financing decisions if rigid hierarchies characterise 
their organisational structure. Because numerous levels of clearance are required at the 
different departmental levels of the structure, their financial decision-making is frequently slow. 
Those CEOs who prefer traditional financing, such as funding through banks, respond less to 
their organisational external environment, and their organisational structure is based on a 
number of departments. That also leads to significant paperwork and time-consuming 
decision-making procedures for CEOs. These conventional finance methods are formal and 
procedural and fit perfectly with the mechanistic organisational structures. Mechanistic 
structures' complex hierarchical structure can be both a strength and a weakness. While it 
guarantees distinct lines of power and responsibility, adjusting to quickly shifting market 
conditions may make it more difficult. As a result, mechanical organisations overlook 
innovative prospects and react more slowly to arising financial issues. In this project, most 
CEOs have organic organisational structures in their SMEs, and they work with their team to 
217 
 
get quick information to share so they can make financing decisions. Decision-making is 
frequently collaborative in these environments, and quick response to market dynamics are 
highly valued. CEOs of SMEs prefer Fintech financing alternatives like funding through 
cryptocurrency, crowdfunding, or peer-to-peer lending, which require timely information from 
their team. Organic structure in SMEs encourages CEOs’ innovative decisions through their 
collaborative working with the board and staff, allowing them to grab opportunities better and 
respond effectively to the challenges of the organisational environment. Therefore, CEOs 
assess their organisational factors and then make Fintech-related decisions to ensure they 
meet the internal stakeholders and external challenging requirements of laws, regulations, 
technological innovation, and economic conditions. Therefore, the study contributes to the 
theory of organisational structure that impacts UK SME CEOs Fintech financing decisions by 
responding to their organisational environment. 
 
6.2.1.3. 
The Significance of the Pecking Order Theory of Financing 
The study contributes to the findings of the discussion paper of Degryse et al. (2009) in the 
European Banking Centre, with practical implications for SME financing decisions. Degryse et 
al. (2009) found that Dutch SMEs' capital structure decisions support the Pecking Order 
Theory. Dutch SMEs prefer to use profit in their first step to lowering the need for short-term 
debt, whereas, for growth, these organisations use long-term finances. This study found that 
UK SMEs prefer to use their internal funds from equity and retrained earnings to meet the 
demand of their financing requirements. In addition, the results show that most SMEs prefer 
to use alternative modes of funding through Fintech, which is consistent with the Pecking 
Order Theory. The main reasons this study has found are that UK SMEs prefer to use 
alternative modes of funding, such as Fintech, which supports the Pecking Order Theory of 
financing, including the lower cost of financing, fewer regulations, and more funding options. 
The lower cost of financing is due to the efficient blockchain technology, which lowers 
transaction costs due to the exclusion of intermediaries such as banks and SMEs' access to 
218 
 
interested investors across the globe. These investors can fund the SME's projects or their 
financial needs in flexible and negotiable terms with less regulatory compliance to maintain 
and offer quick transactions. Banks are regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority, and 
these financial institutions have the regulatory burdens to support anti-money laundering and 
anti-terrorism in financial transactions, which increase the cost of funding. SME CEOs can 
also access various funding platforms without going to the banks, which have strict lending 
criteria and higher interest rates on loans. These platforms include Neobanks, cryptocurrency, 
crowdfunding, and P2P lending, which provide them with multiple options to get cheaper and 
negotiable rates.  
 
6.2.1.4. 
Agency Theory in the Governance 
The study contributes to the extend the governance literature of Samuel (2013), Renders & 
Gaeremynck (2012), Nguyen et al. (2020), and Tibiletti et al. (2021) that governance is a 
significant factor in CEOs in SMEs to influence their financing decisions by reducing agency 
cost and bringing expertise to the Board with the inclusion of independent directors. In this 
study, SMEs have a board, which is a separate body from the management, to achieve good 
governance. Most of the significant financial decisions that impact the SMEs are led by the 
board's chairman, with support from the executive and non-executive members who are 
directors. Most SME CEOs believe in working collectively with other Board members to reach 
Fintech financing decisions. Effective corporate governance practices favour the performance 
of their organisations and enable CEOs to make consistent, valuable, and best financial 
decisions in the interest of their stakeholders. However, the Board's arrangement depends on 
the organisation's size. The board is arranged for various purposes in SMEs. It could be one 
transparent way to show lenders that they have good governance by adding independent 
factors of non-executive directors. Even though there are no regulatory requirements for SMEs 
to follow the UK Quality Code for Governance, the main aims of the governance could be, for 
many reasons, to demonstrate to outside lending organisations that they have an effective 
governance arrangement similar to that of large-sized organisations. Because banks have a 
219 
 
history of granting less funding to SMEs, e.g. evidence confirms that SMEs receive 
approximately 17 per cent of the total loans from banking institutions (Lu, 2018). Conversely, 
large business organisations receive a large portion of these loans. This indicates limited 
lending to SMEs from traditional financing organisations. Today, banks are more risk-averse 
in the face of major financial crises (Lu, 2018). In addition, increases in inflation and interest 
rates caused SMEs to find it challenging to obtain funding from banks. It also poses a risk for 
lenders to have strict lending criteria. Consequently, banks are only comfortable with financing 
corporations with colossal potential to repay the loan plus interest. This is because large-sized 
organisations have effective governance systems to allow the transparency of transactions 
and the confirmation of the loan's payback. Similarly, traditional financing organisations, such 
as banks, often require collateral before providing loans to organisations (Lyon & Owen, 2019). 
However, UK SMEs may lack collateral and steady income sources to acquire loans. 
Consequently, CEOs in UK SMEs seek other forms of financing, such as Fintech, to ensure 
their businesses continue to survive and grow amidst challenging economic times. This could 
be achieved by having an effective governance system that allows for the preferred financing 
choice. The significance of corporate governance in SMEs is that it promotes collaborative 
working on the board to provide confidence to CEOs to make the right financing decisions. 
This is evident from the current study, which confirms that the governance arrangement 
influences the CEOs' decisions. Before making the Fintech financing decisions, CEOs consult 
with their board's executive and non-executive members. Consulting with the board members 
means experts back CEOs' decisions to reflect the transparency of their decisions. The study 
confirms that CEOs are part of their board, which the chairman leads. The Board members 
work collectively for effective financing decisions. The diverse and expert board ensures 
effective governance that benefits SMEs in representing their organisation to potential lenders 
to acquire funds. CEOs are appointed by their shareholders, work as board members, lead 
the operational part of their organisation, and represent their board to external stakeholders. 
Having a board in the SMEs and the role of CEOs in financing decision-making ensure the 
220 
 
protection of their shareholders and support the foundation of the agency theory of 
governance.  
 
6.2.2. 
Contribution to Practice  
The findings of this study revealed different practical implications that can be used to improve 
the adoption of Fintech to leverage associated benefits such as efficiency and accessibility of 
financial services. For example, it was apparent that government laws and regulations 
significantly impact Fintech decisions, as evidenced by how CEOs considered them while 
making financial decisions. The interviewed CEOs indicated they comply with government 
laws and regulations when making Fintech decisions. That contributes to the current literature 
(Chen et al. 2014; Kiwanuka, 2015; Rosavina & Rahadi, 2018; Ghazali & Yasuoka, 2018; 
Owen,et al., 2019; Rosavina, et al. 2019; Goo and Heo, 2020; Ahern, 2021; Zarrouk et al., 
2021; Makki and Alqahtani, 2022; Ng and Pan, 2022) that the government policies and support 
are linked to the greater levels of adoption of Fintech financing. Aligning Fintech decisions with 
governmental laws and policies significantly influences adoption (Dwivedi et al., 2021; Ng and 
Pan, 2022). Most SMEs have their legal department to work on the lawful aspects and 
adherence to rules, regulations and standards to practice in the sector. Therefore, CEOs 
ensure they comply with government laws and regulations to avoid legal consequences for 
non-compliance. The CEOs should collaborate and align Fintech decisions with governmental 
rules and ensure they address all privacy issues, possible threats, and customer protection 
(Takeda & Ito, 2021). 
Another practical implication of the results of this study is that they can be used to improve 
awareness about Fintech services to enable SMEs to leverage benefits associated with the 
technology. Lack of Fintech awareness was a notable reason CEOs indicated for opting for 
traditional financial services. As such, Fintech companies and other stakeholders can improve 
awareness about Fintech to support its adoption. CEOs' Fintech competencies can be 
221 
 
enhanced through training and education, which has been proven to increase Fintech 
adoption (Niu et al., 2020). 
One of the reasons for the SME CEOs' preference for Fintech was the increasing demand and 
challenges associated with traditional financing options. Fintech options can be used to 
address the challenges associated with traditional financing options. For example, Fintech 
services enhance the customer experience and enable firms to build and maintain competitive 
advantage, lower costs, minimise risks, and increase efficiency (Restoy, 2020; Martinčević et 
al., 2020; Alkhawaldeh et al., 2023). Therefore, Fintech options can be used as alternative 
practical approaches for addressing SMEs' financial challenges associated with traditional 
financing methods.  
According to Kvasov et al. (2021), experts play a crucial role in Fintech decision-making. At 
the meso level, CEOs' professional network within the organisational culture and outside 
through professional membership increases their Fintech knowledge and influences their 
funding choice. However, the current study confirms that CEOs are willing to be part of 
professional networking if it solely rates FinTech. Due to the lack of professional networking 
opportunities in England, CEOs have barriers to having sufficient knowledge to make FinTech 
decisions. SME CEOs can, therefore, improve their Fintech decisions by forming professional 
networks to share their expertise and emerging knowledge about Fintech developments.  
Globally, SMEs represent 90% of businesses and contribute over 50% of employment 
opportunities (Sharma, et al., 2023). SMEs contributed approximately 50% to the global gross 
domestic product opportunities (Sharma, et al., 2023). Considering the significant contribution 
of SMEs to the countries and global economy, there is a need to optimise their operations and 
address the associated challenges. SMEs can leverage benefits related to Fintech, such as 
improved access to cost-effective, scalable, stable, reliable, and innovative financial options 
(Suryono et al., 2020; Kumari & Devi, 2022). However, unlike large enterprises, there is low 
adoption of digital technologies such as Fintech in SMEs (Moreira-Santos, et al., 2022). The 
study's findings on challenges impeding Fintech adoption in SMEs, including limited 
awareness of associated benefits and insufficient information and policies that limit decision-
222 
 
making, can be used to improve its adoption. Fintech firms should create awareness among 
SMEs of the available risk mitigation strategies and their effectiveness to improve their 
adoption. Fintech firms should also conduct regular assessments to understand SMEs' 
preferences and the unique challenges they face to facilitate prompt measures to address 
their concerns.  
SMEs can adopt Fintech options to improve their financial viability. Cost savings, fewer 
regulations and more funding options accelerate SMEs' preference for Fintech financing. More 
funding options through Fintech can also help SMEs evaluate various financing platforms. For 
example, through crowdfunding, P2P and cryptocurrency, SMEs can get funding ideas for any 
financing needs by analysing funding data through various Fintech financing platforms. This 
will enable them to be better positioned for short- and long-term financial decisions. On the 
other hand, Fintech companies should conduct a survey and research in the market and 
industry to determine the rising demands and financing needs to attract SMEs so they can 
resolve their financing needs. Fintech financing providers can also arrange workshops to 
educate their clients on the benefits, advantages, effectiveness, accuracy, and convenience 
of using and applying digital solutions in the market. These funding providers can use social 
media for Fintech campaigns. This will improve their product awareness through customer 
engagement and marketing, increasing their chances of success. Social media has become 
the most significant communication tool for Fintech companies to attract SMEs. This is 
because millions of users worldwide interact with their friends and family through mobile-
friendly social media applications. DenizBank in Turkey and Amex worldwide have integrated 
Facebook and bank accounts to run all their online financial services (FinTech Futures, 2018). 
Similarly, banks can use Fintech features in their services to provide improved solutions for 
SMEs.  
 
223 
 
6.2.3. 
Contribution to Policies 
The findings of this study can also be used to inform policy changes about government laws 
and regulations on Fintech technologies. Some CEOs indicated that some government laws 
and regulations about Fintech impede their financial decisions. This finding calls for a more 
favourable and fairer legal environment that supports SMEs' adoption and use of FinTech 
technologies. For example, the government can develop laws and regulations that give more 
freedom and protection to SMEs regarding the adoption and use of Fintech technologies while 
not compromising other crucial implications, such as security and fraudulent activities. 
Through policies and regulations, government support can enhance the development of 
favourable Fintech ecosystems through regulatory sandboxes, legislation, infrastructure 
development, and regulations promoting Fintech services (Nugraha, et al., 2022). Widespread 
adoption of Fintech technologies requires regulatory support and consistent policies to ensure 
the security and privacy of financial transactions (Moreira-Santos et al., 2022; Rehman et al., 
2023). As such, the government should develop laws and regulations that balance the 
potential benefits of Fintech technologies and associated threats.  
To promote knowledge and expertise, the government can establish a financial innovation hub 
that could bring experts from financial and non-financial sectors and countries to promote UK 
Fintech. The proposed financial innovation hub should have a membership for SME CEOs to 
establish professional networking opportunities and an education support service to guide 
them practically on Fintech and further laws and regulatory guidance to streamline their 
Fintech-related decisions and organisational compliance. Lastly, even though the IMF and 
World Bank endorsed the financing through Fintech, the fact that cyberattacks have increased 
on Fintech firms can’t be ignored. There is a need for a discussion involving participants from 
experts from the legal, IT industry, financing and Fintech institutions to introduce 
measurements to prevent cyberattacks.  
 
224 
 
6.3. Limitations 
The research has several limitations because it is based only in England and the UK. Results 
could be generalised to other parts of the world but with caveat because rules, regulations, 
and laws are different as the sample is taken based on the participant recruitment criteria in 
which an SME from the non-financial sector should be established under the Companies 
House Act in the UK. Therefore, we must consider the Article of Association of how the 
organisation should govern and operate under a set of rules per the Companies House Act 
2006. These incorporation rules may be different to the other parts of the world. The other 
participant recruitment criteria include getting the views and opinions of working CEOs in 
SMEs. These SMEs are more than a year old and contain at least two people. A slight change 
in the participant recruitment criteria may bring a different outcome. The study has a sufficient 
sample size due to the nature of the research methodology to get a detailed description of 
CEO factors for their financing preferences and how these factors influence their Fintech 
financing decisions. As such, the findings of this study are limited to working SMEs and may 
not be generalised to those who have already worked and have a wealth of knowledge to 
provide detailed insights due to their experience. The number of participants can be extended 
to enhance the research scope, including their location in other parts of the UK and overseas. 
Other limitations are that the research is qualitative which may include the potential bias with 
the interview data, and as an interviewer, the researcher's value and interpretation of the 
CEO's opinions may be reflected in the study. The researcher ensured to work within the 
ethical guidelines and analysed the CEOs' views and opinions of their financing preferences 
to report unbiased results.  
 
6.4. Reflection 
First, I would like to start with the Covid-19 pandemic which was the most challenging period. 
Despite the intense preventive measures, the researcher was affected twice with Covid-19 in 
2020 and 2021. It affected the researcher’s memory for a shorter period of time. However, 
225 
 
frequent online meetings with the supervisor helped the researcher’s motivation and 
concentration during the recovery period. Second, while interviewing the participants, specific 
valuable insights emerged that were not explicitly conveyed in their responses to the interview 
questions. These subtle insights provided a deeper understanding of the participants' 
thoughts, opinions, perceptions, emotions, and motivations regarding different aspects of 
Fintech. For example, through observation during the interviews, it was apparent that the 
participants were optimistic about the impacts of Fintech financial services. Participants' 
optimism was evident in their body language and enthusiasm as they discussed different 
aspects of Fintech, including its potential and anticipated positive impacts of their decisions. 
In addition, from their personal views, some participants expressed how Fintech had improved 
their financial situations, underscoring their optimistic perspective on the sector.  
From the interviewing questions, it was found that CEOs are very educated and have a wealth 
of experience. They have busy schedules and holding them at a certain time for a volunteer 
interview was very difficult. Their answers were to the point, and they avoided over-justifying 
their views, sometimes used complex business terms which could have various meanings for 
an interpreter, and avoided releasing their personal story for micro and meso questions where 
they felt their answers could compromise their personal and organisational confidentiality. The 
researcher understood that their position in the organisation was not equal to those 
participants who had an exploratory storyline to tell of each question. However, from their 
listening skills, how they responded to interview questions, and the compliments provided to 
probing questions, most participants indicated their great interest in the topic of their decision-
making factors for the Fintech financing. Also, participants' interest was evident in their 
eagerness to engage in discussion. CEOs’ curiosity and desire to deepen their understanding 
of the factors in their decision-making for the Fintech options demonstrated their genuine 
interest in the research.  
 
226 
 
6.5. Further Research Agenda 
The recommendations made are based on the findings obtained in this study. The research 
has already reported a significant contribution to the theory, practices, and policies about the 
CEOs' financing preferences and the contributing factors that influence their Fintech financing 
decisions. However, it is not known whether their financing preference supports the pecking 
order theory of financing or the trade-off theory of financing. In these theories, SMEs choose 
debt or equity, equity over debt or debt over equity, or a combination of debt and equity. What 
is its sequence, and how is it related to the pecking order or the trade-off theory of financing? 
For this purpose, the researcher can take the evidence relating to the dates of the actual 
Fintech Financing decisions by CEOs, the company's financial strategy of what they prefer 
(debt/equity or both) and historical financial records.  
Second, the same research can also be extended by collecting evidence relating to one or 
more Fintech financing options. It can be P2P, Crowdfunding or Cryptocurrency. For example, 
the potential research could be the impact of CEOs on Fintech Financing: Evidence from P2P 
on UK SMEs. In this case, the researchers will have to amend the participant recruitment 
criteria to include only CEOs using the same Fintech option.  
Third, the same research can also be sector-specific in SMEs. For example, potential 
researchers can find relevant non-financial sectors such as construction, health care, 
education, manufacturing, or even technology. In this case, the researchers will have to 
include one more participant recruitment requirement criteria relating to the sector. For 
example, the potential research could be the impact of CEOs on Fintech Financing: Evidence 
from UK manufacturing SMEs. In addition, this study did not compare data between 
participants from rural and urban SMEs. According to Nugraha et al. (2022), a significant gap 
exists in Fintech adoption among SME businesses in rural and urban locations. As such, it is 
recommended that future researchers compare if there are differences in how micro, meso, 
and macro factors influence CEOs' Fintech financial decisions in rural and urban SMEs. In this 
227 
 
case, there will be a slight change in the participant recruitment criteria while everything else 
will remain the same.  
Fourth, further research should be conducted to obtain empirical evidence on the relationship 
between CEOs’ perception of adopting FinTech financing and the factors that affect their 
intentions such as (not an exhaustive list) personal beliefs, alignment with policies and 
regulations, advantages, networking, and superiority over traditional methods. For example, a 
quantitative methodology with quasi-experimental designs can be conducted to determine the 
relationship using statistical means such as regression analysis. Future researchers can 
distinguish the difference between SMEs who use FinTech and those who apply traditional 
financing on factors such as customer satisfaction based on speed, accuracy, and efficiency. 
An analysis of variance (ANOVA) or independent t-test for new adopters can be applied to 
ascertain if there is a statistically significant difference between traditional and Fintech 
solutions. Future researchers could assess the impact of knowledge on the adoption and 
usage of FinTech solutions in an empirical or mixed-method study to establish if creating 
awareness of digital solutions can impact the decisions of entrepreneurs. Specifically, 
considering the associated benefits, it is recommended that future researchers should explore 
how Fintech financing options can be sustained in SMEs. Future studies on financial 
performance and efficiency between Fintech and traditional financing are also recommended 
to facilitate a holistic understanding of the best options for different financial decisions facing 
SMEs. Through a holistic comparison of Fintech and traditional funding, SME CEOs can make 
informed decisions about other financial options that optimise the best interest of their 
businesses.  
Additionally, the benefits associated with FinTech include enhanced customer experience, 
building and maintaining competitive advantage, lowered costs, minimised risks, and 
increased efficiency (Martinčević et al., 2020; Restoy, 2020). Besides the associated benefits, 
Fintech can create risks such as data privacy issues, cybersecurity threats, and regulation 
uncertainties (Jawarneh, et al., 2023). As such, there is a need to explore SME CEO’s 
understanding of Fintech's potential challenges and risks. There is a need for understanding 
228 
 
strategies specific to SMEs that can be used to mitigate Fintech risks and address associated 
challenges. Therefore, future research on SME CEOs' awareness of Fintech risks and 
challenges and potential mitigation strategies based on their experience is recommended. 
Such understanding will form a basis for developing appropriate strategies and approaches to 
address underlying Fintech challenges and risks to improve its adoption in SMEs. According 
to Thomas (2023), financial and economic crises have always negatively affected SMEs' 
availability of financing options. Researchers could also explore feasible and effective 
approaches that CEOs use to overcome financial challenges associated with economic and 
financial crises. Those approaches would help novice SME CEOs and other business leaders 
ensure businesses' sustainability in times of crisis.

315 
 
Appendix 2 – Fintech development in the UK and EU 
In the UK, there are around 788 high grown FinTech companies, out of which 71% companies 
operate from London (Michel, 2019). In 2017, around £2 billion was raised by FinTech 
Companies and they are continuing to grow (Michel, 2019). According to standard industrial 
classification, FinTech includes different sectors and industries such as retail, education, 
fundraising, non-profit and the only few are classified as Banks. Companies like Visa, 
MasterCard, PayPal, and Stripe all revolutionised the payment systems in the UK. Small and 
medium sized companies are adopting new opportunities and models as opposed to traditional 
facilities provided by high-street banks. These enterprises are using FinTech for payment and 
lending. More consumers are securing financial services for free or at low cost. According to 
Bloomberg Intelligence report of emerging FinTech markets, the adoption rate of FinTech 
amongst consumers in the United Kingdom is 42% after China and India. This adoption rate 
is higher than any EU countries such as Spain 37% and Germany 35% (Bloomberg News, 
2019) . In the UK, around $16 billion were invested on FinTech in 2018 and it is likely to grow 
in the subsequent years (Warner, 2018). The same trends are evident in the EU where 
businesses have invested $22 billion in 2017 and $57 billion in 2018 (KPMG, 2018).  
 
 
 
316 
 
Appendix 3 – Fintech development in the USA 
FinTech revolution is a global movement in this new digital age and its impact have been seen 
in the United States over the years. It encompasses the introduction of FinTech in start-ups, 
small businesses and large financial institutions to integrate new technology in their existing 
services to remain competitive. FinTech is growing in the USA due to its competitive models, 
great customer experiences and less regulations. The adoption rate of FinTech in the United 
States consumers is 33% as per Bloomberg Intelligence report published in 2019. According 
to the Business insider report, FinTech secured $59 billion only in the first quarter of 2018 as 
compared 2015 ($59 billions), a target set in 2015 (Nonninger, 2019). SBI Holdings, Standard 
Chartered and BB&T are the biggest investors to revolutionise the FinTech. FinTech firms are 
growing faster in the USA and they have attracted over $12 billion of investment in 2014 and 
$4 billion in 2013. The same trends are seen in the period 2015-18. For example, the US most 
prominent Firms in FinTech like US insurtech Root, US pay check, advance FinTech Earnin 
and Coinbase have raised over $100 million in 2018. The main reasons for these revolutions 
in the USA are the quality of financial services, efficiency, low cost, legacy of IT systems, 
unburden financial regulations and the public confidence (Economist, 2015). It is more likely 
that more firms in the future will get benefits by engaging with FinTech in different sectors 
(Nonninger, 2019).  
 
 
317 
 
Appendix 4 – Fintech development in the ASIA 
Asian market seems to be more prominent market to adopt FinTech. When it comes to the 
innovation in Asia, the world has evident that the China is one step ahead from the rest of the 
world with 69% adoption rate by Chinese (Delacharlerie, 2018). FinTech is the biggest to the 
financial service providers in the ASIA. The revolution in the Asia started in 2004, when 
Alibaba adopted this technology in its platform with Alipay (a PayPal type service) to facilitate 
financial transactions through its e-commerce platforms. After initial success, Alibaba started 
to lend, provide insurance and micro-financing services. Other Tech giants like Baidu and 
Tencent also use Blockchain in the similar fashion as Alibaba.  
According to (Bloomberg News, 2019), Indian market is also one of the earlier adopters of 
FinTech services in Asia with 52% adoption rate after China. In 2016, India has introduced the 
concept of digital banking, a new model of the traditional banking, for example, Paytm. Paytm 
in India use Alibaba backed mobile block chain payment solutions which has achieved the 
status of the bank with a facility to accept restricted deposit and introduction of banking 
touchpoints. Touchpoints are web-based interfaces or it can be a mobile application with 
integration of organisation services to facilitate consumers with its products and services. It is 
also used to collect feedback. Alipay, Tenpay, Chinapay, UnionPay, QQ Wallet, Redpocket 
products have revolutionised the Asian market. Their market shares are more than 66% in the 
digital payment systems and many financial institutions have started to integrate these 
FinTech facilities in their products and services. Hong Kong’s FinTech investment was 
doubled in 2017 which was $546 million as compared to $216 million that is significantly higher 
than investments in Australia ($321 millions), Singapore ($180 million) and Japan ($105 
million).  
The reasons why ASIA market is more prominent to adopt FinTech are friendly government 
policies of ecosystems, less financial regulatory requirements and more consumers’ 
inclination to use user friendly IT interfaces. Other reasons are FinTech companies target low-
income consumers in Asian countries with their convenient, low-cost, and well-interconnected 
financial solutions such as savings, payment and lending as compared to traditional banks. 
This has been evident from the recent survey by EY Global FinTech Adoption Index in 2019 
which considered 27000 digitally active consumers in 27 markets to identify international 
consumers’ adoption rate and results showed that Asia is a global leader in FinTech adoption 
(EY, 2019).  
 
 
318 
 
Appendix 5 – Fintech development in Rest of the world 
The global trend of FinTech adoption and investments on FinTech has increased over the 
years. According to (Statista Research Department, 2019) which is a leading provider of 
market and consumer data, the last year FinTech investment was eight billion U.S. dollars as 
compared to three billion U.S. dollars in 2013. FinTech adoption rate amongst consumers from 
other part of the world has increased significant, for example, Brazil is now the fourth largest 
country of early adopters of FinTech after China, India and UK with 40% share of users who 
are active online, Maxico 36% and South Africa 35% (Bloomberg News, 2019).  
In Brazil, the central bank aims to encourage new technology and innovation in the tradition 
financial services by allowing Peer to Peer Lending and Direct Credit to Borrowers to target 
almost 35% adults’ population who have never used bank accounts as a new way to deliver 
financial services to them. The growth of FinTech companies in Brazil has increased in the 
last decade in lending, investment, payment, insurance, trade in cryptocurrencies and it is 
anticipated that FinTech companies will generate $24 billion in the next decade (TMF Group, 
2018).  
The same sentiment is evident in Mexico where approximately 37% of Mexican have access 
to an official bank accounts (World Bank, 2018) while unbanked consumers and SMEs are 
main targets of FinTech. It is also worth to mention that $89 million round were raised in 2017 
by FinTech Konfio in Mexico.  
According (Finnovista, 2018), statistics of FinTech in Latin America in 2018 was 51% in Brazil, 
40% in Mexico, 52% in Colombia, 56% in Argentina and 22% in Chile which significant higher 
than previous years. Apart from Latin America, FinTech market in the other part of the world 
such as Africa is generating $200 million, and it is likely to grow up to $3 billion by 2020 as per 
EcoBank – a leading independent pan-African banking group. Jumo is a leading a mobile 
platform to provide credits and savings has served over 25 million consumers in Africa and 
generated over $700 million (Reuters, 2018). Tulaa, Ecocash, Zoona, and Cellulant are most 
prominent FinTech companies in African countries (Tsan, et al., 2019) to disrupt financial 
industry with their efficient and effective low-cost financial services to consumers.  
 
 
319 
 
Appendix 6 – Types of crowdfunding 
There are different crowdfunding options for individuals, investors and businesses. Each type 
has its own unique features, benefits and rights to parties involve.  
Equity based Crowdfunding 
The equity-based crowdfunding market in the UK, as of 2015, was valued at approximately 
£332 million (Adhikary, et al., 2018). In terms of size, P2P lending is larger than equity-based 
crowdfunding market in the UK. However, the market has recorded significant growth of 
approximately 300 per cent between 2014 and 2015 and the UK has the fastest growing 
equity-based crowdfunding market in Europe (Zhang, et al., 2015). The first equity-based 
crowdfunding platform developed in the UK was Crowd cube; launched in 2011. However, the 
first platform of equity-based crowdfunding to be regulated by the FCA was Seedrs. The 
research in the UK indicates that the majority of the equity-based crowdfunding financing for 
the SMEs in the UK have occurred at the seed stage (British Business Bank, 2015)). The 
above finding indicates that online equity-based crowdfunding offers an alternative to the 
traditional seed-stage capital that has been sourced from personal savings and loans from 
friends and families. The market also covers early stages as well as start-u, with some 
business angel investors choosing to invest in the SMEs in the UK through the online 
platforms. An example of an equity-based crowdfunding platform that particularly targets the 
angel investors is the Syndicate Room; thus, the financing is more relevant to the innovative 
SMEs that have high growth potential.  
Equity-based crowdfunding is mostly used by the SMEs in the UK for capital expansion, start-
up or seed capital; an indication of the breaking of the pecking order theory of funding 
preferences (Baeck, et al., 2014) The theory holds that SMEs would traditionally prefer internal 
financing from family, friends and founders before the SMEs can even consider the use of 
external financing. In the traditional sense of SME financing, the SMEs would first make use 
of loans from the banks before they can even consider the use of equity. Since equity-based 
crowdfunding uses a distinct model from the established equity market (Brown, et al., 2015), 
it requires less information from the SMEs and little time in the contract negotiations (Macht & 
Weatherston, 2014). Since the financing model focuses on financing the SMEs from the 
crowd, the individual investors from the crowd do not have a big stake that can allow a single 
investor to exert control on the SME’s management.  
The UK SMEs have been attracted to the equity-based crowdfunding as it allows the firms to 
obtain financing more quickly without reducing their autonomy or ownership stake. Therefore, 
it offers a bridge for the SMEs, especially the firms that have high growth potential and are still 
in their infancy (Baeck, et al., 2014). The main advantages for the equity crowdfunding for the 
320 
 
financing of the SMEs in the UK include online access, less dilution of the company in terms 
of limited changes to the ownership structure, as well as the speed of access to the financing 
and the potential to overcome regional barriers to information access. However, despite such 
advantages, there also exist some disadvantages, such as low business awareness of the 
available crowdfunding companies.  
 
Loan based Crowd funding 
In this type of crowdfunding, fundraisers follow lending based funding model. Instead of going 
to the financial institution, they borrow money from peoples by using crowd funding platforms 
(Adhikary, et al., 2018). The main benefits of investors are the financial interests on their 
investment.  
 
Reward Crowd funding 
There are many purposes of reward-based crowd funding, for example, it is used for invention, 
software development, research, and projects. In this type of crowd funding, fundraisers offer 
rewards (Gupta, 2018) in the share of voucher, gift or an event ticket to investors.  Vemagnet, 
Kickstarter and Indiegogo are examples of reward crowdfunding in the UK for SMEs. 
 
Donation Crowd funding 
In this type of crowd funding, fundraises are mostly charities or individuals collect funds for 
specific charitable causes (Gupta, 2018).  JustGiving and Kickstarter are most commonly 
platforms for donation crowdfunding.  
 
Pre-order Crowd funding 
In this type of crowd funding, fundraisers collect funds for projects that are still in the process 
and allow investors to fund for pre-order a product or service for later delivery (Van Looy, 
2016). Kickstarter and Indiegogo are platforms for pre-order crowdfunding. 
 
SME in the UK can raise funds through above crowdfunding platforms to revamp their 
businesses by injecting the needed capital to improve the business operations. Equity-based 
crowdfunding is a method used to raise money for small businesses to expand their 
businesses. An investor will receive a certain percentage of equity invested in a given 
company as a return on investment. The contribution made is in the form of equity to the 
321 
 
business that sought the funding (Chu, et al., 2017). Crowdfunding can be in form of Loan-
based crowdfunding. The contribution is made by the investors and the money is lent out to a 
business just like a bank loan for start-up or expansion. This method of funding is faster as 
compared to bank process of accessing funds. It also creates awareness on the untapped 
funding resources available in the economy depicted through the voluntary contributions. 
Crowdfunding can be riskier in the case of equity-based funding or loan-based funding 
especially for start-up businesses since the money invested might not be ploughed back.  
 
 
 
322 
 
Appendix 7 - The impact of Fintech on financial services from 
traditional banking perspectives 
 
The use of Technology and Social Media 
Information Technology is so much embedded in our daily life that it has been impossible to 
live without technology these days. The main triggers of FinTech are the excessive use of 
technology, communication systems and less regulations in this digital world (Thornton & 
Stone, 1992) to deliver financial services (Natarajan, et al., 2017).  The challenges 
experienced during the global financial crisis were addressed by FinTech. The e-commerce 
platforms came up to offer convenience to customers while performing online purchase needs. 
Barclaycard developed a contactless payment solution in 2007 to enable the customers to get 
contactless credit cards in the UK (Goldfinch, 2018). Bitcoin was developed in 2009 as a new 
digital currency solution. In 2013, google introduced a google wallet that allowed the 
customers to perform transactions through mobile phones using the near-field communication 
technology. FinTech has developed a new way for financial transaction and management 
through platforms such as online banking, automated payment platforms, smartphones 
payment solutions, and online trading sites.  
Social media has become the most significant communication tool by Fintech companies to 
attract and market its customers in the 21st century. It is because there are millions of users 
around the world interacting with their friends and family through mobile friendly social media 
applications. DenizBank in Turkey and Amex worldwide have integrated Facebook and bank 
accounts to run their entire online financial services (FinTech Futures, 2018). Now, the 
Facebook is working with three FinTech companies to launch mobile payment services in 
South Asian countries where it has more than 100 million users (Reuters, 2020).   
Companies have started to provide their products and services online to their customers and 
they are spending a lot to attract customers from all over the world in this digital global age. 
Due to Coronavirus outbreak in the world, the use of social media has increased in the UK 
and Europe as compared to previous years (Johnson, 2020). Social media such as Twitter, 
Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn and many more provide social interactive platforms to people 
to interact with buy and sell options. Social media is a place to connect people with features 
to share posts, ideas, pictures and transform customer-to-customer and customer-to-business 
relationship. People share ideas with each other’s, compliment or complain against the 
products and services offered by companies. Companies such as Olarisbank, Citibank, 
NatWest and American Express are using social media to spread news, updates, links, and 
tweets to influence customers. In the social media, Fintech Marketing has become popular 
these days. Marketing experts from Banks use social media platforms and applications to 
323 
 
analyse customer behaviours and trends with their customer engaging and product selling 
strategies. That not only increase sales but also provide a competitive advantage to maintain 
their customers’ satisfaction. These days UK’ Barclays, US Bank of America, HSBC, 
Santander and Germany’s Deutsche Bank have their own downloadable apps, Facebook and 
twitter accounts to collect feedback from customers, analyse their behaviour and then market 
their products and services to them.  
Now people from all over the world have access to high-speed Computers, Android and Apple 
mobiles, a technology which was not available a decade ago. Customers can download Mobile 
applications and make/lend payments. In the recently report published by (eMarketer, 2018), 
there are estimated 2.55 billion users use social media and mobile applications and due to 
this, mobile commerce is estimated to reach $278.9 billion in 2018. These applications get 
customers data and key information such as salary, job, buying preferences and education 
history to be used by FinTech companies to analyse customers’ preferences, behaviours, 
likes/dislikes and future needs and then by using social media platform they promote their 
services to them. It is also anticipated that the global social networking users will grow more 
than 3 billion in 2020 (Abrams, 2019). Small businesses, Lending Club and Prosper user 
online and social media as a core marketing channels. Banks such as Barclays and Rakuten 
introduced new way of payments routes using Facebook and Twitter.  
Platforms such as Dwolla, Fastacash and Wraps have enabled opportunities to customers to 
give and take money from friends and relative using social media. Facebook peer to peer 
payment enables friends to exchange money via Facebook message app. On the other hands, 
application like Venmo is integrated to bigger platform with payment integrated features to pay 
and lend money features which is same as sharing photos. Small businesses use these 
technologies to facilitate customers’ life in the 21st Century.  
 
Payment and Lending Innovation and its importance to the society 
FinTech has become a threat to the financial services systems in the UK. It creates better and 
efficient products and services for customers, offers competitive pricing, targets untouched 
geographical locations, improve customers’ satisfaction and experience, and use innovative 
solutions to meet customers’ demands.  
Technological advancement such as Automated Teller Machine, mobile and online banking, 
and application payment have enhanced customers’ satisfaction and experience in the lending 
and payment systems.  FinTech is automated financial service with an innovation to address 
customers’ needs. FinTech has a key advantage of being a non-regulated as compared to 
traditional financial organisations. Due to advancement in Information Technology, FinTech 
systems and processes are more efficient to provide on spot or faster solutions to customers.  
324 
 
Financial service sectors believe the FinTech as an unavoidable a threat to their market share. 
To cope with this threat and to remain relevant in their target market, they have started to 
include FinTech features in its products and services. Companies in financial service sectors 
such as banks are making FinTech partners without relying on heavy investments to overcome 
their existing hurdles.  For example, in 2018, Barclays (UK) bank which has a history of 325 
years, become a key partner with an EU leading FinTech business MarketInvoice so that 
Barclay’s SME’s clients will use MarketInvoice online invoicing platform to manage their cash 
flow and growth. In 2014, Santander Bank allocated $100 million funds in FinTech to meet its 
107 million individual customers and $3 million corporate customers worldwide.   
In 2017, HSBC UK has partnered with FinTech Buds to provide an integrated selection of 
financial services to its individual and corporate customers. This shows that Fintech has 
started to show its strength, relevance and impact in the society. Now in the UK, all financial 
institutions and other financial service providers are providing services such as online, mobile 
banking and same trends is going on in the emerging economies. These trends are new and 
will continue to grow with more features. In 2018, UK banks and financial technology firms 
have launched a toolkit to develop partnership with sponsorship from the government-backed 
Fintech Delivery Panel as part of HM Treasury’s Fintech Sector Strategy. That toolkit will help 
partner organisations to identify key issues and resolve them in line with applicable legislation 
and according to customers’ requirements.  
In 2019, smartphone, Apple Pay, Mobile banking and raising money for specific needs via 
crowdfunding has grown a lot as compared to last few years.  
 
Reshaping Financial Service Sector and Regulations 
Financial sector executives believe that consumer and commercial banking are disrupted by 
FinTech. Banks feel threats in their market shares, sales, customers’ preferences, and 
information security/privacy. These financial institutions are still in their early stage of 
innovation as compared to what FinTech proposes. It is because FinTech are offering flexible 
Peer to Peer marketplaces to its customers to secure loan even with no or poor credit history, 
personal financial management tools and platforms like crowdfunding to raise money. To deal 
with threats of FinTech, banks have started to use software solutions offered by FinTech which 
are easily integrated by application program interfaces (API). API enables financial institutions 
to develop interactive and value-added solutions in the banking platforms.   
Other triggers are introduction of new regulations for financial institutions to remain solvent, 
capital regulations, and many structural reforms (Nicoletti, 2017). Further, the governments 
initiated several bails out measures to keep the industry afloat (Hanna, 2019). Banks were 
reluctant to lend out money to the customers by coming up with stringent precautionary tactics. 
325 
 
The banks were on recovery plans and compliance with the statutory and regulatory 
requirements and they never wanted to initiate any change to their system of operation. 
FinTech capitalised on the gap created by the reluctance of the banks by developing a 
responsive financial platform created on the internet to offer an interactive, faster and cheaper 
access to financial services to the customers (Goldfinch, 2018). The need for a safer financial 
solution was on demand. FinTech entirely disrupted the traditional financial service models. 
The financial intermediaries were completely removed by the FinTech's robust revolution in 
the financial industry.  
FinTech put the benchmark at higher levels. These days FinTech is an industry made up of 
organisations that use technology with efficient processes, attract customers with innovative 
techniques to provide financial solutions (Nicoletti, 2017). Banks realised the ever-growing 
acceptance rate of the financial technology service models to initiate a catch-up approach to 
remain relevant in the provision of the financial services. 
 
Changing Consumer Behaviour 
FinTech gained popularity after the financial recession period because of the widespread 
mistrust in the banks after the financial crisis. For example, customers especially young 
generation started to have less faith due to rationale that if the financial institutions are unable 
to cope in managing risks so how can advise them for services and savings (Nicoletti, 2017).  
FinTech has been expeditiously cruising into financial marketplaces and filling the product 
gaps (Koenigsberg, 2017) left by financial institutions, substantially enhancing the users’ 
satisfaction. The massive progress of internet usage across the globe has created a profound 
basis for FinTech companies in the foreseeable future (Desai, 2015). FinTech is perceived to 
have improved by creating new features that help clients in solving obstacles and challenges, 
particularly, relating to financing matters. For example, PayPal was a typical example in 
financial technology companies, laden with innovative measures such as changing the form 
of transactions and also the management of funds (Dudkowski, et al., 2016).  
FinTech products and services have immensely influenced customers’ behaviour in relation 
to managing personal finance, savings, investment, wealth management, and mortgage, 
insurance and so on. People around the world use mobile apps to provide efficient and cost- 
effective payments services to customers and businesses without relying on cheques or ATM 
withdrawal options. New era of Fintech is still growing with services of digital currency, invoice 
trading and real-time payments. It is because people found that new financial technologies 
are easier to use, secure, quick and effective.  
326 
 
FinTech today has changed financial world through innovative financial technologies 
dramatically, from finance and investment via alternative finance such as peer to peer lending, 
crowdfunding and cryptocurrency.  
 
 
327

Appendix 10 – Participant Information Sheet 
The Research Project 
 
Title of project: Exploring CEOs’ decision making in Fintech Financing: Evidence from UK 
SMEs 
 
Purpose of study 
This research aims to explore the phenomenon of CEOs decision making to finance their 
SMEs business operations by using alternative sources of funding options; how their financing 
decisions are influenced by their leadership qualities, age, gender, professional networking, 
values, organisation structure, culture, governance, and government policies, which ultimately 
impacts on SMEs performance. By participating this interview, you are helping the researcher 
to unveil unexplored realities of your Fintech decision making e.g. the acceptance of 
crowdfunding, P2P or cryptocurrency for the SMEs performance. This will also create a 
platform for other SMEs, decision makers, policy makers and future researchers to learn from 
you and further strengthen Fintech financing in the UK.  
 
Research Team:  
 
Arsalan Iqbal 
Postgraduate Researcher 
Faculty of Business and Law 
Anglia Ruskin University 
Dr Ying Wang 
Senior Lecturer in Finance 
Faculty of Business and Law 
Anglia Ruskin University 
 
 
1. Do I have to take part? 
The interview will last up to 50 minutes via online platform. Participation is voluntary. The participants 
can refuse to take part without giving a reason. The participant can leave the study at any time, even 
after consenting to take part by emailing mai149@pgr.aru.ac.uk 
2. Has the study got ethical approval?  
This study has received ethics approval by the School Research Ethics Panel (SREP) and ratified by 
the Faculty Research Ethics Panel under the terms of Anglia Ruskin University’s Policy and Code of 
Practice for the Conduct of Research with Human Participants. 
3. Legislation relating to this study 
This research will comply with the Data Protection Act 2018. The records from this study will be treated 
and kept as confidential. Only the research team will have access to the files and any audio recording. 
No data will be used to personally identify any person or organisation.  
4. What will happen to the results of the study?  
The fully anonymised response will be used in the project report in aggregated formats. It is anticipated 
that the findings of this study might result in articles, books, industry reports and peer-reviewed journal 
papers. These publications will not disclose any personal or organisation identification information. 
The anonymised derived data and findings of this study could be further utilised in the other studies.
